Editorial Policy 


Annali d'ltalianistica seeks to promote the study of Italian literature in its 
cultural context, to foster scholarly excellence, and to select topics of interest to 
a large number of Italianists. Monographic in nature, the journal is receptive to 
a variety of topics, critical approaches, and theoretical perspectives. Each 
year’s topic is announced well ahead of time, and contributions are welcome. 
The journal is issued in the fall of each year. Manuscripts should be submitted 
on diskette and should be accompanied by a stamped, self-addressed envelope. 
Authors should follow the MLA style for articles in English; articles in Italian 
should conform to the Ad/ style sheet. Visit the journal’s website 
(www. ibiblio.org/annali) for further information on the contributions’ style. 
For all communications concerning contributions, address the Editor, Annali 
d'Italianistica, The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Chapel Hill, 
NC 27599-3170, or post <annali(@metalab.unc.edu>. 


Notes & Reviews 
This section occasionally publishes essays and review articles on topics treated 
in one of the previous volumes of Annali d'Italianistica. 


Italian Bookshelf 
Italian Bookshelf is edited by Dino S. Cervigni and Anne Tordi with the 
collaboration of Norma Bouchard, Paolo Cherchi, Gustavo Costa, Albert N. 
Mancini, Massimo Maggiari, and John P. Welle. 
The purpose of /talian Bookshelf is to identify, review, and bring to the 
attention of Italianists recent studies on Italian literature and culture. /talian 
Bookshelf will cover the entire history of Italian literature. 4d/ will review 
books exclusively on the basis of their scholarly worth. To this purpose, junior 
and senior colleagues will be invited to collaborate without any consideration 
to academic affiliation and with an open attitude toward critical approaches. 
Contributions to this section are solicited. Scholars who intend to contribute 
are encouraged to contact the editors or one of the section’s permanent 
collaborators. Book reviews, to be submitted on diskette, should be sent to the 
Editor. For inquiries post <annali(@metalab.unc.edu>. 

The Journal’s Website: www. ibiblio.org/annali 
The tables of contents of all issues, including the list of all book reviews, are 
available online. As of volume 16 (1998), each issue’s introductory essay and 
all book reviews are available online with their full texts.

Dino S. Cervigni' 





Literature Criticism Ethics: 
An Introductory Essay with Some Personal Reflections 


I began writing this essay on September 10, 2001. I returned to it on the morning of the 
following day, September 11, when my wife called me from her office, telling me 
frantically to turn the TV on. For the next several hours, I followed on the screen what 
Journalists immediately called the most tragic and disastrous military attack on the 
United States of America since Pearl Harbor in 1941. As I, together with all people of 
good will, mourn and pray for the countless victims of such a horrific tragedy and 
condemn all forms of violence for whatever purpose and cause, I have also become 
convinced, more so than ever before, of the ethical imperative incumbent upon all 
people, individually and collectively, and of the special privilege and duty befalling all of 
us, as educators, to become aware of its far-reaching implications in all aspects of our 
profession. I also invite all of you to work for peace and join me ina sincere prayer for 
peace: a deeply felt invocation addressed to the Almighty, each other, and the entire 
world community, best expressed in the following words of the Bible: “[...] pax in terra 
hominibus bonae voluntatis” (“[...] peace on earth to people of good will” Luke 2:14). 


1. Literature Criticism Ethics: The Ethical Imperative? 
This 2001 issue of Annali d’italianistica, devoted to the study of the 


| The essays’ summaries that appear in this introduction are mostly written by the authors 
themselves or are drawn from their contributions. I would like to thank all of them for 
making this issue possible. 

: Except the cases in which a philosopher distinguishes, for specific reasons, between 
ethics and moral, the two terms are synonyms in both Italian and English: “[...] 2. ethics. 
(used with a sing. verb). The study of the general nature of morals and of the specific 
moral choices to be made by a person; moral philosophy. 3. ethics. (used with a sing. or 
pl. verb) The rules of standards governing the conduct of a person or the members of a 
profession: medical ethics. [... from Greek éthikos, ethical, from éthos, character.]” 
“moral [...] adj. 1. Of or concerned with the judgment of the goodness or badness of 
human action and character [...]. 2. Teaching or exhibiting goodness or correctness of 
character and behavior [...]. 3. Conforming to standards of what is right or just in 
behavior: virtuous: a moral life. 4. Arising from conscience or the sense of right or 
wrong: a moral obligation. {...] — moral n. [...] 3. morals. Rules or habits of conduct, 
especially of sexual conduct, with reference to standards of right and wrong [...]. [Middle 
English, from Old French, from Lat. moralis, from mos, mor-, custom.]” (The American 
Heritage Dictionary) “Moral philosophy (or ethics)” (The Oxford Companion to 
Philosophy 940; 251; 587-91). “etica. [...] s. f. 7 (filos.) Parte della filosofia che studia 1 
problemi e i valori connessi all’agire umano [...]. 2 Insieme delle norme di condotta 
pubblica e privata seguite da una persona o da un gruppo di persone [...].” “morale. [...] 
s. f. J Parte della filosofia che studia i problemi relativi alla condotta dell’uomo. SIN. 
Etica. 2 Complesso di consuetudini e norme che regolano la vita pubblica e privata [...]” 
(lo Zingarelli). 


Annali d’Italianistica 19 (2001) 


8 Dino S. Cervigni 


interconnections of literature, criticism, and ethics, is the most challenging of the 
journal’s nineteen monographic volumes published thus far. All of us easily 
comprehend the reasons of this great challenge. It is due neither to literature nor 
the practice of criticism, for we, as scholars of literature and practitioners of 
various critical approaches, always accept, even welcome, the difficulties arising 
from our scholarly investigations; rather, it is due to the third dimension added 
by the 2001 issue of Ad/, to the reciprocal interplay of literature and criticism, 
namely, ethics. The study of the ethical implications connected with the analysis 
of literature, the practice of criticism, and our profession as educators, in fact, 
makes most of us feel somewhat uneasy, just as, or even more so than, the study 
of the sacred and religious aspects of literature and poetry does.> 

And yet, as people who live in a community and depend on human society, 
we cannot escape the ethical issue. Simply stated, ethics concerns everything we 
do as humans, as can be easily understood even from the term’s strict 
etymological explanation, whether we employ the one derived from Greek 
(ethics < éthos) or from Latin (moral < mos, moris), both terms connoting the 
“custom,” “habit,” and “habitual character” of people. Any attempt at placing 
ourselves outside or beyond the ethical sphere, therefore, would be tantamount to 
situating ourselves outside or even above the human community. The question, 
nevertheless, remains whether the ethical issue that confronts all of us every day 
of our lives, is immanent to our nature as individuals and to the human 
community within which we live, or, rather, whether this ethical issue transcends 
each and all of us because it comes from outside or above us; or, also, whether it 
originates in each and all of us, individually and collectively, while also finding 
its superior or ultimate sanction outside each and all of us, in the supernatural 
and divine.* 

The interplay of literature, criticism, and ethics has always been present, 
albeit in varying degrees, in Italy’s literary culture, since its medieval 
beginnings, rooted in the Western ancient and Judeo-Christian traditions, until 
contemporary times, during which we have experienced an explosion and 
implosion of —isms, whose dizzying effects have illumined a few of us, alienated 
several, and confused many. 

Then, today, deeply shaken by the tragedy that has affected the United 
States of America and the civilized world, we are forced to reflect upon our role 


3 To illustrate the uncomfortable sense one may experience when confronted by the 
ethical issue, I cannot but refer to the anecdote told by a Spanish scholar, Bandera, 
dealing with the sacred in The Poem of the Cid. The distinguished scholar to whom 
Bandera had submitted his manuscript could not help feeling a form of peculiar allergy 
vis-a-vis the approach to the sacred pursued by Bandera (“Esta cosa me da alergia. No lo 
puedo remediar’ 1). 

4 For the history of ethics, here is but a brief bibliographical list: Brinton; MacIntyre; 
pertinent entries in The Oxford Companion to Philosophy. 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 9 


as educators and scholars and to understand the connections inextricably linking 
the artistic product, its critical assessment, and the moral issue inseparable from 
all human undertakings. 

From an historical perspective, ethics, however differently formulated, has 
always played a constant and fundamental role in all critical endeavors. In this 
regard Wayne C. Booth writes: “Of all the possible critical responses to poetry, 
the one with the most sustained written tradition is the question, ‘Will the 
experience of knowing this poem be good for me or my society?’” (384). 
Booth’s moral-critical question presupposes the moral-critic’s ability to probe 
into the ethical nature of the literary product itself. 

While undergoing revisions and attacks from Plato to modern times, ethical 
criticism, nevertheless, all but disappeared in scholarly literature from the 
Positivist movement of the late 19" and early 20" centuries until recently. Then, 
a few decades ago, the ethical debate suddenly re-emerged, perhaps in 
connection with the rise of feminist and ethnic studies, the investigations into the 
role of the literary canon, and/or the renewed interest in moral concerns and 
issues in general. 

In nearly two decades of scholarly publishing, Ad/ has already investigated 
many of the topics pertaining to ethical concerns in literature, from feminist 
studies (Ad/J 7 [1989]) and mystical writings by women (13 [1995]) to 
autobiography (4 [1986]), anthropological approaches to literature (15 [1997]), 
the modern and the postmodern (9 [1991]), and cultural studies (16 [1998]).> By 
devoting its 2001 issue specifically to the interplay of literature, criticism, and 
ethics, Ad/ intends to focus directly on the role of the ethical issue in the writing 
and reading of literature: in brief, the function of what we can call ethical 
hermeneutics. Steeped in the Judeo-Christian and ancient classical traditions 
throughout its historical development, Italy’s literary culture may be viewed as a 
continuous debate on the dignity of human beings, their duty in society, and their 
lives’ ultimate purpose. One only needs to recall, at the beginning of Italy’s 
cultural history, Saint Francis’s Cantico delle creature and, very recently, 
Roberto Benigni’s reflection on life’s beauty and tragedy in La vita é bella. It is 
a well-known fact that for Dante Alighieri, relying on Aristotle and Christian 
writers and philosophers, ethics played a preeminent role in all speculative as 


5 The introduction to the 1991 issue of Ad/ and its many essays, devoted to the modern 
and postmodern, may be useful to the reader who seeks to understand the notion of the 
postmodern in relation to Italy. The study of ethics within the notion of postmodernity is 
a hotly debated issue, whith sharp criticisms coming from all sides: those who believe in 
an ethics of postmodernity (e.g., Wyschogrod), those who believe in the individual’s 
capability of “inventing right and wrong,” and yet maintain the possibility of a “secular 
religion” (e.g., Mackie), and those who accuse postmodern theories of what they perceive 
as the moral decay of our time (e.g., Makolkin). I have found the most balanced positions 
in authors I would characterize as Christian thinkers (MacIntyre; Desmond). | refer to 
these authors for a bibliography on the subject matter. 


10 Dino S. Cervigni 


well as practical endeavors (Convivio 2:14). Nonetheless, the ethical concerns 
underlying most, if not the whole, of Italy’s literary culture came under attack 
during Italy’s fairly recent history, likely because of Croce’s idealism as well as 
other, more difficult to determine, indigenous or mostly foreign —isms. More 
recently, the writings of some contemporary theoreticians, such as Lévinas, 
Ricoeur, Derrida, Foucault, etc., have unquestionably offered some impetus to 
the resurgence of contemporary ethical investigations. In the January 1999 issue 
of PMLA on literature and ethics, for instance, these contemporoary thinkers 
offered most of the theoretical framework for the contributors’ essays. At the 
same time, one finds it difficult to accept the notion that only those 20"-century 
thinkers mentioned above can provide a valid ground for current inquiries into 
ethical concerns. One cannot but hope, therefore, that Italy’s literary culture, 
with its strong attachment to its pluri-millenary tradition, may offer an 
appropriate and balanced ground for inquiries into the role of ethical 
hermeneutics. Unquestionably, the essays of young and senior scholars who have 
responded to my invitation and have made this issue possible can help all of us 
reflect on the multiple relationships of literature, criticism, and ethics, and 
especially on the moral imperative that falls upon all of us. 


2. A Forgotten Harmony: The Medieval Concordance of Esse, Verum, et 
Pulchrum 

Italy’s literary culture prides itself on a pedigree of the highest spirituality, 
religiosity, and ethical concern. In the so-called “Cantico delle creature,” or 
‘“Laudes creaturarum,” often viewed as the first poetic text of Italian literature, 
Saint Francis invites man to be in harmony with God, 


Altissimu, onnipotente, bon Signore, 
tue so’ le laude, la gloria e l’honore et onne benedictione. 


nature, 
Laudato sie, mi’ Signore, cum tucte le tue creature, 
and one another, 


Laudato si’, mi Signore, per quelli ke perdonano per lo tuo amore 
e sostengo infirmitate et tribulatione. 
Beati quelli ke *| sosterranno in pace, 
ka da te, Altissimo, sirano incoronati. 
(vv. 1-2; 5; 25-26, Antologia della poesia italiana \:22-24) 


In fact, in medieval times, not just saints and poets like Saint Francis, but also 
philosophers and theologians like Saint Thomas proclaim, in the words of 
Umberto Eco, 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 1\\ 


that God was being, goodness, and beauty, and that things participated in these attributes. 
(The Aesthetics of Thomas Aquinas 29)6 


Striking as it may sound to our contemporary way of thinking, Saint Thomas 
even suggests that humans, in pursuing being, pursue not only the good and the 
beautiful but also peace: 


[...] appetitum terminari ad bonum et pacem et pulchrum, non est eum terminari in 
diversa. Ex hoc enim ipso quod aliquid appetit bonum, appetit simul et pulchrum et 
pacem [...]. Unde quicumque appetit bonum, appetic hoc ipso pulchrum. 
([...] if appetite terminates in good and peace and the beautiful, this does not mean that it 
terminates in different goals. By the very fact of tending to good, a thing at the same time 
tends to the beautiful and to peace [...]. So anyone who desires the good, by that very fact 
desires the beautiful.) 

(De veritate 22, | ad 12; qtd. Eco 33) 


The identity of being with the good, the beautiful, and the peaceful eliminated 
necessarily any breach between art and morality, therefore establishing an ideal 
view of reality, both uncreated and created, and of all human activities, 
formulated metaphysically, ethically, and aesthetically.’ 

Eco reminds us, nevertheless, that Saint Thomas “was devaluing the modus 
poeticus’’; therefore, almost as a consequence, “the poets themselves evaded his 
intellectual influence; they initiated a new, mystical approach to poetic texts, a 
new way of reading which, through various avatars, was to survive down to our 
times.” Therefore, while sharing the same “unified world view” characteristic of 
Saint Thomas’s philosophy, Dante proposed a way of writing and reading poetry 
as “‘a new instance of prophetical writing [...] endowed with spiritual senses just 
as the scriptures are” (162). 

Fundamentally in agreement with Scholastic philosophy and_ theology, 
Dante presents himself, first and foremost, as the poet of rectitude, seeking to 
view the whole of humankind and history from this ethical and poetic 
perspective. In this issue’s first essay, “Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic 
Choice,” Olivia Holmes argues that, in the Commedia and other works as well, 
Dante figures the good life as the right road, and he implicitly represents himself 
as a version of Hercules in bivio, choosing between virtue and vice. Drawing on 
classical, biblical, and romance sources, Dante conflates imagery connected with 


6 | refer the reader to Eco’s book for an in-depth analysis of these issues and further 
bibliography. 

7 The Old and New Testaments add another transcendental, i.e., justice, to those 
mentioned above: God and His Son, Christ, provide peace and justice to all people of 
good will (see pertinent entries in Vocabulaire de théologie biblique). For the identity of 
justice with rectitude (“rectitudo actus”), see St. Thomas 1-2 q. 21 3c: q. 46 7 ad 2; etc. 


12 Dino S. Cervigni 


the voyage and the erotic choice: the two roads are viewed also as two ladies, 
and the downward pull of the vicious lifestyle is figured as an act of seduction. 
Dante’s allegory is deeply layered, and the relation between the two ways and 
the two women (and thus between the individual’s basic options) turns out also 
to be not one of strict opposition but rather of continuity and hierarchy: the 
protagonist passes by way of one lady to attain the other. The depiction of ethics 
as a choice between contradictory alternatives, Holmes argues, is ultimately 
resolved synthetically so that dualism is overcome and binary oppositions 
become progressive continuities. 

Thus viewed, the ethical issue affects every individual, Everyman, since 
every one experiences some form of smarrimento, loss, and fault in the pursuit 
of the truly good, both individually and collectively. In fact, in Dante the 
individual’s pursuit of the good is always situated within the collective and 
universal context of the whole of humankind in its past, present, and future 
history. In her essay entitled “Dante’s Utopian Political Vision, the Roman 
Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans,” Brenda Deen Schildgen focuses not so 
much on Dante’s politico-ethical vision of the Empire but rather on the eternal 
destiny of those ancient spirits who either lived before Christ or lived after Him, 
but did not know Him. Dante accepted the Church Fathers’ idea that the Roman 
Empire was chosen providentially for the unfolding of Christ’s Redemption and 
humankind’s salvation (Par. 6-7). In his attempt to see Divine Providence as 
history’s guide, Dante chose to condemn the ancient poets’ negative view of 
history, replacing it with his own visionary theory of a universal empire 
simultaneously and independently governed by a spiritual guide and a secular 
leader. Schildgen argues that Dante confined the greatest poets of antiquity, of 
whom he made himself a joyful associate, to Limbo because of their political 
blindness; at the same time, he specifically singled out for salvation other 
Romans or ancient spirits whom he made co-partners in his own vision. Dante’s 
utopian political vision put faith in a history guided by Divine Providence, 
whereas, in reading his great literary forebears, he recognized that they did not 
share this hope. 

Apart from their specific readings, these two essays on Dante bear out two 
principal points: first, Christianity’s view that God’s will to save all people is, at 
the same time, true and mysterious; and, second, every indvividual and the entire 
human community are charged with an ethical imperative, planted within each 
one of us while also transcending all of us, to seek and pursue the good. The 
voice of Dante, a self-proclaimed poet of rectitude, has been announcing a poetic 
gospel of justice and ethics for seven hundred years, and will do so for many 
more to come. In fact, Dante’s prophetic voice and ethical gospel seem to appeal 
to English-speaking people, Americans in particular, in a very special manner. 

It is well known that Dante’s oeuvre is focused on his personal experience, 
especially his undeserved exile. For reasons mostly quite different from those of 
Dante, Petrarch also focuses on the experiences of the self, which is constructed 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 13 


poetically and spiritually. In his essay on “Scrittura e alterita in Petrarca,” 
Massimo Lollini is interested in ethics as a point of intersection between 
philosophy and literature. Lollini studies how the notion of otherness in literature 
emerges in Petrarch’s writings, which, for Lollini, constitute a necessary premise 
to modern critical discourse on alterity. In the writing process Petrarch discovers 
two main form of alterity, first of all, the alterity of the face of Laura, which he 
cannot “write” or present in its proper form (Canzoniere 308: 5-8). Lollini then 
argues that the ethical moment in the Canzoniere is located precisely in 
Petrarch’s awareness of the impossibility to reduce the face of the other to a pure 
representation because in that face there is something infinite (Canzoniere 339: 
9-14). The second fundamental dimension of alterity explored by Petrarch is 
related to his reflection on time and death, through which he introduces a notion 
of truth grounded not on the ontological and transcendental plane but on the 
ethical discourse. The poet is thus aware of the strict interconnection that links 
writing to time and tries in different ways to evade the notion of a crystallized 
subjectivity related to the hypostasis of the present created by the writing 
process. Petrarch points to the presence of the future in the present and to the 
alterity of an element always resistant to human reason, not only in his letter to 
future human beings (Posteritati), but also in his reflection on death as 
something irreducible to human control (Testamentum). Death is considered by 
Petrarch a “refugio” (Canzoniere 331: 63), something that creates the actual 
possibility of evading the hypostasis of time while opening the perspective of 
eternity: a different kind of temporality that disrupts the present moment of the 
human subject writing in solitude. Following Augustine (Confessiones 9: 10, 24) 
and developing a Christian notion of eternity and Resurrection, Petrarch situates 
the search for authenticity made by the human subject in the intentio directed 
toward the eschatological future (7riumphus eternitatis 67-69). Notwithstanding 
the human recta intentio, this search is destined to be open-ended and not to 
exhaust itself in writing. Petrarch’s writing cannot truly break the hypostasis of 
the present and remove the ontological difference between divine eternity and 
human temporality. On the ethical ground, finally, he realizes all the difficulties 
implicit in the attempt at transcending the pragmatic context in which the poet 
lives and through which he tries to make sense of the world (7riumphus 
eternitatis 61-62). 


3. Contemporary Literature: Valuable Shards of Lost Ideals 

No attempt is made here to trace the development of ethics, and _ its 
interconnections with literature and criticism, from the time of Petrarch to the 
second half of the 20" century, on which most of the essays of this issue focus. 
According to historians of ethics, the Aristotelian tradition pervaded the study of 
ethics from the 14" to the 16" centuries, while strong interests in the ethical 
ideas of Platonism, Stoicism, and Epicureanism continued, oftentimes viewed 
independently from Christian moral ethics (Kraye 63-79). The Protestant 


14 Dino S. Cervigni 


Reformation, with its emphasis on the individual’s ability to interpret the Bible 
independently and relate to God directly without any intermediary, and the 
Catholic Reformation, with its emphasis on the believer’s need to be guided by 
the Church’s leaders, were extremely influential in the formation of modern 
ethics from the middle of the 16" century until the Enlightenment and the French 
Revolution. In addition to the Protestant and Catholic Reformations, other 
historical changes affected the modern notion of ethics, such as the destructive 
wars waged in the name of religion, the discovery of the new world with its 
different customs, the emphasis on the enlightened human reason viewed as 
capable of guiding man, and ultimately the focus on the moral autonomy of the 
individual (Schneewind 80-95). Emmanuel Kant’s (1724-1804) importance in 
the development of modern ethics is fundamental, as Schneewind states very 
succinctly: “Kant went beyond anyone else in asserting our moral autonomy” 
(93).8 The 19" century, according to Schacht (106), begins with the 
radicalization of Kant’s moral autonomy by Fichte (1762-1814) and ends with its 
radical rejection by Nietzsche (1844-1900), passing through Hegel (1770-1831), 
Schopenhauer (1788-1860), Marx (1818-1883), and Kierkegaard (1813-1855). 

And yet, no sooner have we bidden farewell to the 20'"-century’s two world 
wars and countless other devastations waged in the name of a condemnable 
supremacy or abhominable tyranny, religious intolerance or sheer fanaticism, 
than the 21" century was struck at the very core of what so many of us view as 
the center of the civilized world. The horrific tragedy of September 11, 2001, 
invites us to reflect on the manner in which we must behave individually and 
collectively, reminding us that everything we do carries a powerfully ethical 
weight. It is my hope that the following essays will help us understand, in a small 
albeit significant manner, our ethical duty. 

With “The Return of the Spiritual, with a Note on the Fiction of Bufalino, 
Tabucchi, and Celati,” Charles Klopp offers the reader a kind of panoramic 
view of the second half of the 20" century, while looking for the lost shards of a 
perennial ethics. Klopp argues that one result, perhaps, of the recent implosions, 
transmogrifications, or movements to higher levels (as the cases may be) of both 
the Italian Communist and the Christian Democrat parties is that Italian 
intellectuals are now freer than they have been for many years to express 
themselves in public on such issues as religion and ethics. Judging from the 
fiction and the cultural commentary that has issued from Italy in the last decades, 
the anti-clerical, anti-Catholic, and sometimes anti-religious stances assumed by 
many Italian intellectuals as part of their opposition to Christian Democrat 
political hegemony have been replaced by different attitudes toward these 
matters. The political and materialist nature of much cultural discourse earlier in 
the century (whether or not this posture was accompanied by adherence to 
dialectical materialism as a philosophical or political creed) has given way to 


8 A recent translation of Kant’s thought on ethics, Lectures on Ethics, is now available. 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 15 


more spiritual considerations involving such matters as ethical behavior and the 
role that spirituality and the sacred play in human life. 

Alberto Casadei situates his essay, titled “La narrazione etica: esempi dal 
romanzo italiano,” within an insightful theoretical context, which ranges from 
the ancient philosophers to contemporary theorists and which he then proceeds 
to illustrate through several examples drawn from contemporary Italian 
narrative. After providing a useful survey of theorists concerned with ethics in 
literature, Casadei distinguishes between works of narrative that impose upon 
readers some kind of ethical reading and those that challenge readers for an 
ethical response without forcing a specific answer upon them. Casadei also 
emphasizes the importance for “i grandi romanzi [...] di esprimere un’etica 
possibile sulla base della capacita di rappresentare la morte,” man’s defining 
moment. In his view, the novel calls for a specifically ethical collaboration on 
the part of the reader-interpreter, who is confronted by a text that often times 
proposes a comprehensive reinterpretation of the meaning of human existence in 
ethical terms. By way of exemplifications, Casadei focuses on works by 
Giuseppe Pontiggia (Nati due volte), Camilla Baresani (// plagio), Diego De 
Silva (La donna di scorta; Certi bambini) and Walter Siti (Scuola di nudo). For 
Casadei, these narratives either call for the narrator to provide his own 
interpretation or, in the cases of Pontiggia and Siti, offer an ethical construct that 
ultimately calls for the reader’s ethical interpretation. 

In “Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling,” Norma 
Bouchard continues the previous essay’s focus on contemporary narrative, 
concentrating specifically on Consolo and conducting her analysis from the 
perspective of Lévinas and the postmodern. Vincenzo Consolo — Bouchard 
argues — has often attributed an ethical status to his fictional writing. Yet none 
of his historical novels provides the reader with the totalizing ethical truths that 
are consistent with the conceptual framework of modernity. From // sorriso 
dell’ignoto marinaio to Lunaria, from Retablo to the very recent Lo spasimo di 
Palermo, Consolo’s narratives question the edifice of representation and the 
metaphysical claims upon which such an edifice rests. In short, despite 
Consolo’s humanistic conception of writing, his novels weaken the 
epistemological guarantees that ground norms for moral behavior. Consolo’s 
aesthetic practice, however, cannot be disregarded as a symptom of the 
insecurities of the present age, but prompts one to ask the following questions: 
Can a narrative that is traversed by the debunking of the conceptual framework 
of modernity still articulate an ethical call to responsibility and justice? Is the 
ethical imperative resilient enough to survive a post-rationalist episteme? If so, 
how can ethical writing be deployed after the de-legitimizing effect of 
postmodernist thought? In order to address these questions, Bouchard re- 
examines the ethical claims made by Consolo in the interview Fuga dall’Etna. 
She argues that the reciprocity between novelistic writing and ethical imperative 
is no longer located, for Consolo, in the so-called legislative and ethical truths of 


16 Dino S. Cervigni 


modernity, but it resides in a revision of the genre of the historical novel and its 
referential language. Such revision is reflected in // sorriso dell’ignoto marinaio. 
While the open-ended plot of this 1976 historical novel dramatizes, without 
solving, ethical paradoxes, its pluralized and fragmented narrative discourse 
dissolves the stability of the enunciation, thereby suggesting that language has 
ceased to be a vehicle for the transmission of absolute truths. Nonetheless, since 
a humanistic commitment to justice and responsibility is a constant in Consolo’s 
work, Bouchard outlines a conceptual framework to assess the ethical import of 
Il sorriso. She does so by combining Lévinas’s post-rationalist description of the 
ethical moment with Newton’s Lévinas-inspired model of ethical narratology. 
Bouchard then turns to a reading of // sorriso and suggests that the ethical 
discourse of the novel emerges out of an intersubjective encounter that binds 
tellers and listeners, including us, the readers and critics of narrative. In the gaps 
created by the interruption of a continuous, stable flow of narration, and in the 
differential patterns of representation established by a plurality of voices and 
points of view, readers will articulate an ethical moment of signification. No 
longer founded upon a predetermined system of morality and judgment, this 
ethical moment arises out of the unconditional act of responsibility and 
obligation of one-for-the-Other. 

At this juncture, after reading and introducing Bouchard’s essay, I would 
like to reflect, however briefly, on the meaning of the other and/or Other as 
understood by 20"-century philosophers. At first reading, the dictionary’s 
definition of the term — “A different or additional person or things. [...] People 
aside from oneself’ (American Heritage Dictionary) — may not strike us as 
substantially different from the one provided to us by an encyclopedia of 
philosophy: “Other, the. Primarily understood as the other human being in his 
or her differences” (The Oxford Companion to Philosophy). In fact, the same 
encyclopedia helps us understand Lévinas’s importance in any debate on the 
other and the ethics concerned with the other: 


Levinas charged previous philosophy, including that of Husserl, with reducing the Other 
to an object of consciousness and thereby failing to maintain its absolute alterity: the 
radically Other transcends me and the totality into whose network I seek to place it. 
According to Levinas, by challenging my self-assurance the Other opens the question of 
ethics. The priority of the Other becomes equivalent to the primacy of ethics over 


ontology. 
(The Oxford Companion to Philosophy 637) 


At this point, | may state my satisfaction, at the very least, with the notion that 
“the radically Other transcends me” (how can we possibly know the other, since 
we so often are unable to know ourselves?) and also that “the Other opens the 
question of ethics.” For, in fact, without the other I would necessarily live in 
total isolation, and neither I nor anyone else could possibly judge my ethos. At 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay \7 


the same time, the issue of the other opens up the question of male-female 
relationships and the West’s encounter with non-Western cultures. Finally, and 
most importantly, for all believers the truly Other is the Supreme Being, who not 
only opens the question of ethics but indeed provides the believers’ ethical 
concern with its true foundation.? Several of the following essays return to the 
notion of the other as presented by Lévinas. 

In “Ethics, Transcendence, and the Other: Milione of Marco Polo and 
Calvino’s Le citta invisibili,’ Steven Shankman argues that the possibility of 
dialogue with the Other haunts Calvino in Le citta invisibili, which focuses on the 
relationship between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan derived from Milione. In 
Calvino’s novel, the dialogue between Marco and the Khan hovers on the brink 
of the transcendence of the participants’ egos. This transcendence, however, 
never quite happens because of the nature of language, which — Calvino 
suggests —- remains within the bounds of representation rather than in its 
capacity to welcome and address the Other. Shankman then asks what we mean 
when we say “the Other.” In contemporary critical theory, and particularly in 
postcolonial theory, the Other generally refers to the person who has been 
marginalized by the dominant power structure: women, persons of color, the 
disadvantaged and disempowered. Within this context, the word has a negative 
valence; namely, to turn that person into a thing, an object, to dehumanize her or 
him. Shankman, however, seeks to recover a different, positive meaning of the 
word Other, deriving it from the philosophical work of Martin Buber and 
particularly Emmanuel Lévinas. Within this context, to speak in the language of 
the phenomenological tradition, this positive alterity evades conceptualization, 
for conceptualization would automatically violate the otherness of the other 
subject, since it entails knowing and, hence, the assimilation of alterity by the ego 
that seeks to know. At the same time, the unknowable Other demands my 
responsibility. 

Issues concerning violence and death are at the very core of any debate on 
ethics. In “A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi,” 
Angela M. Jeannet examines the connections between this author, who is 
considered a major voice of postmodernism, and the phenomenological tradition. 
The immediacy of his characters’ empathy with the humiliated and oppressed is 
based on the body’s primacy in constructing human experience. Language is the 
privileged medium for conveying, however fragmentarily, that immediacy. 
Pursuing the reflection initiated by the European literature of the 1930s and 
1940s, Tabucchi’s fiction proposes the possibility of recognizing the Other as 
Other and at the same time as Self. Certainty is not possible, however, as the 
question asked by one of his characters reminds us : “Ma la scrittura puo salvare 
da un’umiliazione incancellabile?” (222). 


9 For Lévinas’s relation to religion and thus God, I refer to the section “Religion” in The 
Levinas Reader (166-234). 


18 Dino S. Cervigni 


Several essays focus more specifically than others on the ethical challenges 
of the poet, the academic, the scholar, and the educator. 

In Italy’s cultural history, we recognize the privileged role of Dante as a 
poet-prophet. In the twentieth century, was it possible for any poet to play such 
a role? In “The Ethics and Pathos of Ungaretti’s ‘Ragioni d’una poesia,” 
Thomas E. Peterson discusses the articles, talks, and essays from the period 
1922 to 1969 that Giuseppe Ungaretti used to introduce his collected poems, 
entitled Vita d’un uomo (‘“Ragioni d’una poesia” Ixi-xcv). Having lived his first 
twenty-four years in Alexandria, Egypt, Ungaretti came to Italy in 1912, then 
moved to France. With the outbreak of World War I, he returned to Italy and 
enlisted. After the war, he again resided in Paris before moving to Rome in 1920, 
then taught in Brazil in the late 1930s, and subsequently moved to Rome. Much 
of the “Ragioni” is a revisitation of these places, viewed as sites of poetry and 
revelation. While the critical literature on the poetry is extensive, little has been 
written on the essays and almost nothing on Ungaretti’s autoexegesis. Peterson 
argues that here Ungaretti assumes the stature of the “poeta di oggi”: the poet of 
his day and of destiny. While such a claim is not unfamiliar to readers of such 
poets as Carducci or d’Annunzio, in Ungaretti’s case the monumental figure of 
the national poet or vate is turned upside down. Not a “hero” so much as a 
“nomad,” Ungaretti is only a vate in the oracular sense of one who sees deeper 
into nature, both its realities and illusions. Ungaretti is a poet-prophet, a modern 
classic who would speak in unanimity with the people, a “man of pain,” a 
wanderer and a visionary in an era of moral blindness. 

Through an analysis of the role of technology in Primo Levi and Daniele 
Del Giudice, Nicoletta Pireddu, in “Towards a Poet(h)ics of Techné: Primo 
Levi and Daniele Del Giudice,” explores the possibility of an ethical relationship 
able to transcend the divide between the aesthetic and the instrumental. La 
chiave a stella and Atlante Occidentale recodify technology by bringing it back 
to the Greek roots of techné as both craftmanship and art. But if technology 
works as an ethical device that promotes interpersonal connections through the 
act of making, Pireddu also examines the differences between Levi’s and Del 
Giudice’s conception of that relationship with the other. Whereas Levi endorses 
an ethics of responsibility founded upon an inevitable but ultimately unknowable 
alterity a /a Lévinas, Del Giudice constructs an interdiscursive and _ inter- 
emotional dimension aiming at reciprocity and symmetry. La chiave a stella and 
Atlante Occidentale can hence be seen as two consecutive steps in the creation 
of what Pireddu defines as a poet(h)ics of techné, namely, an alliance of poiesis, 
poetics and ethics, fostered by technology. 

Primo Levi’s work constitutes, once again, the focus of another essay. In 
“How Much Home Does a Person Need? Primo Levi and the Ethics of Home,” 
Robert S. C. Gordon examines the motif of the home as a marker of ethical 
practice and value played out across Levi’s writing, both in testimony and 
beyond. For Gordon, Levi draws a map of an ethical universe in an attempt to 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 19 


question the radical evil of the Holocaust and also the ordinary vices and the 
ordinary virtues of our everyday lives. Furthermore, Levi’s ability to look “at the 
Holocaust through the lens of ordinary values” and “the specific nature of the 
values in question [...] makes it possible for his work to resonate historically as a 
locus of confrontation between two faces of Europe’s past: its rationalist, 
bourgeois, post-Enlightenment, progressive face and its dark, violent, totalitarian 
face [...].°. Thus Gordon rereads Levi’s autobiographical writing, his 
extraordinary dialogue-from-afar with Jean Améry and his narrative, as Levi 
contrasts the experiences and myths of exile and deportation with the anchored, 
memory-centered affect of home, using this contrast to forge his very particular 
ethical voice. 

Sergio Parussa, in “Literature in Question: Figuring Auschwitz and 
Redefining Literature in Post-Holocaust Theatre,” focuses on recent works by 
contemporary Italian and American playwrights, who represent European 
totalitarianism and the Holocaust as metaphors for modernity. In exploring the 
pedagogical, theoretical, and artistic implications of these figurative parallels, 
Parussa points out how, by using the Holocaust as a metaphor, theater questions 
the balance among aesthetics, ethics, and politics in a literary text. Parussa’s 
considerations are worth the reader’s close attention, especially what he writes 
about the language “reduced to stammering, noise, and silence by the brutality of 
concentration camps.” In conclusion, Parussa argues that, in spite of Teodor W. 
Adorno’s statement that “there cannot be any poetry after Auschwitz [...], Levi’s 
work stands out as an example of trust in the act of writing, and as a challenge 
against those who would like the Holocaust to be unspeakable.” 

Giancarlo Gaeta, in “La scelta delle ‘cose migliori’. Intellettuali e societa 
di massa secondo Nicola Chiaromonte,” focuses on this journalist’s and critic’s 
legacy, which is striking for the directness and force of the ethical imperatives it 
proclaims. Paraphrasing Chiaromonte (Rapolla, Potenza, 1905 — Roma, 1972), 
Gaeta asks what is left nowadays of a millenary culture. Chiaromonte’s answer, 
albeit minimalist in its succinct appearance, is positive, although not absolutely 
categorical: ‘What is left, if it is left, is the ability to judge that what is good is 
good, what is evil is evil, and it cannot be otherwise (and one must not do 
anything to make it appear otherwise)” (Che cosa rimane 9; my trans.). Most 
appropriately, Gaeta concludes his essay by adding to Chiaromonte’s ideas his 
own belief (which I trust all people of good will do share) that the truth exists 
and is desirable, and that the good is both a need and an aspiration rooted in each 
one of us. 

Alessandro Montani, writing on ‘“Manganelli, ovvero  l’etica 
dell’intelligenza,” proclaims, since the beginning, the problematic nature of any 
ethical considerations on Manganelli, who remained always faithful to the notion 
of “letteratura come menzogna.” And yet, Montani convincingly shows not only 
Manganelli’s ability to identify the peculiarly literary traits of a work but also, at 
the same time, to uncover and unmask in the written work the darkest areas of 


20 Dino S. Cervigni 


the human psyche and the monsters that dwell within humankind. Thus Montani 
argues that Manganelli employed such ethical and religious notions as sin in 
evaluating so-called literary works aesthetically as well as ethically and 
ontologically. For Manganelli — Montani reminds us — “La scrittura [...] é 
probabilmente un esercizio sul nulla, un’esplorazione del nulla, ma, appunto, 
non deve essere il niente.” From this ethical statement derives the critic’s ethical 
imperative: that of unmasking and denouncing the nothingness of literature 
whenever such is the case. Furthermore, Manganelli reminds us of “the infernal 
moment of literature,” the centrality of this infernal moment, the immorality of 
literature in presenting it and of the critic in accepting it, instead of condemning 
it. 

Francesca Parmeggiani, in ‘“Morselli e il tempo,” focuses on a 
fundamental issue at the basis of any ethical discourse: the function and meaning 
of time in our human existence. Through the analysis of time here and now (the 
ancients’ hic et nunc), the rewriting of past history, and our looking ahead 
toward the future, Morselli seeks to construct another form of existence in which 
all individuals question the meaning of their own existence, while avoiding 
romantic, idealistic, and historicistic views, as well as what Morselli calls the 
“instancabile ‘iificazione’ delle cose (e degli esseri)”; namely, the “riduzione 
della realta a mero prodotto del pensiero, che ha portato a un soggettivismo 
esasperato, fonte ora solo di insicurezza e angoscia esistenziale.” 

In a regrettably extremely timely essay, “Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret 
Service: Questions of Accountability in the Cinema of the ‘stragi di stato,’” 
Giancarlo Lombardi seeks to probe the ethical responsibility of Italian film 
directors in rendering cinematically three tragic events in Italy’s recent history: 
the bombing of Piazza della Loggia, the crash of the Itavia flight in Ustica, and 
the bombing of Bologna’s railway station. Lombardi reminds us of the horror of 
terrorism: it strikes when least expected, chooses random targets out of ordinary 
people, and disseminates terror in entire populations. Lombardi argues that Italy 
experienced two forms of terrorism: left-wing terrorism, which disseminated 
terror through systematic knee-cappings, abductions, and homicides, while right- 
wing terrorism spread fear in the entire country through a series of bombings that 
caused countless victims. Focusing on three cinematic renderings of the tragic 
events mentioned above, Lombardi analyzes the encounter between ethics and 
cinema. Thus, he contends that, while cinema can hardly shed light on the 
darkness surrounding those mysterious episodes of recent Italian history, it may 
still accomplish a unique task: namely, proposing daring interpretations through 
which responsibility is inevitably assigned. Questioning moral accountability, 
placing blame, and asking viewers to bear testimony, this specific cinema fully 
engages in an ethical discourse rooted, according to Emmanuel Lévinas and 
other philosophers, in responsibility for and towards the other. Lombardi 
concludes with a statement that all of us fully accept: “Ethics teaches us not only 
to think about the other but also to act on behalf of the other, while prompting us 


Literature Criticism Ethics: An Introductory Essay 21 


to seek and question accountability.” 

Finally, | very much welcome the contribution of the only non-italianist 
scholar. Eugene TeSelle, in his “Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of 
Culture: A Retrospective Analysis,” invites all of us academics to reflect on 
other ways through which we may expand our mission as educators beyond its 
traditionally understood boundaries. TeSelle provides us with an excursus on the 
roles played by engaged intellectuals from antiquity to modern times. He points 
out the challenges of academics, who are often viewed as belonging to an ivory 
tower or an unrealizable world of ideas, when they decide to become directly 
engaged in matters concerning society at large. TeSelle presents the issue in 
poignant terms: “If the proper concern of intellectuals is with transcendent 
values, abstract ideas, theories with universal intent, or artistic representations 
that have potentially universal appeal, then how should this concern relate to 
particular social and political issues and to the intellectual’s own location within 
history?” In TeSelle’s view, there are three basic challenges facing intellectuals: 
1) They may be so caught up in ivory-tower research or artistic self-expression 
as to be indifferent to politics; (2) when they do engage themselves in politics, 
they too often become dangerously fanatical; and (3) in all cases they may be 
deceived about both their independence and their lack of self-interest.” Finally, 
TeSelle proposes an essential agenda of social commitment worth pursuing: that 
of the intellectual “who earns a living in academe but is involved in public issues 
on a ‘volunteer’ or ‘part-time’ basis.” TeSelle’s self-portrait is revealing and 
poignant for the three quite different public roles he has played in his adult life: 
those of professor, reverend, and citizen. This example of an academic outside 
Italian studies provides all of us Italianists, especially in North America, with 
valuable insights into possible contributions to the society at large outside the so- 
called ivory tower we inhabit. 


3. Final Remarks 

Faithful to the journal’s longstanding tradition of open-mindedness, this 2001 
issue has not tried to impose on its readers any specific perspective; at the same 
time, this issue’s contributions, spanning several centuries of Italy’s literary 
culture, challenge all of us by foregrounding the primacy of ethhics. 

The tragic events of September 11, 2001, have made it clear to all of us that 
we, as educators, scholars of literature, and critics are called upon to view the 
ethical issue not just as one of the many elements of culture but as the foremost 
component of our lives, the society we live in, and our mission as educators. 

This volume does not, and could not, touch upon all aspects concerning the 
theoretical, applied, and practical spheres of ethics. Concerning the theoretical 
sphere, further areas of investigation may focus on the position of ethics vis-a-vis 
the artistic and the aesthetic;!9 the relevancy of ethics in writing, publishing, and 


10 For the hisotry of aesthetics, I refer to the bibliography in Eco’s volume. 


22 Dino S. Cervigni 


interpreting; the moral and social responsibilities of literature and hence of 
criticism; the historical development of ethical criticism; the ethical dimensions 
of specific theoretical approaches. Concerning the applied sphere, ethical 
hermeneutics must by necessity be applied to the analysis of literary texts 
throughout Italy’s literary culture. Finally, in relation to the practical sphere, we 
must be willing to question ourselves ethically, ponder our ethical obligations as 
scholars, teachers, and educators, and be ready to carry out our mission in 
accordance with the principles of a well-informed, and well-formed, moral 
conscience, while respecting the other — that is, every human being — in the 
same manner as we want the other to respect us. 

At the threshold of the third millennium, every one is, more than ever 
before, confronted by rapidly evolving technological advancements, the ongoing 
changes on the world’s political map, and the profound transformation of 
individuals’ and society’s life as it was known until very recently. Furthermore, 
we have all been shocked by the most horrendous act of terrorism in American 
history, and look ahead with much trepidation and anxiety to a world that may be 
forever destabilized because of our inability to understand and respect each 
other. One must, therefore, all the more rely on every individual’s ability to 
evaluate and question contemporary society’s myriad choices, alternatives, and 
possibilities in the public and private spheres. Thence derives the essentiality, 
indeed the primacy of ethics: namely, the ability of individuals to discern and to 
pursue what everybody desires — the true bonum — for their physical, 
intellectual, and spiritual welfare, within the context of the ever expanding global 
community and a temporal frame of reference that goes beyond the ephemeral 
and therefore cannot but be teleological. 


The University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill

Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 


At the beginning of the Divina Commedia, the protagonist, already in the middle 
of his life’s journey, has wandered off the straight path and lost his way in a dark 
wood. After centuries of commentary, there can be little doubt that the “diritta 
via,” now “smarrita,” represents the virtuous life that leads to God and eternal 
salvation and that in stepping off the direct route, Dante the pilgrim has made his 
journey to Heaven more roundabout and arduous. As Dante the poet explains in 
his treatise, the Convivio, the supreme desire of every created thing is to return to 
its maker, but we may lose the path through error: 


Che si come d’una cittade a un’altra di necessitade € una ottima e dirittissima via, e 
un’altra che sempre se ne dilunga (cioé quella che va ne l’altra parte), e molte altre quale 
meno allungandosi e quale meno appressandosi, cosi ne la vita umana sono diversi 
camini, de li quali uno é veracissimo e un altro é fallacissimo, e certi meno fallaci e certi 


meno veraci. 
[4.12.18] 


There is available to the pilgrim, on the one hand, the Lord’s straight highway 
through the wilderness prophesied in Isaiah 40.3, and, on the other, the 
labyrinthine wanderings of the ancient Israelites in the desert for forty years. ! 
Like Israel, the pilgrim does reach his promised goal in the long run; indeed, | 
will argue that the simplest way to describe the narrative structure of the entire 
poema is in terms of departure and return, or loss and recovery.2 There are 


| Raffa (Dialectic 100) points out that St. Augustine yokes together several of the 
figurative markers of sin from Isaiah 40.4, including the image of the winding path made 
straight. in Confessions 9.4, and that for Augustine, iniquity is not a substance but a 
perverse swerving of the will away from God (Conf. 7.16). According to Raffa, “[i]f 
Dante’s way in the Commedia is the ‘straight way,” this is because he conceives of the 
alternative — veering off course and following a tortuous route — as an overarching 
metaphor for the moral and societal woes of his world” (Divine Dialectic 105). For an 
interpretation of the Divine Comedy as patterned after the Jewish exodus from Egypt to 
Jerusalem, see Mazzotta, Desert, esp. 120-21. 

2 See Martinez (“Lament”) for an analysis of the Purgatorio, and the Christian salvation 
history on which it is based, as expressing “a shift from laughter to tears, or as a palatal 


Annali d'Italianistica 19 (2001) 


26 Olivia Holmes 


numerous other Biblical and patristic precedents for figuring the good life as the 
right road; see, for instance, Psalm 1.6: “Quoniam novit Dominus viam iustorum; 
/ Et iter impiorum peribit” (“For the Lord knows the way of the just, and the way 
of the wicked shall perish”) as one example. Classical precedents abound, too, 
and critics have connected the character Dante’s taking the wrong path with the 
Pythagorean topos — picked up by early Christian writers such as St. Jerome 
and Lactantius — of comparing human existence to the letter “Y”. The fork 
represents the choice in each man’s life between virtue and vice.? In some 
medieval commentaries on Virgil’s Aeneid, the golden bough given Aeneas in 
Book Six is interpreted as the Pythagorean “Y,” symbol of virtuous choice.* 

As Mazzotta has indicated, Dante’s epic consists of the continual 
dramatization of ethical choices, and Dante (or the pseudo-Dante) explicitly 
inscribes the poetry of the third canticle within the orbit of ethics, or moral 
philosophy, in the Epistle to Can Grande (Dante’s Vision 23). This inscription is 
conventional: medieval commentaries typically begin by assigning the work 
under consideration to its proper part of philosophy, and poetry is generally 
classified as belonging to ethics (Allen 5-6). The twelfth-century Arab 
philosopher Averroés (translated into Latin in the thirteenth) paraphrased 
Aristotle’s Poetics as saying “Every poem and all poetry are either blame or 
praise,” and his understanding was also extremely influential.» Poetry was seen 
as teaching by offering examples of both virtuous and sinful behavior to be 
either imitated or eschewed. For medieval critics, the intent of literature was 
indisputably that of influencing ethical decisions. But in ranking the sciences in 
Convivio 2.14, Dante makes an extraordinary claim for ethics, unsupported by 


inflection from sweet to bitter, and back again, through the redemption wrought by 
Christ” (49). His “Mourning Beatrice” reads the Vita nuova similarly in terms of the 
dialectic between Threni (Lamentations) and the Song of Songs.

earlier classical or medieval authorities, by moving it to first place, ahead of 
physics and metaphysics (Gilson, Dante 99-112). And whereas the attribution of 
a didactic function, especially to classical texts, may seem to impose an alien 
ethos onto what we now usually take as essentially aesthetic constructs — as 
when Fulgentius and his imitators read the Aeneid as an allegory of human life 
from birth to maturity with Aeneas as a sort of everyman — in Dante’s case, the 
ethical meaning is inscribed deeply within the poem. According to the Epistle to 
Can Grande, “If indeed the work is taken allegorically, the subject is man, in the 
exercise of his free will, earning or becoming liable to the rewards or 
punishments of justice.”® 

The entire Commedia can be interpreted as an exemplary acting out of the 
consequences of free will, and the poet-protagonist’s journey is from the slavery 
of sensual desire through the liberation of the will so that he can freely choose 
virtue. According to Aristotle’s Ethics, an important text for Dante, “the 
incontinent man acts from desire but not from choice, while contrariwise the 
continent man acts from choice but not from desire (3.2).”’ At the beginning of 
Purgatorio, Virgil says to Cato of the character Dante, “liberta va cercando” 
(1.71). This central canticle is itself centered around a discussion of the concept 
of “libero arbitrio” in cantos 16-18, and when the pilgrim arrives at the top of the 
mountain of Purgatory, Virgil declares his will now “libero, dritto e sano” 
(27.140). Dante’s upright will (“dritto arbitrio”) corresponds to the straight road 
(“diritta via”) that he lost at the poem’s opening. He is finally back on track, and 
the last canticle — in which a “beatitudo” both Aristotelian and Christian is 
represented — may be seen as gratuitous, in terms of the narrative, as an extra, 
an act of grace, or contemplative corollary.8 Early in the Paradiso, the pilgrim is

My point is not the temporary impasse in which he finds himself here, but simply

As I pointed out before, Dante depicts himself as having already made a 
wrong choice and stepped off the true path when the Commedia begins. In the 
context of the poet’s entire corpus, this erroneous choice is implicitly portrayed 
as a flirtation with a woman other than Beatrice, the “gentile donna” of Vita 
nuova 35.2, allegorized as Lady Philosophy in Convivio 2.12. The choice 
between the paths of virtue and vice is always also an erotic choice between two 
courtly beloveds. The pilgrim’s return to moral righteousness and _ perfect 
happiness is dramatically staged in his reunion with his original beloved Beatrice 
in the Earthly Paradise where she accuses her lover: after her death he “‘volse i 
passi suoi per via non vera / imagini di ben seguendo false” (Purg. 30.130-31).

placer volser miei passi” (Purg. 31.35; see also Par. 1.130-35). In order that he

Sirens in the future, Beatrice explains that she represented for him the “sommo 
piacer,” and he never should have been drawn toward “pargoletta / o altra 
novita” (Purg. 31.43-60). An enormous amount of critical literature has been 
devoted to identifying this new “pargoletta,” or young girl, and the precise nature 
of the guilt of which Beatrice accuses the pilgrim, but I simply mean to point out

Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 29 


that here, as elsewhere in Dante’s writings, the two ways are two ladies, and the 
downward pull of the vicious lifestyle is figured as an act of seduction.!! 

The election in one’s lifestyle of the path of virtue or pleasure is already 
represented as an erotic choice between two women in the classical allegory of 
Hercules at the Crossroads, a version of the Pythagorean “Y.” Dante would have 
known this story principally by way of Cicero’s De officiis, in which it is 
recounted that when Hercules was just becoming a young man, he went out to a 
lonely place and pondered which road to take, for he could see two: “unam 
Voluptatis, alteram Virtutis” (“one of pleasure and another of virtue” 
1.32.118).!2 The two alternatives are not explicitly personified as two women in 
the De officiis, but they are in Xenophon’s Memorabilia, which Cicero cites as 
his source. Xenophon, an ancient Greek general and writer, was unknown in the 
Latin West during the Middle Ages, however, and Panofsky argues that the 
Hercules tale, which became a favorite theme of the humanists, was neglected by 
medieval scholarship because it implies a secular moral context that was 
unimaginable for the medieval Christian, since the two roads stand for what is 
worthy of praise or of blame in an exclusively earth-bound ethical system (155- 
56). Emma Edelstein counters, however, that in Xenophon’s story Hercules 
accomplishes such meritorious deeds that he lives among the immortals — 
Heaven is his reward for choosing Virtue — and that the story was understood in 
this eminently Christian sense by early Christian writers like Justin Martyr and 
St. Basil.!3 Justin Martyr relates Xenophon’s personifications with Vice in a 
luxurious dress and with a seductive expression on her face, and Virtue with a 
squalid look and dress, but offering the promise of everlasting grace, which he 
interprets to mean that “every one who flees those things that seem to be good, 
and follows hard after what are reckoned difficult and strange, enters into 
blessedness” (Ch. 11). Yet Dante is unlikely to have known this version of the 
incident, even indirectly; once again we come up against the stumbling block of 
Greek. 


Il See esp. Dronke, Dante 77-79. According to Frye, it is a convention of the nineteenth- 
century novel that the hero must choose between the love of two women, one dark and 
one light, and the plot usually requires getting rid of the dark one if the story is to end 
happily (101). Dante’s figures are perhaps among the ancestors of this more modern set 
of archetypes, both of which are versions — at least partially — of the abiding 
virgin/whore dichotomy. Dante makes use of (and problematizes) familiar female 
stereotypes in constructing his complex symbols for the abstractions of virtue and vice. | 
am not denying, however, the simultaneous literal imports of Beatrice and her rival.

passage from his De Cain et Abel, in which the saint personifies pleasure and 
virtue as two women who dwell within us (PL 14:322C). One might also cite St. 
Jerome, who explicitly connects Ecclesiastes 10.2, “Cor sapientis in dextera eius, 
/ Et cor stulti in sinistra illius” (“The wise man’s heart leads him aright, / the 
fool’s heart leads him astray”), with the Sybil’s words to Aeneas when he arrives 
at the place where the road forks, leading to Elysium on the right and Tartarus on 
the left (Aeneid 6.540-43).!4 In numerous other patristic and later commentaries 
as well, the Christian is seen to choose between two roads: “una culpabilis, altera 
laudabilis,” “una carnis, altera spiritus,” “una mortis, altera vitae.” !5 Thus it is 
perhaps safe to assume that the theme of Hercules in bivio — so similar to that 
of the Pythagorean “Y,” and also alluded to by both Boccaccio and Petrarch 
(Smarr 146-52; Mommsen 175-96) — was not entirely uncongenial to the 
medieval thinker. It loosely resembles the story of Jesus’s temptation by the 
devil in the desert (Matt. 4.1),!© and in the wake of Boethius’s Consolation of 
Philosophy (4 meter 7), Hercules was taken as a prototype of the vir sapiens et 
eloquens, whose activities conquered vice and ultimately earned him access to 
the contemplative life, as well as of Jesus Christ himself, who harrowed hell and 
conquered heaven with his labors.'!’ Dante identifies Hercules as a surrogate 
Christ in /nferno 9.98-99, and almost all the classical monsters that the pilgrim 
encounters in the lower realm were conquered by Hercules, too, in the course of 
his labors (Miller 1-17).

own implicit self-presentation as a “Hercules” choosing between two ways and 
two women, for the conflation of imagery of itinerary and erotic choice is also 
scriptural. In Proverbs 7, for example, “Solomon” warns the Israelite to prefer

Wisdom to the ‘“mulier ornatu meretricio” (“woman dressed as a harlot,” 7.10). 
He cautions, “Ne abstrahatur in viis illius mens tua, / Neque decipiaris semitis 
eius / [...] / Viae inferi domus eius” (“Let not your mind be drawn away in her 
ways: do not be deceived with her paths [...] Her house is the way to hell,” 7.25-

stulta et clamosa” (Proverbs 9.13), “via mulieris adulterae” (Proverbs 30.20), are 
repeatedly contrasted with the righteous path of her rival, ‘‘sapientia.” Part 
personification of an attribute of God and part goddess, Sapientia appears 
throughout the wisdom writings of the Hebrew Bible; she is the master 
craftswoman at God’s side in shaping the world, his wise counselor, queen, and 
mother.!9 But in a radical act of Christian revisionism, St. Paul identified Jesus 
Christ with “Dei virtutem, et Dei sapientiam” (“the power of God and the 
wisdom of God”; |. Cor. 1.24; see Par. 23.37), and medieval Christian thinkers 
came to see Christ the Word, second person of the Trinity, as foreshadowed in or 
identical with the Old Testament figure of Wisdom.2° As Charles Singleton has 
convincingly argued, when Beatrice reappears at the summit of Purgatory, she 
bears a resemblance not only to Christ, but to this female-gendered version of 
Christ, Sapientia, who was identified also with Sanctifying Grace (Dante Studies 
esp. 2:78-84 and 122-34). 

Let us trace some of the different forms taken by Dante’s portrayal of the 
ethical options available to the traveler-as-lover (to Homo viator doubling as 
homo amator) as an erotic choice between two attractive women. In a sequence 
of transformations that is a play of sameness and difference, Dante’s two 
beloveds appear in various incarnations in his various texts.2! I have already 
mentioned the contrast between Beatrice and the “donna gentile” in the Vita 
nuova, and between Beatrice and the “pargoletta” in the Earthly Paradise (Purg. 
31). There are other versions of the same paradigm. The first poem of the

popolari e colti, religiosi e laici [...]” (7).

Ecclesiasticus, and, according to some modern interpreters, Psalms, Song of Songs, and 
others (Crenshaw 801). On the theological concept of Sapientia (or, in the New 
Testament, Sophia), see Newman 9-37. 

20 See Thomas Aquinas, Summa 4.12: “How the Son of God may be called the Wisdom 
of God.” 

21 As Corti points out, “Ogni opera di Dante é un mondo a sé, un microcosmo, una stella 
che ruota sulla propria traiettoria entro il macrocosmo dantesco,” but, at the same time, 


“esiste un continuum nello sviluppo di un artista, che Dante stesso ci ricorda [...]” (154). 


32 Olivia Holmes 


Convivio, “Voi che ’ntendendo il terzo ciel movete,” belongs to the lyric genre, 
conventional among the troubadours, called chansons de congé (songs replacing 
one courtly beloved with another) by Jeanroy, and “narratives of two women” by 
Kay. In Dante’s canzone, the conflict is staged between “un soave pensier” of 
an angel in Heaven and “uno spiritel d’amor gentile” that advises obeisance to a 
new lady. In the Convivio’s prose commentary, this “battaglia intra lo pensiero” 
(2.2.3) is allegorized as a conflict of loyalties between dedication to Beatrice and 
to “la filosofia” (2.12.5), with Lady Philosophy winning at this stage. Whereas in 
the Vita nuova, the lover’s substitution of the “donna gentile” for Beatrice, after 
her death, is presented as an act of weakness and contrary to reason, in the 
Convivio, the “noble lady” has become a personification of human reason, and 
Beatrice is already becoming, by implication, something like Faith. Despite the 
martial vocabulary of the canzone, the new little spirit of love does not really 
appear to be in conflict with the thought of the blessed lady in the treatise, or 
even an independent and entirely adequate substitute for her, but rather to have 
come to Beatrice’s aid in the absence of her corporeal presence, “che l’aspetto 
suo [i.e., della gentile donna] aiuta la nostra fede” (3.7.15). 

Quite a number of other poems by Dante might also be classified as 
chansons de congé: in addition to the third canzone of the Convivio, “Le dolci 
rime d’amor ch’i’ solia” (in which the speaker claims to turn away from love 
altogether), three from the Vita nuova, “Cavalcando laltr’ier per un cammino,” 
“L’amaro lagrimar che voi faceste,” and “Gentil pensero che parla di vui” (the 
first of which marking a turn from one screen-lady to another, and the latter two 
that from Beatrice to the “donna gentile”); as well as, among the “rime 
estravaganti,” “Parole mie che per lo mondo siete” (which takes leave of the 
“donna gentile” again) and ‘Per quella via che la bellezza corre” (in which 
Lisetta is dismissed by another woman, presumably Beatrice). In Purgatorio 
19.7-33, the theme of the battle between ladies returns: the pilgrim Dante has a 
dream in which a “femmina balba,” who is turned into “dolce serena” by the 
dreamer’s amorous gaze, is put to rout by a “donna [...] santa e presta,” whom 
commentators generally interpret as representing something like Reason, Virtue, 
or Charity.23 

Already in some early versions of the allegory, however, the relation 
between the two roads and the two ladies (and thus between the individual’s 
basic options) seems to be one of continuity and hierarchy, rather than strict 


22 Jeanroy 1:121; Kay 26. See also my discussion of this genre (which I refer to there as 
the “chanson de change,” following Salverda de Grave) in Assembling the Lyric Self, esp. 
44-45. 

23 Dante’s clearest model for this dream is the opening scene of the Consolation of 
Philosophy, although its transformation is complex, and Lady Philosophy can be loosely

Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 33 


opposition. In Dante’s sonnet “Due donne in cima de la mente mia,” the 
personifications “Bellezza” and “Virtu” speak to the poet’s intellect “e fan 
quistion come un cor puote stare / intra due donne con amor perfetto”; their 
debate is related to the scholastic quaestio, alluded to in Paradiso 4, of how the 
free will chooses between two equal options. The issue is resolved here by “‘il 
fonte del gentil parlare” (Love), who declares that one can love both: Beauty 
“per diletto” and Virtue “per operare.”24 This duality is clearly analogous to 
Dante’s discussion in Book 4 of the Convivio (following Aristotle’s Ethics, Bk. 
10) of the “due felicitadi” that we can have in this life, “secondo due diversi 
cammini, buono e ottimo, che a cid ne menano: I’una é la vita attiva, e l’altra la 
contemplativa”; by means of the first, we arrive at “buona felicitade,” while by 
means of the second, at “ottima felicitade e beatitudine” (4.17.9). These two 
ways of life are the meaning, he tells us (adhering to a conventional medieval 
interpretation), of the story of Mary and Martha in the gospel of Luke: when 
Christ is received in their house, Martha busies herself with serving, and he says 
her service is necessary, but Mary, who only sits at her Savior’s feet and listens, 
has chosen the better part (“‘ottima parte ha eletta”; 4.17.10). Despite Dante’s 
elevation of ethics over the other sciences in Convivio 2.14, he remains faithful 
to Christ’s (and Aristotle’s) assertion of the primacy of the contemplative over 
the active intellect.2> He presents the lesser of the two, however, which he 
associates with the donna gentile, as still able to console the lover, and to 
provide happiness in this life, just as in the dialectical thought of St. Thomas 
Aquinas, rational truth appears as a lower, but not contradictory, aspect of the 
truth of faith, and nature is perfected, but not supplanted, by Grace.?®

In the pilgrim’s third dream, in Purgatorio 27.94-108, Dante adopts, rather, 
two Old Testament women, Leah and Rachel, as symbols of the two ways of 
life.2” The appearance of Leah, gathering flowers and singing, anticipates that of 
Matelda in the following canto; and the relation of continuity between Leah and 
Rachel (‘“good:better” rather than “bad:good”) also anticipates that between 
Beatrice’s forerunner Matelda and Beatrice herself.28 But Dante’s true beloved 
is also in her turn only the morning star, a herald or harbinger of the Virgin

27 The canonical interpretation of Mary and Martha as types of the vita activa and

28 In his reading of Purg. 27, Contini asserts that the entire plan of the Commedia should 
be connected, rather than to the Convivio’s thesis of “due felicitadi,” to the corrected 
variant in Monarchia 3.16.7-8, in which Dante defines man’s two goals as “happiness in 
this life,” which “is figured in the earthly paradise,” and “happiness in the eternal life,” 
which “is signified by the heavenly paradise.” Dante goes on to say that the first can be

made subservient to the Pope, who guides in accordance with Revelation. See also Marco

29 Both recall Song of Songs 6.9: “Quae est ista quae progreditur quasi aurora

Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 35 


Empyrean, she sits “nel terzo giro / dal sommo grado” (‘in the third circle from 
the highest tier,” Par. 31.67-8), below Mary and Eve, but next to Rachel. As 
Gorni points out, the first two woman, Mary and Eve, are in an oppositional 
relation, which Dante underlines in the ferzina, “La piaga che Maria richiuse e 
unse, / quella ch’é tanto bella da’ suoi piedi / é colei che l’aperse e che la punse” 
(Par. 32.4-6), but Rachel and Beatrice crowd the third seat ‘ta pari merito” 
(Lettera 81). Rachel and Beatrice are interchangeable here as symbols for the 
contemplative life and its corresponding “beatitudine.”>° 

Like the diametric opposition of two different and antithetical alternatives, 
this figural relation of resemblance and continuity between options, and 
dialectical resolution, also has scriptural justification. As Jacoff has documented, 
Leah and Rachel (and other similar pairs of siblings in Genesis narratives) were 
widely allegorized by Christian exegetes, not only as types of the active and 
contemplative life, but also as figures for “the Old Law and the New, the Old 
Testament and the New, the Synagogue and the Church” (6). Whereas the Old 
Testament and classical traditions generally depict the individual deciding 
between a good road and a bad one, the New Testament offers the possibility of 
choosing between a good road and a better one. “Ego vox clamantis in deserto: 
Dirigite viam Domini,” says John the Baptist in John 1.23, echoing Isaiah 40.3 
(and the Baptist’s words are cited by Dante in Vita nuova 24.4 apropos of 
Beatrice’s predecessor Giovanna/Primavera); “Ego sum via et veritas et vita,” 
says Jesus Christ later in the same gospel (14.6).3! The Christian needs to pass 
by way of the Hebrew Bible in order to reach the gospels with understanding, to 
go through corporeal things to get to spiritual ones. Dante’s ethical concept 
involves a necessary and gradual passage from lower to higher objects of love, 
and is essentially Platonic, as in St. Augustine’s parable of life as a wandering 
away from and back to our native country, rather than exclusively dualistic, as in 
the Church Father Lactantius, according to whom the two roads actually lead in 
opposite directions.32 Closer to Dante’s own time, mystic thinkers like Richard

appearance in Purgatory 9, where she carries the pilgrim, while he sleeps and dreams, up 
the steep climb below the entrance to the terraces of Purgatory proper, thus allowing the 
poet to rise to a higher theme (see the address to the reader in Purg. 9. 70-72).
