between Cavalcanti and Dante) can also be detected throughout the lita nuova between 
the two “screen-ladies” and Beatrice: see my Assembling 129-44. 

32 Divine Institutes 6.3, trans. McDonald 398. Augustine warns that if the actual 
traveling delighted us too much, “Nollemus cito viam finire et perversa suavitate 
implicati alienaremur a patria, cuius savitas faceret beatos” (“we would be reluctant to 
finish our journey quickly, being ensnared in the wrong kind of pleasure and estranged 
from the homeland whose pleasures could make us happy”; De Doctrina Christiana 


36 Olivia Holmes 


of St. Victor and St. Bonaventura effaced or bridged the distinctions between the 
traditional stages or psychological faculties by which we progress from the 
lowest to the highest, or from the temporal to the eternal, by inserting 
intermediate terms and suggesting that every step in this unified upward progress 
represents some form of revelation (Vossler 1:144). 

Another notion relevant to the formation of Dante’s allegory of ethical and 
intellectual choice is the tradition of personifying heavenly and earthly love as 
twin goddesses. Plato’s Symposium describes two Aphrodites: a Heavenly one, 
accompanied by love of the soul, and her counterpart, the Common or Popular 
Aphrodite, whose partner is indiscriminate physical love (180d-182a). This 
concept was picked up by Plotinus and the Neoplatonics, who interpreted the 
Heavenly Aphrodite in an abstract, metaphysical way as “the Intellectual 
Principle” or “the Soul at its divinest: unmingled as the immediate emanation of 
the unmingled; remaining ever Above,” and she eventually became Boethius’s 
“amor / Quo caelum regitur,” namely, the love that rules the universe, binding all 
things together.33 This cosmological principle of universal harmony did not 
remain entirely aloof from Matter, however, but developed a downward 
tendency and a “mingled” nature in later thought with the Incarnation of Christ. 
The twin goddesses also survived into medieval Neoplatonism; according to 
Bernardus Silvestris’s Commentary on the Aeneid, for instance: 


[T]here are indeed two Venuses, one lawful, and the other the goddess of lust. The lawful 
Venus is the harmony of the world, that is, the even proportion of worldly things, which 
some call Astrea, and others call natural justice. This subsists in the elements, in the stars, 
in the seasons, in living beings. The shameless Venus, however, the goddess of lust, is 
carnal concupiscence which is the mother of all fornications.>4 


In his prosimetrum De planctu naturae, Bernardus’s pupil at Chartres, Alanus de 
Insulis or Alan of Lille (c. 1128-1202), recombines the opposing Venuses, but 
then distinguishes between two aspects of Venus’s character, cae/estis and 


l.iv.4; trans. R. Green; ). Also see Boethius, according to whom all men strive to obtain 
happiness (“beatitudinem”) by different paths, but they are led astray after false gods 
(Consolation 3. p. 2). 

33 Plotinus, Enneads 3.5.2; Consolation 2. m. 8; see: Dronke, “L’amor che move il sole”; 
Economou 17-50; Groos 241-46. The idea of two Venuses or two kinds of love that are 
associated with her can also be found at the beginning of Book 4 of Ovid’s Fasti, where 
Venus is invoked as the “mother of the twin loves,” and in Pythagorean writings of late 
antiquity, where the traditional deities, including Venus, are placed in two 
“hemispheres,” one celestial and one infernal, so as to make pairs (Robertson 124-25).

Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 37 


scelestis, corresponding to a divine and a corrupt (or human) love, who war for 
possession of the human soul (“Venus in Venerem pugnans”; | m. 1.5). 

Dante, too, distinguishes two different Venuses in the Commedia: “Lo bel 
pianeta che d’amar conforta” (Purg. 1.19), which he sees in the east when he 
emerges at daybreak on the shore of the island of Purgatory, and “la bella 
Ciprigna,” which the world once believed, to its peril, that “il folle amore / 
raggiasse” (Par. 8.2-3). In the figure of Beatrice, Dante combines Bernardus 
Silvestris’s Astraea, the lawful Venus, with the Virgin goddess of Justice whose 
predicted return in Virgil’s Fourth Eclogue was taken by centuries of exegetes as 
a prophecy of Christ’s advent. But more than the presence of specific literary 
allusions, what I am trying to establish is the general influence on Dante of the 
notion of two competing, but almost identical, female archetypes. The 
ambiguous valence of these two ladies, who stand roughly for profane and sacred 
love — or Nature and Grace — permeates his cultural references. As Peter 
Dronke indicates, although there could not be a complete identity between the 
two kinds of love in more orthodox Christian thought, since “the one was an 
absolute value, the other a relative one, at best imperfect, at worst evil,” the 
language of mystics such as Bernard of Clairvaux was to a great extent simply a 
transference of the sensual imagery of the Song of Songs and of the language of 
human lovers, while the imagery of courtly love-poets such as Guido Guinizzelli 
was frequently the same as that of the mystics (Medieval Latin 1:57-62). 

In the first canzone of the Convivio, “Voi che ’ntendendo il terzo ciel 
movete,” and its exegesis, Dante specifically blames the influence of the Heaven 
of Venus for his attraction to the Donna Gentile, Beatrice’s rival in the Vita 
nuova.>> In the Commedia, Leah is associated with the planet Venus, appearing 
to the pilgrim in a dream just before dawn, “Ne l’ora [...] che de l’oriente / 
prima raggiO nel monte Citerea” (Purg. 27.94-94). One canto later, Matelda is 
also associated with Venus (this time, with the goddess herself); the poet says of 
Matelda’s eyes: “Non credo che splendesse tanto lume / sotto le ciglia a Venere” 
(Purg. 28.64-65). In Paradiso 8, the pilgrim Dante actually rises to the sphere of 
Venus, which is described as “la stella / che ’! sol vagheggia or da coppa or da 
ciglio” (Par. 8.11-12). By “wooing the sun, now at the nape (from behind), now 
at the brow (from in front),” the poet is referring to the planet’s appearance as 
morning or evening star, heralding or concluding the day. If Dante’s other 
various beloveds correspond to Venus in her capacity as morning star, then 
Beatrice corresponds to the sun, as we have already seen, but the equation Venus

: Sun is complicated by the traditional notion that there are two Venuses, as well 
as by Dante’s argument in Purgatorio 16 that there are two suns. And the 
analogy is further complicated by Beatrice’s role as forerunner of or figure for 
the Virgin Mary.2° The constant splintering and play of reference from figures to 
fulfillments that are also figures, in their turn, is dizzying. 

Numerous different reference points and commonplaces come together in 
Dante’s image of a choice between paths and beloveds. As | have tried to show, 
this allegory is a palimpsest on which elements of classical culture (such as the 
Hercules fable and that of the two Venuses), Scripture (especially the 
personifications of Wisdom and Folly), and Romance (the courtly love lyric in 
general, and the chanson de congé in particular) are constantly being written 
over each other. But the sources that | have mentioned are just tips of the 
iceberg: Dante’s figures are spectacularly over-determined. | am not claiming to 
assert anything that has not generally been observed regarding the poet before. 
Scholars such as Bruno Nardi, Marie Thérése D’Alverny, and Maria Corti, 
among others, have extensively explored the intersection, especially in Dante’s 
Convivio, of philosophy with theology, or classical authors such as Aristotle and 
Cicero, on the one hand, with the Wisdom of the Books of Solomon .and the 
Word of the Gospel of John, on the other. The confluence of human science and 
revealed doctrine is already evident before Dante in the medieval commentary 
tradition on the Consolation of Philosophy, in which Boethius’s personification 
of Classical Philosophy is identified, by ideological extension, with the scriptural 
Sapientia Dei, and transformed into a hybrid, totalizing notion that, according to 
Corti (75-76), excludes any distinction between the sacred and the profane.>7 
Boethius’s Consolation, which Dante claims to have read shortly after Beatrice’s 
death, is also the most important and obvious source for Dante’s theme of the 
battle between ladies. It begins with Lady Philosophy driving out the 
meretricious Muses, who encouraged the sick protagonist to wallow in the sweet 
poison of self-pity. Boethius’s personification is abstract and hard to picture, 
neither young nor old, tall nor short, and not a flesh-and-blood woman (although 
a spiritualized one) like Beatrice; but her opposition to the Muses allows Dante 
to recast their struggle in the terms of the lyric poetry of his own time as a battle 
between two “real” objects of affection.28 In Dante’s kaleidoscope of shifting 


36 Marianne Shapiro (110) points out the contrast between what is said of Cupid in the 
heaven of Venus, (he “sedette in grembo a Dido” Par. 8.8), and what is said of the two 
angels who drive off the serpent with their swords in Antepurgatory (“vegnon dal grembo 
di Maria” Purg. 8.37). The parallel supports my intuition that the Virgin is a version of 
the “good Venus.” Both Venus and Mary are mothers of Love, in fact. 

37 Also see D’Alverny 5-24; Dronke, “L’ amor” 404-06; Courcelle, esp. 29-66. 

38 It is this shift in Dante towards increasingly mimetic representations which gives rise 
to the seemingly endless, and largely fruitless, critical literature attempting to identify 
“positivistically” the ladies to whom Dante’s lyrics are addressed (the various screen- 


Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 39 


references, one lady displaces the other in continual sequence, for the Beatrice of 
the Commedia may also be interpreted as a resurrected Muse returning to re- 
evict Philosophy. 

Dante’s allegorical figures are not only “polysemous” and drawn from 
apparently conflicting symbolic systems, but they are also versions of the same, 
kaleidoscopic repetitions of the one true referent. In order to understand Dante’s 
imaginative transformation of different cultural traditions, | find very helpful 
Sigmund Freud’s description of the condensation of the ““dream-thoughts” in the 
production of dreams: “Not only are the elements of a dream determined by the 
dream-thoughts several times over, but the individual dream-thoughts are 
represented in the dream by several elements (/nferpretation 318).”39 Dante’s 
parable of two women both combines or layers a number of sources (“‘dream- 
thoughts”) in a single image, and repeatedly fractures a single idea into two 
different images that exist in a relation of figure and fulfillment, after the model 
of Christian figural exegesis, in which connections were established between Old 
Testament persons or events and Christ and the Redemption, so that the first was 
understood to signify the second, and the second to encompass and complete the 
first.49 Freud also found that dreams for the most part disregard logical relations 
such as those represented by the conjunctions “if,” “because,” “just as,” and 
“although,” as well as contraries and contradictories; even negation seems not to 
exist in them (/nterpretation 347-53). Thus the alternative “either/or” cannot be 
expressed, but both alternatives are usually inserted into the dream as though 
they were equally valid. Similarly, Dante’s various beloveds appear in a relation 
that is both contradictory and complementary. On the one hand, the “other 
women” are Beatrice’s rivals, and represent wrong paths that the Christian must 
avoid. On the other hand, they are her handmaids, and represent necessary 


ladies, Beatrice, the Pargoletta, the “donna gentile,” the “donna pietra,” Lisetta, etc.), 
what Contini calls, “il noioso problema delle molte donne dantesche™ (Rime 303). 

39 My use of psychoanalytic categories is not meant as a subjection of poetry or literature 
to “science” or “theory,” but arises, rather, from the conviction, as Brooks puts it, “that 
the materials on which they exercise their powers of analysis are in some basic sense the 
same: that the structure of literature is in some sense the stucture of mind” 
(Psychoanalysis 24; see also Trilling 956). White connects Freud’s reflections on the 
relations between the dream-content and dream-thoughts generally to the relation 
between literal and figurative meanings in poetry, and views the way that the individual 
dream-thoughts are represented in the dream by several elements “as a kind of 
metaphorical conflation — in the way that Dante represents the Divine Presence in his 
Commedia” (111). 

40 On medieval figural or typological interpretation in general, see Auerbach 11-76; on 
Dante’s use of it, Chydenius. A homology between figural allegory and the texture of 
divinitory dreams was already suggested in the Middle Ages: see Gregory, esp. 372 
(“Identiche le cause degli eventi futuri e delle immagini oniriche [...]”). 


40 Olivia Holmes 


choices or stages in the mind’s journey to God: one leads to the other, and the 
second fulfills the first.4! 

Among the distinct “dream-thoughts” that determined Dante’s oneiric 
narrative, | have placed the most emphasis until now on the universal story of the 
Christian’s journey from sin to salvation, but it was also determined by the 
poet’s historical memory of his own experience as a private and public 
individual, among other things. Dante depicts himself as facing not only the 
binary moral decision confronted by a Christian Hercules at the fork between 
roads leading to Heaven or Hell, but also the autobiographical choices made by 
the writer and intellectual on the way to relating his vision of God at the end of 
the Paradiso. And both as Christian and as historical author, he needs to take a 
detour, to descend before ascending. The story of Dante-the-pilgrim’s journey to 
salvation is always also a meta-textual story, about the poem’s own coming into 
being. Accordingly, the protagonist needs to err, to make the wrong choice, or 
his narrative journey would reach its destination too quickly. As Peter Brooks 
has compellingly shown by superimposing Freud’s model in Beyond the 
Pleasure Principle for the functioning of the mental apparatus onto that of the 
novel, the drive of narrative is ultimately the desire for the end and quiescence, 
but textual energy is always on the verge of premature death or short-circuit, 
which possibility can be represented by “all manner of threats to the protagonist 
or to any of the functional logics which demand completion: it most commonly 
takes the form of temptation to the mistaken erotic object choice” (“Freud’s 
Masterplot” 296-97).42 Dante’s plot is thus typically novelistic avant /a lettre, 
both specifically in terms of his masterwork, the Commedia, and more generally 
in terms of his entire corpus. 

Yet as we have seen, as the texts progress we learn that even the options 
represented by Beatrice and Lady Philosophy do not exist only in opposition, but 
also in continuity: Beatrice’s young admirer needs to pass by way of a devotion 
to “Philosophy” in mid-career in order to return eventually to “Theology,” to 
participate in the active life before the contemplative life, to love his neighbor in 


41 Raffa relates Dante’s ability to “have it both ways” on issues that would normally 
seem to require definitive “either/or” solutions — which he sees as a defining quality of 
the poet’s thought — to the paradoxical logic of the Incarnation, which violates the 
Aristotelian principle of non-contradiction by making Christ at once both completely 
human and completely divine (see the introduction to his Divine Dialectic). 

42 Also see Brooks’s Psychoanalysis and Storytelling, where he employs the notion of 
“fore-pleasure” from Freud's “Creative Writers and Daydreaming™ to imply “the 
possibility of fetishism, the interesting threat of being waylaid by some element along the 
way to the ‘proper’ end, taking some displaced substitute or simulacrum for the thing 
itself, a mystification in which most literature deals, sometimes eventually to expose the 
displacement or substitution as a form of false consciousness, sometimes to expose the 
end itself as a false lure” (30). In Dante’s case, we seem to be dealing with the former 
(the exposure of the substitute as a form of false consciousness). 


Dante's Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 41 


order to love God. The lover’s turn from one lady to another represents both the 
individual’s ethical deviation away from God in the direction of earthly pleasures 
and back towards God in an act of Christian conversion, as well as a single 
continuous motion. As Freccero has shown (Dante 73-74), Dante’s pilgrim is 
traveling upwards even during his descent into hell, for according to Aristotelian 
cosmology, one must travel downward with respect to our hemisphere in order to 
rise in an absolute sense. But his turn can also represent, for instance, the 
passage from one stage or poetic style to another in the poet’s artistic 
development. Beatrice’s death marks the transition from the simpler, more 
“courtly” manner of his early lyrics to the denser, more “philosophical” poems 
that generated the Convivio, and her reappearances at the end of the Vita nuova 
and in the Commedia set in motion a third, more theological phase, which is also 
a synthesis, a partial return to the more literal or mimetic, less overtly allegorical, 
style of his early poetry (see Nardi, Dal “Convivio” esp. 1-5). In the Commedia, 
the reason for the detour is even more explicit: Hell comes before Purgatory, just 
as contrition and confession must precede punishment and absolution.*? 

In the canzoni commented upon in the Convivio (where the commentator 
claims that he understands allegory in the sense that it is used by poets, in which 
the literal level is generally taken as fiction), the literal level, or vehicle, is still 
generated by the Romance imagery of “courtly love,” whereas the allegorical 
levels, or tenor, include meanings drawn from both secular philosophy and 
Christian theology. Yet even in the Convivio, Dante insists upon the authenticity 
and historicity of the experiences described in the poems.*4 In the Commedia, on 
the other hand, where the style is more mimetic and, according to Singleton’s 
influential dictum, “the fiction [...] is that it is not fiction,” this elaborate 


43 | mean to imply a rough analogy, not an exact equation. See Augustine, Confessions 
4.12: “descendite, ut ascendatis.” On the conceptual pattern of Descent to Humility 
before Ascent to Salvation, and its repetition throughout the Commedia, see esp. 
Singleton, “The Vistas” 77-80 and, more recently, Freccero, “Introduction” 172-79. 

44 D’Andrea makes this point initially about the Vita nuova, in which, he says, 
conventional lyrics acquire “valore di verita autobiografica”: the apparition of Love in 
the sonnet “Io mi senti’ svegliar dentro a lo core,” for instance, “é presentata, nel 
racconto in prosa che precede il sonetto, come un’ ‘imaginazione’, come un’ esperienza 
effettivamente vissuta al livello dell’ immaginazione e non come un espediente retorico” 
(73-74). Nardi, however, writes that in the second poem of the Convivio, “Amor che ne la 
mente mi ragiona,” “il velo dell’allegoria € diventato cosi trasparente che appena 
s’avverte [...] non puo trattarsi di donna mortale, per bella ch’essa sia” (Dal “Convivio” 
11-12). But the woman who is celebrated in this poem for her physical beauty, for her 
“occhi” and “dolce riso,” her “atti soavi” and a voice that “dolcemente sona,” is still, at 
least ostensibly, the Romance lover’s courtly beloved. See also Damon, who says of the 
canzoni of the Convivio, “Dante clearly invites the reader to come at their ultimate 
meaning through a surface that is, within the limits of a very conventionalized mode of 
representing reality, real” (“The Two Modes” 149). 


42 Olivia Holmes 


scaffolding is removed, and the literal level becomes identical with the 
anagogical one, the literal subject of the poem being “status animarum post 
mortem” or the state of souls after death.4> But, inasmuch as Beatrice is the 
poet’s “real” beloved whom he lost to an early death and then recovers upon 
being reunited with her in the Purgatorio, this level of the text is also still 
generated in part by the erotic language of vernacular lyric (both Dante’s own 
and that of earlier poets). In the end, the love that inspires him as courtly lover 
turns out to be the same at that which moves the sun and the other stars.*® | do 
not mean to suggest that for Dante there is no such thing as “bad” love, however. 
As I have already established, he shows the individual as choosing between a 
straight road and a crooked one, and once the protagonist is inside the threshold 
of Purgatory proper, we learn that “mal amor” keeps souls away from this gate 
“perché fa parer dritta la via torta” (Purg. 10.2-3).47 Dante’s admittedly sensual 
love for Beatrice never partakes of this “mal amor,” however, for mediation 
turns out to be possible. In the philosophy of St. Thomas, for instance, man’s 
place is between the beast and the angel, or matter and God, and reason has a 
similarly central position between sensory perception and divine revelation. In 
the medieval Neoplatonic conception also, man is at the midpoint of creation 
between earth and heaven, and because, like Christ, he partakes of both, he is 
uniquely capable of mediating between them. He can rise from one kind of love 
to another just as knowledge rises from the act of perception to the rational 
concept to the mystic Idea, but he must go down first. Thus he may not have to 
choose between roads, after all, but can move forward, first left and then right, in 
a spiral.48 


45 Dante Studies 1:62: Epistle to Can Grande 8.24. Also see Nardi, Dante e la cultura 
medievale 312: “il viaggio dantesco e il rapimento che lo conclude, prima del significato 
allegorico vogliono averne uno letterale o ‘istoriale.’” For a critique of the “literalist” 
interpretation of the poem, however, see Freccero, “Introduction” 183-87. 

46 See Singleton, Dante Studies 1:70, who finds in Dante “no love that is not a gift of 
God, that is not a participation in the love of the Father for the Son and of the Son for the 
Father”; also Psaki, according to whom, in the Commedia “the love between humans is 
both a figure for that between soul and God, and [...] a step towards , a precursor for, or 
agent in it,” and “this love of Dante for Beatrice is salvific, and simultaneously erotic” 
(14). 

47 Compare how in the dream of the Siren the pilgrim’s look colors the wan features of 
the femmina balba, “com? amor vuol” (Purg. 19.15). See also the contrast in Par. 26.62- 
63 between “I’amor torto” and “diritto.” The motif of a choice between a good and a bad 
love was already present in the romance tradition. In the troubadour Uc de Saint Circ’s 
palinodic chanson de congé, “Anc mais non vi temps ni sazo,” for instance, the speaker 
celebrates his dedication to a new lady, “Car sui estorz a mal’ amor” (“for now I have 
escaped from a bad love”); see my Assembling 41-46.

Monarchia, echoing the Neoplatonic and pseudo-Aristotelian Liber de causis, “homo 


Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 43 


If the poem’s underlying design is based on Beatrice’s presence, departure, 
and subsequent return (or even on the pilgrim’s journey away from and back to 
God; or on his three guides; or on the three canticles; or on the ferza rima verse 
pattern), then its general structure is tripartite. The fact that Dante’s thought is 
trinitarian and is reflected in his poem’s structure is news to no one, of course.49 
But what I have been trying to suggest is that the repeated dialectical pattern that 
I have described — the depiction of ethics as a choice between contradictory 
alternatives — is ultimately resolved synthetically so that dualism is overcome, 
binary oppositions become progressive continuities, and the twos become 
threes.>° It happens again and again in the course of Dante’s corpus. In his “rime 
estravaganti,” this shift can be seen in the passage from the sonnet “Due donne in 
cima de la mente mia,” which opposes “Bellezza e Virtu,” to the great canzone 
of exile “Tre donne intorno al cor mi son venute,” in which beauty is identified 
as an essential property of the moral virtue of justice (Martinez, ‘““Nasce il Nilo” 
120). The opposition in Convivio 4.17.9-12 between Martha and Mary as 
symbols of the active and the contemplative life is resolved shortly thereafter by 
Dante’s adaptation from the gospel of Mark (16.1f.) of the three Maries, who go 


solus in entibus tenet medium corruptibilium et incorruptibilium; propter quod recte a 
phylosophis assimilatur orizonti, qui est medium duorum emisperiorum” (“man alone 
among created beings is the link between corruptible and incorruptible things; and thus 
he is rightly compared by philosophers to the horizon, which ts the link between the two 
hemispheres”; 3.16.3). For the pilgrim’s spiral movement in the Commedia as an 
adequate spatial representation of his journey toward the final reconciliation of his will 
and his intellect, see Freccero, Dante, esp. 70-92. 
49 For a description of the pilgrim’s itinerary in terms of Thomas Aquinas’s “three 
lights” and “three conversions,” see Singleton, Dante Studies 2:15-56; for the 
significance of ferza rima, Freccero, Dante 258-71. 
50 As Raffa indicates (Divine Dialectic 9), this is the movement of dialectical reasoning, 
and is related to Hegel’s teleological conception of history and his notion of “sublation™ 
(Aufhebung), the simultaneous erasure and preservation of contradictions as they are 
“raised up” to a higher and unified truth, as well as to Stephen Dunning’s recent 
description of a dialectic of transformation that “acknowledges strong binary oppositions; 
but [...] refuses to let contradiction have the last word, and so it seeks a union of the 
opposed poles” (Dialectical Readings 7) The eschatological perspective of medieval 
typological exegesis is also dialectical: one historical event is related to another in terms 
of their movement toward reciprocal fulfillment at the end of time (Charity, esp. 199). 
Such dialectical principles were well-known and widely used in the twelfth and thirteenth 
centuries: Thomas Aquinas’ pattern for resolving a guaestio (“videtur quod ... sed contra 
. respondeo dicendum’”) is dialectical in nature, for instance, as was his entire project of 
reconciling Christianity with Aristotelian philosophy. According to Garin, the term 
dialectica, which was earlier interchanged indifferently with /ogica, was being restricted 
in the course of the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries to refer to the method and 
technique of disputation (ars opponendi et risponendi) involving two opposing positions 
that are both possible. 


44 Olivia Holmes 


to the sepulcher to anoint Jesus but do not find him, as symbols of “le tre sette de 
la vita attiva, cioé li Epicurei, li Stoici e li Peripatetici,” none of whom can find 
blessedness in the present world, but need the help of Christian revelation, 
figured by the gospel’s angel in white garments (4.22.13-15).>! 

In the Vita nuova, Beatrice appears to Dante in her eighteenth year “in 
mezzo a due gentili donne, le quali erano di pil lunga etade” (3.1), and this 
pattern is repeated when he sees her again in a place of worship, likely a church, 
as if positioned between the “gentile donna di molto piacevole aspetto” who will 
become his “schermo de la veritade” and the “regina de la gloria” (5.1-3). The 
trinitarian theme is developed in the course of this work to such an extent that 
upon her death Beatrice is said to be (“per similitudine”) the number nine: “che 
tre via tre fa nove”(19.6). 52 Beatrice’s role as mediator between Dante and 
Mary, who is in her turn a mediator between humanity and God, is picked up and 
greatly expanded in the Paradiso. As Francesco Mazzoni puts it, ‘l’apoteosi 
trinitaria di Beatrice dal libello si espande fino alla struttra trinitaria dell’ intiera 
Commedia” (“Il ‘trascendentale’ dimenticato” 115). Beatrice is also seen from 
its beginning in a hierarchy of “tre donne benedette”: in /nferno 2, Virgil relates 
how a “donna [...] gentil” in Heaven, almost certainly Mary (Galanti 30-32 and 
passim), sent Lucy as messenger to Beatrice, seated beside Rachel, to encourage 
her to save her lover from the death that was assailing him. When toward the end 
of Paradiso we see Beatrice once more in her rightful place in the Celestial 
Rose, she is still seated next to Rachel in the third row down, below Eve, who is 
seated below Mary (Par. 32 4-9). The sequence “Beatrice > Eve > Mary” 
implicitly recapitulates the pattern of “presence > absence > return” or “same > 
different > same” that I have suggested. This scheme recalls Plato’s description 
in Timaeus (34-37), picked up by medieval Neoplatonics, of the cosmos’s 
creation in terms of the Same and the Different that combines with and returns to 
the Same.>3 


5! Earlier in the Convivio it is said that through the three Christian virtues, faith, hope, 
and charity, “si sale a filosofare a quelle Atene celestiali, dove li Stoici e Peripatetici e 
Epicuril, per la I[ucJe de la veritade etterna, in uno volere concordevolemente 
concorrono” (3.14.15). 

52 On the generally tripartite movement of the |ita nuova through three major canzoni 
defining three subject matters or stages in love, see Singleton, Essay 78-109; but for both 
binary and ternary elements in Dante’s /ibe//o, Gorni, Lettera 98-107 and Raffa, “Love’s 
Duplicity” 15-26. 

53 Also see Plato’s recognition of the need for a third mediatory element in par. 31: “[...] 
it is not possible to combine two things properly without a third to act as a bond to hold 
them together.” Also Macrobius 1.6. On the Neoplatonic cosmic pattern of progression 
and return to God, and in relation to Dante, see Durling and Martinez, Time, esp. 6-18; 
53-70. For a model of narrative transformation whereby the “récit” (plot) “se constitue 
dans la tension de deux catégories formelles, la différence et la ressemblance” see 
Todorov 240. 


Dante’s Two Beloveds: Ethics as Erotic Choice 45 


It also recalls the Christian structuring of history. Mankind was originally 
created, male and female, in the image of God; indeed all created things 
resemble God. This is the first lesson that Beatrice imparts to her disciple Dante 
in Paradise: “Le cose tutte quante / hanno ordine tra loro, e questo é forma / che 
l’universo a Dio fa simigliante” (Par. 1.103-5).54 But the divine image in human 
beings was annihilated by Eve, who, in committing the first sin and inducing 
Adam to follow suit, introduced difference and division into their relationship 
with God. As Beatrice explains in Paradiso: “Solo il peccato € quel che la [i.e., 
l’umana creatura] disfranca / e falla dissimile al sommo bene” (7.79-80). Yet 
humanity regains its likeness to the Highest Good by way of the Virgin Birth in 
Christian doctrine. The poet imagines the recovery of this likeness to God, and 
the return from the Many to the One, in the final passage of the poem, in which 
the pilgrim sees the second Person of the Trinity “pinta de la nostra effige” (Par. 
33.131), and the dual nature of Christ, human and divine, is resolved in the 
vision of a God who is miraculously both “trino e uno” (Par. 15.47). The ethical 
dualism described earlier in this article is thus overcome. The protagonist 
discovers that what is decisive is not that he choose the right, but that he choose 
rightly, and that by opting for the sacred rather than the profane — Beatrice’s 
virtue, say, rather than her human beauty — he earns both; he has his cake and 
eats it too. But the final vision rewards not only the character Dante with an 
unmediated apprehension of the true nature of God; it offers the reader as well a 
signpost indicating the way to Heaven in Dante’s path.>>

Brenda Deen Schildgen 





Dante’s Utopian Political Vision, the Roman Empire, 
and the Salvation of Pagans 


“[...] velut in ultimum finem, omnia nostra opera ordinantur, quia est pax 
universalis [...].” 
(Monarchia |. lv.5)! 


“Ethics,” writes Giorgio Agamben, “begins only when the good is revealed to 
consist in nothing other than a grasping of evil [...] truth is revealed only by 
giving a place to non-truth — that is, as a taking-place of the false, as an 
exposure of its own innermost impropriety” (Coming Community IV). The 
premise that truth cannot be understood except by showing the false drives both 
the ethical and poetic rhythms in the Commedia. Starting with the false, the 
poem consistently exposes its contrasting truth. However, because of the 
apparently moral standing of the virtuous pagans, one of the more perplexing 
and elusive aspects of this dichotomy between false and true is the ethical 
conundrum Dante poses about the judgment that condemns the pagans to Limbo. 

This essay argues that Dante’s utopian politics based on Orosius’s 
historiographical legacy is precisely what directs him to exile his Latin poets — 
Virgil, Ovid, Horace, and Lucan — to Limbo. Their appearance in the poem in 
contrast to those pagans Dante selects for salvation suggests that Dante believed 
his ancient poets lacked the vision to see the Empire as the instrument of 
providential history. 

In Inferno 4, Dante the author exalts himself (“m’essalto” 120)* in the 
company of the ancient poets, even while he seems to be arguing that despite 
their literary gifts, the poets deserved Limbo, the “verde smalto” (118). Dante 
returns to the issue of the virtuous excluded from salvation throughout the poem, 
but Paradiso 19 and 20 particularly address the problem. Indeed, scholars have 
argued that Par. 19: 70-78, where Dante raises the issue of the salvation of non- 
Christians, perhaps suggests Dante’s doubts about the condemnation of the 
virtuous pagans in Limbo (Padoan 120-22; Foster; Sanguineti 235-54; Casella, 
“Figurazione’”’). Of course, Dante does select certain figures from the ancient 


“[...] all our human actions are directed as to their final end. That means is universal 


eace.” 
Throughout this essay, I refer to the Giorgio Petrocchi critical edition of The Divine 


Comedy used by Singleton. 
Annali d'Italianistica 19 (2001) 


52 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


world as redeemed pagans — Statius, (Purg. 21 and 22), Cato (Purg. 1), Trajan 
(Par. 20: 44), and Ripheus (Par. 20: 68). Therefore, his decision to exile the 
ancient poets emphasizes that Dante chooses to distinguish pagans he deemed 
worthy of salvation from those he assigned to Limbo. Also, the tantalizing void 
created by the absence of Livy and “| buono Augusto” (/nf. 1: 71), and Virgil’s 
strong presence in /nferno and Purgatorio (along with the great poets of his 
century, Ovid, Horace, and Lucan) emphasizes Dante’s distinction between his 
condemned Latin poets and other pagans he chose to save. The ethics of 
salvation is thus revealed by one “non-truth” giving way to an absolute truth, but 
the latter cannot be seen without also understanding and knowing the other, as 
Agamben argues. 

Taking into consideration Dante’s commitment to the Roman imperial 
model of government, the cultural legacy of Virgil, and the status of the salvation 
of pagans as an intellectual and theological issue from the twelfth century 
onwards, this essay examines the ethical criteria Dante adopts for the salvation 
of pagans. 


Salvation of Pagans 

The salvation of pagans is not nearly the unorthodox position that many believe 
it to be (Capéran; Sullivan). There were strong arguments from the twelfth 
century on reaching back to Pauline and Augustinian statements that moral Jews 
and pagans who had lived before the coming of Christ had equal access to 
salvation. For if all mankind had the same roots and the same creator God, they 
all had to have access to the same possibility of salvation. This is the argument 
that Augustine made on several occasions: “All together we are members of 
Christ and are his body; and not just we who are in this place only, but 
throughout the world; and not at this time only, but — what shall I say — from 
Abel the just man until the end of time, as long as man begets and is begotten, 
whoever among the just made his passage through this life, whether now, that is, 
not in this place, but in the present life, or in generations to come, all the just are 
this one body of Christ, and individually his members” (Serm. 341:9.11 Col. 
1499-1500). Note here Augustine’s emphasis on the Roman virtue of “justice,” 
which makes mankind members of the body of Christ. Similarly in De Vera 
Religione, he wrote that in his time Christianity was the one true religion 
(XXV.46) but in the Retractationes, pointing out that Christianity did not exist in 
antiquity, he contended that Christ’s salvation had to be open to all mankind 
from the beginning of time because God’s purpose in creating the universe was 
the salvation of humanity (I.xiii.3). Here there is no stipulation about baptism or 
“knowing” Christ intellectually. 

The rediscovery of Aristotle in the twelfth century likewise gave rise to 
persuasive arguments, particularly presented by Peter Abelard, among others, 
that the Greeks — Plato, Aristotle, and Socrates — as well as the great Latin 
writers must have access to heaven under the same rules as the Hebrew 


Dante's Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 53 


patriarchs (/ntrod. ad. Theol. PL 178. I. 25. Col. 1034 C-D; Theol. Christ Il. 
Col. 1179C and Expositio ad. Rom.” | Col. 802 D). But even more important, as 
argued by Hugh of St. Victor (De Sacram. II. pt. VI.vii), Albert the Great 
(Commentarii in tertium libri sententiarum, dist. XXV, B, art. II, Ad. 6), 
Thomas Aquinas (Summa Theologica. Secunda Secundae, Quest. 1; art. VII), 
and Bonaventure (/n tertium librum sententiarum. d. 25, a.1, q.2, ad. 6), for 
those who had not received the sacrament of baptism, a conversion of heart 
might be sufficient, for the providence of God was deemed most merciful 
(Capéran 170-200). Thus there was widespread belief in the possibility of 
salvation, particularly for those who had lived before Christ, or for those who 
had never encountered Christianity but were nonetheless just. Furthermore and 
more importantly in a sense, from the twelfth century on, the salvation of pagans 
was a topic open to theological discussion without any absolute convictions 
regulating the conclusions except that those people who were eligible for 
salvation had to be virtuous. 


Dante’s Political Ideas 
‘“Romanae spatium est urbis et orbis idem” (“The space of Rome’s city is the 
same as the world” Fasti 2.684). Ovid’s proclamation equates the orbis terrarum 
with the Roman Empire and with the city of Rome as its center. This legacy of 
the ancient Roman imperial hegemony shaped Dante’s geo-political imagination 
because for him, the peace of the political world was coterminous with the space 
of the Roman Empire (Mazzotta 1-13). The birth of Christ during the reign of 
Augustus Caesar, first Roman Emperor, was, as for Orosius and in contrast to 
Augustine (Fortin vii-xxvi), too fortuitous for Dante to dismiss as mere 
coincidence. Dante, in fourteenth-century Italy plagued by civil strife, whether 
inter-city or intra-city, looked back to an unproblematic peace he imaginatively 
created out of the memories of the ancient Roman Empire, the model for the time 
of peace he desired for the present. Dante’s desire is a desperate attempt to 
restore Rome as the world’s center and to reinstate Roman civic virtue 
(Hollander and Rossi 74). In his own time, their value had been shattered by 
Italy’s inter-city squabbling, the relocation of the Pope to Avignon, and the 
rejection of Henry VII, whom, in his Letters V, VI, and VII, Dante had 
welcomed to Italy to be crowned emperor-elect (1310-1312). 

Dante’s views on the Roman Empire are, of course, well known to scholars 
of his work.2 Memory of the Roman imperium as a structure of civic power 
formed Dante’s desire for a restored Roman Empire. In this political vision, the 


3 For works on Dante’s idea of Rome, his politics, and the roles of pope and emperor, see 
Nardi, “Il concetto” 215-75 and “Intorno” 151-313; Silverstein 187-218; d’Entréves 311- 
50; Barbi 49-68; Kantorowicz: Davis, Dante and the Idea of Rome and “Dante’s Vision” 
23-41; De Angelis; Limentani 113-37; Mazzoni, “Teoresi” 9-111; Mazzotta 66-106; 
DuBois 28-51, 52-70; Ferrante 3-131; Schnapp 14-35; Sistrunk; Armour; Scott 29-59. 


54 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


virtues with which he characterized the Romans, justice in particular, would rule, 
and the Church would lose all temporal power. But in the Monarchia, the 
Commedia, Convivio, and the political letters, there is no imperial infrastructure, 
no concrete political program; only utopian political visions based on nostalgia 
and desire. Davis sums up Dante’s theory of history and politics: “It rests on 
memory and desire, memory of an alleged golden age under Augustus, a 
universal peace that Dante believed existed only once in human history, and 
desire for a savior, evidently a new Augustus, who would restore this unique and 
vanished order to the modern world” (“Dante and the Empire” 73). 

To eliminate wars and their cause, as Dante proposed in the Convivio, it is 
necessary that “tutta la Terra, e quanto all’umana generazione a possedere é 
dato, esser Monarcia, cioé uno solo Principato e uno Principe avere, il quale, 
tutto possedendo e pit desiderare non possendo, li re tenga contenti nelli termini 
delli regni, sicché pace intra loro sia, nella quale si posino le cittadi, e in questa 
posa le vicinanze s’amino, in questo amore le case prendano ogni loro bisogno, 
il quale preso, l’uomo viva felicemente; ch’é quello per che l’uomo é nato” (4. 4. 
4). Dante’s political vision incorporates the whole earth under a single rule and a 
single ruler! This grand poetic project, like his later prophetic utterances in the 
Paradiso, expresses his utopian desire for peace in the world. As Justinian says 
in Paradiso 6, “Poi, presso al tempo che tutto ’! ciel vole / redur lo mondo a suo 
modo sereno, / Cesare per voler di Roma il tolle” (55-57). 

Dante probably developed his political ideas under the influence of Remigio 
dei Girolami who was a /eftore at the studium at the Dominican Santa Maria 
Novella in Florence (Davis, “The Florentine Studia” 347-61). Remigio, a 
Florentine born in 1247, was a disciple of Thomas Aquinas in Paris in 1269, and 
died in 1319 in Florence. He gave courses at the studium that lay people could 
attend, and among his many sermons are De bono pacis, De iustitia, and De 
peccato usurae, all of which are themes in Dante’s poetry. Remigio, in contrast 
to Dante, however, developed a philosophy of community closer to Thomistic, 
Augustinian, and Aristotelian social-political thought, which emphasized the idea 
of social collectivism and civitas, both of which would affect a general political 
theory (Davis “Remigio de’ Girolami’; De Matteis; Capitani, “Il De Peccato 
Usure”;). Two other sources of inspiration for Dante’s political ideas, 
particularly in terms of church-state relationships, are John of Salisbury and 
Bernard of Clairvaux. John’s Policraticus discussed at length the respective 
roles of king and emperor (Pézard 163-91); likewise, Bernard’s De 
Consideratione addressed the limits of papal power in the secular domain 
(Botterill 13). Unlike Remigio’s more practical, although visionary politics, 
Dante’s empire, emperor, and pope as conceived in the Monarchia are utopian 
ideals (Capitani, “Spigolatura” 81), for he has constructed the myth of the pax 
romana as a radical critique of his own age of political turmoil and fratricidal 
conflict. The Monarchia is clearly in dialogue with the documents issued by the 
offices of the Emperor, the Pope, the King of France and the King of Naples, but 


Dante’s Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 55 


Dante’s politics nonetheless remain a project of his desires and do not conclude 
in a specific political action (Limentani 133; Davis, “Dante and the Empire’’). 

Whether the poet’s idea of the empire was in support of Henry VII or 
another emperor, hope fuelled his commitment to the possibility of a world 
unified by justice and liberty. This was the point made by Giovanni Gentile in a 
lecture titled “La profezia di Dante,” delivered Feb. 17, 1918 (Studi di Dante 
69). As a literary undertaking, Dante’s political utopia was a mimesis of the 
Roman Empire as ruled ideally by Justinian (Par. 6), according to Law, not the 
military-political project of Constantine or the political-monarchical aspirations 
of the French kings who had initiated the crusades (Schildgen, “Dante and the 
Crusades”). Dante was the first to advocate the idea of a “universal temporal 
community,” which would demand the “collaboration of a completely unified 
human race” (Gilson 165-66). 

Although Convivio (4. 5) and Monarchia (1. 16) recall the historical time of 
peace when Augustus Caesar reigned, by providence contemporaneously with 
the birth of Christ, to prove why Rome should be the center of his utopian plans, 
Dante also turned to the legendary story of Aeneas and the Virgilian text. He 
features Aeneas’s miscegenated family history and Rome’s hybrid culture to 
support his argument for Rome as the heir to universal government. In other 
words, Rome’s and the Romans’ intrinsic superiority, in addition to being 
divinely supported (Convivio 4. 4), he argued, was earned by virtue and by 
hereditary right (Monarchia 2-3) (Silverstein). Rome was uniquely situated to 
take on leadership of the world because its exemplary “nobility” was its “hybrid” 
inheritance. Dante argued that through Aeneas Rome combined all three 
continents (Asia, Europe, and Africa), thus moving the center of the world, in 
Dante’s view, to Italy through hereditary rights and miscegenation. Dante 
rereads history through poeta noster, Virgil, and he adopts the Roman poets’ 
imperium as his literary project, even though some of these poets were clearly 
less optimistic about these universal ideals than Dante. Thus Dante adopts the 
idea of the Roman Empire of his Roman forbears, but his attitude towards it 
differs. While the Roman poets proclaim it, they simultaneously veil their 
proclamations with unresolved tensions about its goals and achievements, 
whereas Dante uses it as his symbolic geo-political utopia that he wishes his own 
world to imitate. 


The Legacy of Virgil 

Critics generally agree that the Roman poets share a common pessimism about 
human behavior, particularly in the political sphere, which called into question 
the goals of the Empire.* This pessimism in his poetic forebears may indeed lie 


4 Those who have taken up these tensions in the Roman poets include Leach 191; 
Johnson, Darkness Visible, whose thesis is the visible darkness in Virgil’s poem; Conte 


56 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


behind Dante’s confinement of “le gente di molto valore” (/nf. 4, 44) to Limbo. 
Virgil himself tells Dante that he and the others are there for no other fault (“non 
per altro rio” /nf. 4, 40) than that they did not adore God properly. Now they live 
in desire without hope, a fitting contrapasso for those who lived without hope 
but with desire in life: 


Es’ e’ furon dinanzi al cristianesmo 
non adorar debitamente a Dio: 
e di questi cotai son io medesmo. 
Per tal difetti, non per altro rio, 
semo perduti, e sol di tanto offesi 
che sanza speme vivemo in disio. 
(Inferno 4, 37-42) 


Because Christian political optimism and hope in Orosian historiography was 
linked to the Roman imperium, this living without hope distances the Roman 
poets from Dante’s idea of a redeemed history. It is true that the added pathos of 
Dante’s parting from his maestro in Purgatorio 27 provides a substantial 
aesthetic reason for assigning Virgil to Limbo. But the tragic, or hopeless, and 
ultimately Stoic view of history that Virgil reveals in his poem might also help 
explain why Dante’s Virgil ends up in Limbo along with all the other Roman 
poets who concomitantly shared Virgil’s misgivings about the Empire. 

Dante deviates from medieval writers who allegorized Virgil and made him 
a hidden Christian. This reading habit was based on Eclogue 4, which seemed to 
prophesy the coming of the savior and also on allegorical readings of the Aeneid 
that heralded a triumphant Christian Rome.> Hollander and others are correct in 





141-84: Segal: Galinsky 210-61; DuBois 28-51: Armstrong 93-116; Quint 21-96; and 
Johnson, Momentary Monsters. 

> The range of readings of Virgil in the long millennium called the Middle Ages is 
expansive. Besides the interpretation that he was a prophet of Christianity, those readings 
also include allegorizations of the Aeneid, as for example. by Fulgentius, the fourth 
century Macrobius, followed later by the twelfth century Bernardus Silvestris, both of 
whom advance neo-platonic readings. Servius’s fourth-century commentary adopted a 
more literal approach, whereas the twelfth-century John of Salisbury’s discussion of the 
Aeneid in Policratici | and Il, as Augustine’s earlier use and reference to Virgil, reveal an 
intense appreciation of his poetry, which could prove philosophically useful. One might 
also include here Dante’s Monarchia, which, while interpreting Virgil’s poem, makes it a 
political allegory for the founding of Rome as part of God’s providential plan. For a 
recent and thorough summary work on these readings, see Courcelle and Pierre and 
Jeanne Courcelle. For readings of Virgil in the Middle Ages, see Comparetti’s still 
outstanding survey: for Servius’s commentary, see Thomas; de Lubac 233-62. Hollander 
surveys the late classical and later traditions for reading Virgil, which included 
literal/historical, allegorical, and moral (Donatus, Servius, Macrobius, Fulgentius, 
Prudentius, Augustine, Jerome, Isidore of Seville. and Bernardus Silvestris) in Jacoff and 


Dante's Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 57 


arguing that Dante’s Virgil is “first and foremost the Virgil of the Aeneid and not 
the Virgil of the commentators” (Allegory 96). Particularly in the Commedia 
where he quotes the Aeneid frequently, the acuity of Dante’s reading habits 
makes it very evident that he has moved beyond the commentaries circulating in 
his times. His reading is not the literal Virgil of Servius, nor the allegorical 
reading of Fulgentius, nor the neo-platonist allegory of Bernardus Silvestris, 
although hints of all of these can be seen in the Commedia.® Dante’s reading of 
Virgil shares with Saint Augustine’s a great appreciation for the lyric power of 
Virgil’s poetry (Hagendahl): 


“Or se’ tu quel Virgilio e quella fonte 
che spandi di parlar si largo fiume?” 


“Tu se’ lo mio maestro e *] mio autore, 
tu se’ solo colui da cu’ io tolsi 
lo bello stilo che m’ha fatto onore” 
(Inf: 1, 79-80, 85-87) 


Dante has in common with Augustine also a similar habit of reading the 
Aeneid as history. In the Monarchia, Dante freely uses Virgil’s story of Aeneas’s 
conquest of Latium to establish the superior nobility of the Roman people and 
their right to rule the world. In the Commedia, he continues his imperial polemic, 
but he sets the story of the founding of Rome against the poet who sang its 
glories. Having Virgil label his own poem a “tragedy” (‘alta mia tragedia” /nf- 
20, 113), Dante presents his ancient friend as blind to the providential history of 
Rome. Virgil is “de li altri poeti onore e lume” (/nf. 1, 82); he is Dante’s guide 
through “la citta dolente”; he is “il lume” (Purg. 22, 68) to whom Statius could 
say, “Per te poeta fui, per te cristiano” (Purg. 22, 73). However, he is confined 
to a permanent “esilio,” rhymed with “Virgilio” (Par. 26, 116, 118), to where, as 
Adam says, Dante’s lady moved him (“mosse tua donna” Par. 26, 118), perhaps 
an ironic use of the word, since Virgil can never move (Barolini, Dante’s Poets 
253; Allan; Barolini, “Q.”). 


Schnapp 96-103. Of Dante’s reading he writes, “the major impulse behind Dante’s sense 
of Virgil springs from his reading Virgil as Virgil ‘read’ himself; treating the literal sense 
as the record of actual events” (103). See also Mazzoni, “Saggio” 29-206; Hollander, 
“Dante’s Misreadings” 79-93; 265-270; Padoan; Iannucci 69-128; DuBois 28-51; 
Barolini, The Undivine Comedy 76-80; see also Hollander, “I! Virgilio dantesco.” For the 
role of tensions between Augustine’s critique of Virgil’s poem and Dante’s undermining 
of Virgil, see Mazzotta, Dante, Poet of the Desert 147-91; Paratore; Pasquazi 45-69; 
Hollander, “The Tragedy” 131-218. 

© Hollander, Allegory 92-103; see also Ronconi, who concludes that “Dante sa che 
scrittori cristiani, da Lattanzio a Prudenzio a Abelardo, intesero l’ecloga come profetica; 
ma il pari di S. Girolamo non crede a un Virgilio ‘cristiano senza cristo’” (570). 


58 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


Dante’s Redeemed Pagans 

The criterion for the salvation of the pagans in Dante’s poem provides an 
important clue to his own theology of salvation for the ancient world. In 
Paradiso 19 and 20, Dante takes up his own concern about whether there is 
salvation outside of Christianity (Jacomuzzi). Canto 20, with the appearance of 
Ripheus, a pre-Christian pagan character (68) from Virgil’s Aeneid (II. 339, 394, 
and 426-27), and Trajan, a post-Christian pagan (44-45), introduces a question 
about salvation outside of Christian time (Foster; Russo; Schildgen, “Dante and 
the Indus’). Likewise, the appearance of Cato, a pagan suicide and supporter of 
the Roman Republic in Purgatorio | and 2, unsettles any convictions that the 
poem conforms to a narrow version of Christianity. The presence of these figures 
on the way to salvation, or already saved souls, disrupts the claims to an 
exclusively Judeo-Christian heaven. When we consider both whom among the 
pagans Dante chose to save and whom he praised while leaving in silence their 
places in the afterlife, we can see an interesting pattern. In fact, Capéran suggests 
that Dante is more restrictive on the salvation of pagans than the theology of his 
own time gave him license to be (206-12). Therefore, we have to look for some 
special reasons for his choices. In Paradiso 20, Dante hints at what 
characteristics single out the saved pagans. Speaking of Trajan, Dante has the 
Eagle say, 


“Ché Puna de lo ’nferno, u’ non si riede 
gia mai a buon voler, torno a | ossa; 
e cid di viva spene fu mercede: 
di viva spene, che mise la possa 
ne’ prieghi fatti a Dio per suscitarla, 
si che potesse sua voglia esser mossa.” 
(Par. 20, 106-111) 


The other, Ripheus, “tutto suo amor la gill pose a drittura” (20, 121). Thus, 
the first, Dante doubly emphasizes, possessed living hope, whereas the second, 
also given over to righteousness, possessed hope and love. What made these 
figures unique was their openness and commitment to hope and to love. His 
emphasis on love and hope (rather than the Roman cardinal virtues alone) points 
to the criteria Dante has elected to decide which pagans to save and which to 
leave in Limbo; their selection reflects this dispensation. 

Dante sends Statius, who stands in poetic opposition to Virgil, who is not 
saved, to heaven. Like Ripheus, Cato, and Trajan, Statius was deeply committed 
to the good of the state, and, with Domitian’s patronage, he was a strong 


Dante's Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 59 


supporter of the Empire.’ While claiming that Virgil’s poetry made him both a 
poet and a Christian, Statius nonetheless is very different from Virgil. But 
although his poetry, like Virgil’s, condemns Greek violence and treachery, in 
focusing on Greek failures Statius’s poetry lacks Virgil’s pessimistic concern 
with the generalized human incapacity to control impulses, an emotional 
handicap that Virgil places at the heart of history’s blindness in the Aeneid. 
Virgil’s position follows from the Stoic legacy that informs the poetry of all his 
Roman poetic companions in Limbo. 

Although Stoicism in particular experienced a rebirth in the Renaissance, 
and Seneca’s importance in the later period is well established (Pena; Daraki; 
Colish; Verbeke 1-19), it has not been generally recognized how widespread 
both Stoic and Epicurean ideas were in the Middle Ages. Cicero and Seneca 
were the two most important sources of Stoic ideas throughout the Middle Ages, 
but Ovid, Lucan, Horace, and Virgil all show interest in one or the other 
philosophy. Virgil’s hint about the transmigration of souls in Book VI of the 
Aeneid (724-51) links him with Platonism and Stoic ethics, and his sense of 
ineluctable and destructive forces, whether of human passions or of divine 
origin, likewise links him with Stoicism. In the tradition of the Stoic critique of 
Roman and Macedonian imperial aggrandizement, Lucan’s Book 10 of the 
Pharsalia reflects this Stoic conviction. The Pythagorean discourse of Book 15 
in the Metamorphoses exposes Epicureanism as Ovid contrasts Pythagorean 
philosophy’s Stoic ethic to Epicurean pleasure (15, 59-478). Padoan had argued 
that Dante used Fulgentius to develop an allegorical reading of Statius’s 
Thebaid, whereby Theseus as hero became a figure for Christ. More convincing 
is Barolini’s argument that Statius identifies himself with providential history 
because he says, echoing Virgil’s “buon Augusto” (/nf. 1, 71), that he was born 
under “| buon Tito” (Purg. 21, 82; on this issue see: Barolini, Dante’s Poets 
263; Di Scipio 85-86). Dante identifies Titus as one of the critical emperors in 
Justinian’s providential history of Rome (Par. 6, 92). Thus one dimension of 
Dante’s decision to save Statius is not merely his support of the Empire, but 
recognition that he represents an optimistic view of history, which saw the 
founding of Rome and the pacification of the Mediterranean basin by Romans as 
central moments in salvation history. 

Ripheus — ‘“Cadit et Ripheus, iustissimus unus / qui fuit in Teucris et 
servantissimus aequi” (“Ripheus, too, falls, foremost in justice among the 


7 This fact was known in the Middle Ages because all medieval Statius manuscripts 
identified him with the reign of Domitian. For discussions of Statius and his Christianity, 
see Brugnoli, “Stazio in Dante” who argued that Statius’s Christianity was an invention 
of Dante’s. But in a more recent article he argues that, though he believes that Dante had 
special reasons for making Statius Christian, it was not his invention (“Lo Stazio”). See 
also Barolini, Dante's Poets 256-69; Heilbron; Padoan argues that for Dante, Statius’s 
hero Theseus was a type of Christ figure (134-36). 


60 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


Trojans, and most zealous for the right” Aeneid Il, 426-27) — is among the just 
rulers in Paradiso (20, 68). As a Trojan noted, according to Virgil, for justice, 
Ripheus belongs to a select group of moral pre-Christian pagans whose people 
would participate in the providential founding of Rome. On the other hand, 
Dante recalls the emperor Trajan (b. 53, d. 117; reigned 98-117) who lived after 
Christ’s birth as the emperor “il cui valore / mosse Gregorio e la sua gran 
vittoria” (“whose worth moved Gregory to his great victory” Purg. 10, 74-75). 
Trajan’s story was actually a celebrated exemplum of the possibility of the 
salvation of pagans in the period, because believing that Trajan could be saved, 
even after death, left open the possibility that any model pagan could also be 
saved (Capéran 167-68). A medieval tradition recorded in Vincent of Beauvais’s 
Speculum Historiale (X1. 46; XXIII. 22) and told in the Vita S. Gregorii Magni 
of Paulus Diaconus, held that Pope Gregory the Great (590-604), seeing the 
famous story of a widow’s appeal on Trajan’s column, was so “moved” that he 
prayed that Trajan be released from hell (Vickers). John of Salisbury’s 
Policraticus refers to Trajan as “Gloriam tamen militarem moderatione 
superavit, Romae et per provincias omnibus se aequalem exhibens” (“By 
moderation he has risen above military glory both at Rome and throughout the 
provinces, showing himself fair to all” Policraticus 1.V.8). Thomas Aquinas 
praises him in the same way (Swmma III, suppl.71.5). Tradition held that Trajan 
had responded with generosity and a sense of justice to the woman whose son 
had been killed. Dante writes, “Or ti conforta; ch’ei convene / ch!’ solva il mio 
dovere anzi ch’i’ mova:/ giustizia vuole e pieta mi ritene” (Purg. 10, 91-93). 
Dante’s version of Trajan suggests the influence of John of Salisbury’s 
Policraticus where he receives an encomium for his ideal leadership which 
concludes, ‘“Felicior Augusto, melior sis Traiano” (“Happier than Augustus, 
better than Trajan”) Dante characterizes Trajan with essential Roman virtues, 
“giustizia” and “pieta,’ both celebrated in Virgil’s Aeneid as ideals, but 


8 “Gloriam tamen militarem moderatione superavit, Romae tamen et per provincias 
omnibus se aequalem exhibens, amicos salutandi causa frequentans vel egrotantes vel 
festis diebus, cum eisdem indiscreta vicissim habens convivia, vehiculis eorum et 
vestibus indifferenter utens, publice et privatim ditans omnes, immunitates civitatibus 
largiens, relaxans tributa provinciis, nulli gravis, carus omnibus, adeo et usque ad nosram 
etatem in senatu non aliter principibus acclametur: Felicior Augusto, melior sis Traiano!” 
“Quare Traianus videatur omnibus praeferendus” (By moderation he has risen above 
military glory, both in Rome and throughout the provinces showing himself the equal of 
all. For the sake of greeting friends he spent time with the sick, and on festive days he, 
more than once, gave them all a place at the dinner table. He would ride in their carts and 
dress like them. Publicly and privately he would enrich all, bestowing exemptions on 
citizens, reducing taxes in the provinces. He was heavy-handed with none, dear to all. So 
that even in our day in the Senate, leaders are acclaimed in no other way than, “You are 
happier than Augustus, better than Trajan’” Policraticus 1. 315). 


Dante's Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 61 


transformed and translated into Dante’s own ideological idiom.? Like Ripheus, 
Trajan finds himself among the just rulers in Dante’s heaven (Par. 20, 44-45). 

But there is another feature to Trajan that cannot be casually overlooked. 
Not only was the story of Trajan’s generosity to the widow widespread, but so 
too was the conviction that his sins had been remitted as a consequence. Thus 
Thomas Aquinas wrote “quod Traianus precibus beati Gregorii ad vitam fuerit 
revocatus et ita gratiam consecutus sit, per quam remissionem peccatorum habuit 
et per consequens immunitatem a poena” (“that by the prayers of blessed 
Gregory Trajan would be called back to life and thus attain the grace through 
which he had the forgiveness of sin and consequently freedom from punishment” 
Summa Ill, supp. 71, 5). Repeating John of Salisbury’s “Felicior Augusto, melior 
sis Traiano” (Comentum II] 285), Benvenuto da Imola’s gloss points to the 
virtues “umilta” and “giustizia” in Trajan: “certe maxima humiliatio fuit quod 
altissimus princeps ita inclinaret imperatoriam maiestatem ad audiendam 
mulierculam plorantem, sub superbis signis, in campo martio superbo, inter 
equites superbos” (‘certainly greater was that humiliation whereby the highest 
emperor so bent down his imperial majesty to hear the simple woman begging 
beneath the exalted emblems, in the exalted martian field, among his exalted 
knights” Comentum III 288-89). 

Even though Dante clearly associated Trajan with this story of the widow, 
which he uses as a circumlocution for talking about him without naming him, 
“colui che pit’ al becco mi s’accosta,/ la vedovella consolo del figlio” (Par. 20, 
44-45), Trajan was equally famous for restoring the Empire from decadence, a 
history celebrated on Trajan’s Column in Rome and recorded in Orosius and 
repeated by John of Salisbury in Policraticus. The column had survived, 
tradition held, because Gregory had seen the story of the widow carved into it, 
which led to his concern for Trajan. Orosius writes that Trajan was selected 
when Nerva recognized that the state was decadent and almost in ruins, “qua 
quondam legerat Nerva Hispanum virum Traianum, per quem respublica 
reparata est [...]” (“with which Nerva had chosen the Spaniard Trajan, through 
whom the state was restored” VII.34, 2). Thus Trajan is a figure who represents 
the idea of rulership based on worthiness, rather than on inheritance, a position 
Dante also had argued in Convivio, for which he gives the example of Hugh 
Capet, “figliuol d’un beccaio di Parigi” (Purg. 20, 52), and which Cacciaguida 
also supports in Par. 16. Furthermore, Orosius emphasizes Trajan’s role in 
pacifying the Roman territories, for under Trajan the Empire reached its largest 
expansion, including not just the Mediterranean basin but Gaul, Britannia, 
Germania, the Euphrates and Tigris river basins, Seleucia, and Babylonia. 
Orosius conferred divine intervention on Trajan’s reign because Nerva’s 





9 See Garrison for a history and analysis of the use of pietas and iustitia from the ancient 
Romans to Dryden; Ball; Silverstein for the importance of pietas in the De Monarchia 
(192-94); Hollander and Rossi also discuss Roman republican virtues (69-75). 


62 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


selection of Trajan as his adoptive son and heir was “per quem revera adflictae 
reipublicae divina provisione consuluit” (“through whom by divine foresight the 
good of the state so severely wounded was restored” VII, 11,1). Thus Trajan’s 
story combines four central issues for Dante: divine and providential interest in 
history which led to the pacification and expansion of the Empire; rule based on 
worth rather than blood; and commitment to the people of the Empire exercised 
with the Roman civic virtues of humility and justice. 

Dante has no precedent for placing Cato (95-46 BC), a supporter of the 
Roman Republic, in Purgatory. Silverstein drew attention to how Dante deviated 
from standard views on Cato in the Middle Ages. He wrote, “Dante’s estimation 
of the suicide of Utica thus appears in opposition to that of his Christian sources, 
preferring the eulogies of Cicero, Seneca, and the other ancient authorities to the 
dispraise and silence of Augustine and Tolomeo and untouched by the cool 
reserve of John of Salisbury” (208; Hollander and Rossi 63-64). But Cato, the 
lawgiver, like Ripheus and Trajan, is famous for his commitment to liberty, a 
Roman virtue central to Dante’s utopian political vision.!° In the Monarchia, 
Dante singles out those 


qui pro salute publica devotas animas posuerunt, ut Livius, non quantum est dignum, sed 
quantum potest, glorificando renarrat; accedit et illud inenarrabile sacrifitium severissimi 
libertatis tutoris Marci Catonis. Quorum alteri pro salute patrie mortis tenebras non 
horruerunt; alter, ut mundo libertatis amores accenderet, quanti libertas esset ostendit, 
dum e vita liber decedere maluit quam sine libertate manere in illa. 

(Monarchia 2.5: 15) 
(who laid down their lives dedicated to the salvation of the community, as Livy relates to 
their glory, not in terms worthy of them but as best he can; and that sacrifice [words 
cannot express it] of the most stern guardian of liberty, Marcus Cato. The former for the 
deliverance of their fatherland did not recoil from the shadows of death; the latter, in 
order to set the world afire with love and freedom, showed the value of freedom when he 
preferred to die a free man rather than remain alive without freedom.) 


Thomas Aquinas (Summa 5, Il, II, q.64; II, I, q. 125 a.2; III, q. 47, a. 6) and 
Augustine (Cité de Dieu, 1, XVII-XXIII, XXVII) both recalled Cato as a figure 
who exemplified the value of liberty, and John of Salisbury in the Policraticus 
remarked that he was a venerable image of virtue (I, 197). As the first figure 
Dante sees on entering Purgatory, he represents the absolute justice of God, for 
Whom distinctions between suicide and self-sacrifice, political liberty and 


10 Mazzotta remains by far the best discussion of Cato in the Comedy (14-65). A brief 
list of critical studies of Cato in the Comedy includes Pasquazi (A//'eterno 175-94), who 
gives an overview of the critical discussion concluding that Cato represents “il valore 
della liberta” (179). Sanguineti argues that Cato represents a fusion of the Christian idea 
of the freedom of the will and an ideal free political regime (125-47). See also Pézard, 
“Le Chant” 175-90; Renucci 296-311; Auerbach 11-76; Kaske 1-18. 


Dante’s Political Values, the Roman Empire, and the Salvation of Pagans 63 


absolute liberty are more subtle than the human intellect can easily comprehend. 
Thus, Dante presents Cato’s suicide as a heroic act, because it was not out of 
selfishness but out of selfless love of liberty for his country. Cato does not 
appear at the beginning of Purgatorio as a type of the future-saved pagans, as 
Auerbach argued in “Figura” (11-76). In contrast to Brutus and Cassius, who 
chose civil war when faced with what they perceived as tyranny and suicide 
when their efforts failed, Cato chose suicide rather than the betrayal of liberty. 
As Dante put it, quoting Cicero: “[...] and he had always persisted in any resolve 
or plan he had undertaken, it was fitting that he should die rather than set eyes on 
the face of the tyrant” (‘[...] semperque in proposito susceptoque consilio 
permansisset, moriendum ei potius quam tyrampni vultus aspiciendus fuit” 
Monarchia, 2.5: 17). 

Such a sacrifice — “‘illud inenarrabile sacrifitium severissimi libertatis 
tutoris Marci Catonis” (Mon. 2:5:15) — makes Cato the typological opposite to 
Brutus and Cassius, the betrayers of liberty and the creators of universal schism, 
whom Dante has just seen in the mouths of Satan (/nf. 34). In an act of 
transcendent hope, “To set the world afire with love of freedom” (Mon. 2.5: 15), 
Cato had chosen death at his own hands. As the guardian of the gates of 
Purgatory and as lawgiver, Cato exemplifies hope spurred by Roman civic 
virtue, the foundation of a new world based on law and liberty, limits and 
freedom, although they are interdependent and ruled by the human will. In the 
Pharsalia, Lucan presents Cato as willing to sacrifice his own life if his death 
could save his people from civil war (2:312). Dante’s act of saving Cato shows 
Roman history can be made consistent with providential history (Mazzotta 21, 
59), for like Dante’s Trajan and Ripheus, Cato too believed in justice and liberty, 
virtues central to romanitas. Thus, rather than ultimately undermining Empire, 
Cato’s suicide is providential because Roman political ideals and the good of the 
state inspired it. 

In saving some pagans, Dante chooses those figures that supported the 
values that he has assigned to Rome as Republic and as Empire. He connects the 
ideal goals and achievements of the Empire with its model citizens: Ripheus, as 
an exemplary just Trojan, a type of the future Roman; Cato, as one who 
condemns the curse of civil war and symbolizes the Roman civic virtue of 
“liberty”; Statius, supporter of the Empire; and Trajan, exemplary restorer of the 
Empire’s boundaries and model of justice and piety. Although not a strong 
argument, because it argues from silence, one cannot help wondering, given 
Dante’s apparent criteria for selecting pagans for salvation, whether indeed he 
did leave open a space for Augustus Caesar and for Livy, “che non erra” (/nf. 28, 
12). Both, after all, practiced Roman civic virtue and supported the stability of 
the imperium, which brought an end to civil war and ushered in an age of peace. 
In contrast, Virgil, Lucan, Ovid, Seneca, Cicero, and Horace shared the same 
profound doubts about the providentiality of the Empire, for as Virgil says of 
himself and his co-residents in Limbo, “non adorar debitamente a Dio: / e di 


64 Brenda Deen Schildgen 


questi cotai son io medesmo” (/nf. 4, 38-39). In their case, baptism or being born 
before Christ (/nf. 4, 35-37) may not indeed be their central deficiency. Rather, 
not only in the present do “sanza speme vivemo in disio” (/nf. 4, 42), but indeed, 
this pessimism characterized how they had actually lived. Dante is seized by 
sadness when he hears of Virgil’s and the other ancient luminaries’ mutual fate, 
for their suspension — “pero che gente di molto valore / conobbi che ’n quel 
limbo eran sospesi” (/nf. 4, 44-45) — is, according to Dante’s view, like the 
hopelessness of their vision of human history. 

Dante took the Roman Empire as represented by the Roman poets and 
transformed it into his political ideal, but in his attempt to see divine providence 
as history’s director, he condemned his poets’ pessimism and replaced it with his 
own visionary geo-political theory. He confined his great lights, of whom he 
made himself a joyful associate, to Limbo because of their blindness, but 
specifically he singled out other Romans for salvation whom he made co- 
partners in his own vision. Dante’s utopian political vision put faith in a history 
guided by divine providence, whereas in reading his great literary forebears, he 
recognized that they did not share this hope. 

Returning to the ethical theme undertaken in this discussion, it is in Dante’s 
contrast between saved pagans and those he assigns to Limbo that he clarifies the 
criteria for his poetic choices. As Agamben, rephrasing Aquinas wrote, “the 
inhabitants of Limbo do not receive an afflictive punishment, like that of hell, 
but only a punishment of privation that consists in the perpetual lack of vision of 
God” (11). The ethical principle operating here is that salvation is both the 
experience of and the reward for those whose just actions are inspired by hope.
