122 Norma Bouchard 


the contrary, must be seen as an additional mode of remembrance. As Consolo is 
often fond of arguing, at a time when the media, in their attempt to secure an 
ever-increasing base of mass consensus have developed a standardized Italian — 
“una super-koiné, una sopra-lingua” (27) — the recovery of the disappearing 
languages and cultures of Italy becomes imperative. Hence, as in a museum of 
ethnographic memory, Consolo’s plurilinguism subverts the homogenizing 
effects of referential language by juxtaposing a variety of fictional and official 
texts written in the Italian literary idiom with fragments of Southern dialects in 
their Greek, Arabic, French, and Spanish ascendance. 

The overt absence of ethical “truths,” of totalizing norms and criteria for a 
just behavior suggested by the interview of Fuga dall’Etna, is certainly reflected 
in Consolo’s narrative practice in // sorriso dell’ignoto marinaio. Published in 
1976, // sorriso inscribes a story and a discourse profoundly traversed and 
irreducibly shaped by the debunking of the conceptual framework of modernity 
and the ethical thought it has produced. 

Briefly summarized,® the story of // sorriso dell’ignoto marinaio is set 
against the background of the “Risorgimento” in Sicily, from September 12, 
1852, to December 18, 1860. Its nine chapters and numerous appendices narrate 
how the stratified classes of the peninsula responded to the events leading to the 
unification of the peninsula. While Giovanni Interdonato represents the 
committed revolutionary, the man of action who has had first hand experience of 
the barricades of 1848, Galvano Maniforti is a symbol of the ancien régime, 
loyal to the Bourbons and clearly opposed to the liberal cause. Consolo situates 
Barone di Mandralisca between these two extremes. Although not opposed to 
the liberal cause, nor completely oblivious to the harsh Southern social reality, 
Mandralisca is initially only a detached observer of the historical process. In 
fact, he devotes most of his time to cultivating his intellectual and artistic 
pursuits. In the beginning of the novel, for example, as Mandralisca travels from 
Lipari to Cefali' with the portrait of the “Unknown Sailor” by Antonello da 
Messina (1430-?79), he fails to recognize, in a man who bears a striking 
resemblance to the portrait itself, the revolutionary Giovanni Interdonato. More 
significantly still, while the man comments on the “mal di pietra” affecting the 
miners of Lipari, Mandralisca distances Interdonato’s reminders of an unjust 
social reality by engaging in a private evocation of poetic images of nature. 
Later on, when Mandralisca meets Interdonato again, he reacts with great 
circumspection to the latter’s call for help in ousting the Bourbons during the 
revolt of Cefalt’ in 1856. Quite correctly, Cesare Segre has commented that 
Interdonato, although confined to the role of the deuteragonist, is, at this point of 
the narrative, “il vero cervello politico e l'uomo impegnato” (78). However, the 
events following Garibaldi’s landing in Marsala in 1860, bring about a series of 
important changes in Mandralisca’s involvement with history. The middle 


* For a detailed summary of this novel, see Farrell 64-70. 


Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 123 


chapters of Consolo’s novel relate how the Sicilian peasantry interprets 
Garibaldi’s landing as the occasion to avenge century-old wrongdoings by 
reacting with violent uprisings, such as that of Alcara Li Fusi. The 
condemnation of the rioters of Alcara and their subsequent imprisonment in a 
labyrinth built under the castle of Maniforti, shake Mandralisca’s conscience, 
spurning him to put an end to his quiescence. In a letter addressed to 
Interdonato, who is now “Procuratore Generale” in Messina, Mandralisca 
narrates his journey to Alcara after the massacre has taken place. In the chapter 
that follows the letter, Mandralisca recounts his visit to the prison and his 
discovery of the writings by twelve rioters. Transcribed as the ninth chapter of // 
sorriso, these writings provide a version of the uprising from the perspective of 
the revolutionaries themselves. However, the ethical conflict that results from 
the acquisition of the rioters’ version is not resolved. In a reversal of the 
conflict-resolution pattern of modern ethical thought,’ // sorriso closes without 
demanding a just polity nor offering norms and criteria for correct moral 
behavior. At the end of the novel the reader is simply left with three appendices. 
The first one is a polemical article by Luigi Scandurra that questions the 
decision of the court to free the surviving rioters. The second one is the death 
certificate of the bracciante and rioter Giuseppe Sirna. The third, and final one, 
is a proclama of the pro-dictator Mordini. Dated October 15, 1860, it exhorts 
Sicilians to become loyal subjects to the newly formed Italian State and 
announces the upcoming plebiscite. 

The disquieting effect produced by a story that dramatizes — without 
solving — ethical conflicts, is doubled in its narrative discourse. Despite its 
division into nine chapters and several appendices, // sorriso subverts any 
possibility of continuous and linear narration through a consistent juxtaposition 
of non-causal and autonomous episodes. Moreover, by way of a plurality of 
points of view and speech-styles, it brings about the dissolution of the coherent, 
self-identical subject of the enunciation that is typical of traditional historical 
fiction. As a result, reality is dissolved into a kaleidoscope of localized 
interpretations, suggesting that language, from its former status as a shared 
means of communication, has become a shifting space of regressive and 
unstable meaning, a vehicle finally unsuitable for the transmission of absolute 
“truths.” For example, in the first chapter an anonymous “Antefatto,” written in 
a telegraphic style, introduces the motive of the journey that will take 
Mandralisca from Tindari to Cefali on September 12, 1852. The narrative that 
follows, however, juxtaposes traditional passages in indirect and direct discourse 
with fragments of speech originating from the most varied texts. These include 
Sicilian poetry, art catalogs, mystical recitations, and prosaic songs cast in 


° Bauman writes: “Modernity is about conflict-resolution, and about admitting of no 
contradictions except conflicts amenable to, and awaiting resolution” (Postmodern Ethics 
8). 


124 Norma Bouchard 


Southern dialect. This chapter itself concludes with two appendices. The first 
one is a letter dated 1840 and is allegedly written by Mandralisca as a preface 
for a scientific catalog to be published by ‘“stamperia Oretea — Via 
dell’Albergaria num. 240.” The other one is a short note dated 1842. It also 
bears the name of Mandralisca. The fragmentation of enunciation suggested here 
is also at work in subsequent chapters, with the result that readers remain 
uneasily posed between voices coming from different sources and bearing 
different perspectives. Hence, to give just one more example, in the second 
chapter, what appears to have begun as a fairly stable description of the journey 
of Giovanni Interdonato to Cefalu, is abruptly interrupted by the inclusion of 
two appendices. The first one is a written text of a speech given by the historian 
Francesco Guardione on the occasion of the 1856 riots of Cefalu. As in the case 
of most writings of this sort, it is a relatively sober, scholarly account of 
historical events. The second one, however, is a highly idealized literary passage 
from none other than Abba, the “memorialista Garibaldino” and author of 
Noterelle d’ uno dei Mille. 

Considering both the open-ended, inconclusive story and the fragmentary 
and pluralized discourse of // sorriso, are we to assume that Consolo’s novel 
finally comes to rest within a negative theology similar to the one that has 
moored some of the more radical schools of hermeneutics? Are we to assume 
that, in an era that has lost modernity’s faith in totalizing and universal “truths,” 
any claim to responsibility and justice have become logical impossibilities? In 
short, can a novel that is traversed by a postmodern textuality, such as that 
articulated by // sorriso, still express an ethical call? While some thinkers would 
argue that such calls are no longer legitimate and that we should learn to rejoice 
in our belonging to a post-deontic era,'° others have more cautiously sought to 
negotiate a balance between the de-legitimization of the grand-narratives of 
modernity and the danger of an unbounded postmodernist relativism. Among 
this latter group of thinkers, Emmanuel Lévinas has been widely acknowledged 
as a fundamental point of reference to overcome the predicament--some would 
call it the paralysis--between postmodernist skepticism and the resilience of an 
ethical imperative. According to Zygmunt Bauman’s Morality and_ the 
Holocaust, since Levinas’s moral philosophy maintains a humanistic 
commitment to responsibility and justice despite its emergence out of a post- 
rationalist episteme, it can unveil “an ethical demand in the postmodern” (qted 
in Eaglestone, Ethical Criticism 5). Since such demand is relevant to my 
discussion of // sorriso, it is to Lévinas’s work that I will now turn. 

Among the many bold moves made by Lévinas in his 1971 Totality and 
Infinity, is his claim that the ethical moment must be freed from the traditional 
ontological assumptions of modernity. These assumptions require that the 


'° | am referring to Lipovesky, Le Crépuscule du devoir. For additional discussion of 
Lipovesky’s work, see Bauman, Postmodern Ethics 2-3. 





Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 125 


ethical moment be derived from an a-priori, theoretical system of morality and 
judgement assumed to be universally valid and self-evident.'' Hence, in a 
reversal of the modern rationalist episteme, in the pages of Totality and Infinity 
ethics becomes a primordial experience or, in the words of Derrida, “an Ethics 
of Ethics” (Writing and Difference |11).'* Furthermore, this primordial ethical 
moment does not take place at the level of consciousness, but at the level of 
sensibility.’ Hence, it is no longer the product of a process of objectification, 
but depends upon a complete obligation to the Other in all its irreducible alterity. 
This alterity is what Lévinas calls the “face” of a being escaping the rational 
economy of sameness and identity and therefore irreducible to the logocentric 
order of Being: 


The way in which the other presents himself, exceeding the idea of the other in me, we 
here name face. This mode does not consist in figuring as a theme under my gaze, in 
spreading itself forth as a set of qualities forming an image. The face of the Other at each 
moment destroys and overflows the plastic image it leaves me, the idea existing to my 
own measure and to the measure of its ideatum — the adequate idea [...]. To approach the 
Other [...] is to welcome his expression, in which at each instant he overflows the idea a 
thought would carry away from it. It is therefore to receive from the Other beyond the 
capacity of the I, which means exactly: to have the idea of infinity [...]. The relation with 
the Other, or Conversation, is [...] an ethical relation. 

(Totality and Infinity 50-51) 


As this quotation suggests, the articulation of the ethical encounter as a 
primordial experience with the “face” of a mysterious, non-totalizable Other, 
represents an important development for traditional accounts of the constitution” 
of the knowing subject, such as those provided by the noetic-noematic structure 
of transcendental phenomenology. By describing the ethical moment as an 
encounter preceding any rational understanding, Lévinas no longer allows for a 
synthesis of phenomena by a subjectivity constituted a-priori, that is to say, for 
the representational ability of an intentionality. More specifically, by positing 
the other as absolute alterity, as radical and irreducible otherness, Lévinas 
effectively questions the constitution of the knowing subject and of the ego in 
acts of objectification, in moments of totalizing representation that are taken to 
be essential to the activity of consciousness. In Levinas’s words: 


A calling into question of the same [...] is brought about by the other. We name this 


'' Por additional discussion of Lévinas’s reversal of the privilege of ontology for a pre- 
rational ethical obligation, see Ciaramelli. 
'2 For additional discussion of ethics as a primordial experience, see especially Lévinas, 


“Ethics as First Philosophy.” 
'3 For additional discussion of Lévinas’s concept of “sensibility,” see Otherwise than 
Being 14-15, 61-72, and Totality and Infinity 187-93. 


126 Norma Bouchard 


calling into question of my spontaneity by the presence of the Other, ethics. The 
strangeness of the Other, his irreducibility to the I, to my thoughts and my possessions, is 
precisely accomplished as a calling into question of my spontaneity, as ethics. 
Metaphysics, transcendence, the welcoming of the other by the same, of the Other by me, 
is concretely produced as the calling into question of the same by the other, that is, as the 
ethics that accomplishes the critical essence of knowledge. 

(Totality and Infinity 43) 


In his second major work, Otherwise than Being of Beyond Essence, 
Lévinas further develops his meditation on the subject not as a state a priori, but 
as an entity created as a result of subjection to the Other — a subjectivity-as- 
subjection — completely hostage to the Other’s commands and demands, needs 
and wants. And precisely because, as Alphonso Lingis has acutely commented, 
“[...] to acknowledge the imperative force of another is to put oneself in his 
place, not in order to appropriate one’s own objectivity, but in order to answer to 
his need, to supply for his want with one’s own substance” (“Translator’s 
Introduction” xxviii), the ethical exposure to the alterity of the Other is a 
responsibility that requires a complete process of substitution. Nonetheless, 
substitution does remain fundamental to the self. It is only by putting oneself in 
the place of another one, by assuming a total responsibility for the Other, that 
subjectivity can be constituted and inscribed into space. 

In addition to further refining the notions of self and Other, in Otherwise 
than Being or Beyond Essence Lévinas also departs from the ontological 
terminology that he had employed in Totality and Infinity to describe the ethical 
encounter with the “face.” As a result of both self-critique'* and following 
Derrida’s essay “Violence and Metaphysics” (Writing and Difference 79-| 55) ae 
Lévinas submits his previous philosophical speech to a deconstructive turn. The 
result of this operation is “amphibiology,” a language described by Lévinas as 
the product of the braiding of the “saying” with the “said” and that, for clarity’s 
sake, can be usefully reformulated in terms of Derrida’s articulation of the 
interweaving of writing and speech.'® Like Derrida’s logocentric speech, the 
“said” is the language of metaphysics. Its aim is that of making present, of re- 
presenting, of turning diachrony into synchrony. Hence, the “said” designates 


'* Compare the following statement by Lévinas: “7otality and Infinity was my first book. 
I find it very difficult to tell you, in a few words, in what way it is different from what 
I’ve said afterwards. There is the ontological terminology. I spoke of being. I have since 
tried to get away from that language” (The Provocation of Lévinas 171), qted. in 
Critchley 51. 

'S For an excellent reading of “Violence and Metaphysics,” see Eaglestone’s “*Questions 
of Language and the Question of Language’: Derrida on Lévinas and ‘True 
Representation’ 130-36. 

'© See especially the chapter “The End of the Book and the Beginning of Writing,” from 
Derrida’s Of Grammatology 6-18. 


Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 127 


and thematizes, assigns identity and establishes essence, states and asserts: 
“Language qua said can then be conceived as a system of nouns identifying 
entities, and then as a system of signs doubling up the beings, designating 
substances, events and relations by substantives or other parts of speech derived 
from substantives, designating identities--in sum, designating” (Otherwise than 
Being 40). By contrast, the “saying,” for which, in Otherwise than Being, 
Lévinas can obviously not give a definition without the danger of falling into the 
realm of the “said,”'’ is the space where the ethical moment comes to signify. 
As such, it is the site where the identity of the one breaks down when summoned 
by the “face” of the Other. It is also the site of substitution and infinite 
responsibility for the Other, of infinite subjection and proximity. 

It could be argued that, since any appearance of the “saying” would result in 
its betrayal, or better yet, in a thematized, re-presented Being belonging to the 
order of the “said,” the ethical moment is fated to remain outside representation, 
forever confined to silence and oblivion by the totalizing system with which it is 
interwoven.'* Yet, Lévinas indicates that this is not the case. Through an often- 
employed metaphor of a thread whose continuity is interrupted by knots,'” he 
repeatedly suggests that ‘“‘amphibiology” implies two orders of meaning. These 
orders are the order of the “saying,” which becomes readable as the non- 
thematizable, as the ethical supplement that prevents the order of the “said” to 
achieve the totalizing closure of ontological language: 


The correlation of the saying and the said, that is, the subordination of the saying to the 
said, to the linguistic system and to ontology, is the price that manifestation demands. In 
language qua said everything is conveyed before us, be it at the price of a betrayal [...]. 
Language permits us to utter, be it by betrayal, this outside of being, this ex-ception to 
being, as though being’s other were an event of being. (Otherwise than Being 6)°° 


'7 Eaglestone proposes the following description of the “saying”: “1. the break-up of 
identity and the approach to the other. 2. substitution, passivity beyond passivity, the state 
of being a hostage, exposure, proximity. 3. the relation to the other and the responsibility 
imposed by the other” 143. For further discussion, compare also Peperzak. 

'§ Compare for example: “The saying appears only as a betrayal of itself, a ‘saying 
teleologically turned to the kerygma of the said’ [...]. The saying is transformed into 
doxa, to such an extent that it is forgotten [...] in the said. It is impossible to say the 
saying because at the moment of saying it becomes the said, betrayed by the concrete 
language which is the language of ontology. The saying, which is unthematisable, 
impossible to delimit, becomes limited, thematised, said” Eaglestone 147. 

'° Lévinas, Otherwise than Being 25, 105, 165-71. 

*° Compare also the following: “ontological thematization is the necessary condition for 
any consideration of the non-thematizable. One can see nothing without thematization, 
and all talks of an ethic without ontology is blind. Once again, the relation of the Saying 
to the Said is not one of absolute independence, but rather the interdependence of 
irreconciliable orders of discourse,” Critchley, The Ethics of Deconstruction (169). 


128 Norma Bouchard 


Because of the emphasis placed by Lévinas on the discursive structures that 
are constitutive of the “amphibiology” of language, it becomes clear, at this 
point, how his mediation between a humanist concern with the ethical 
imperative and a post-rationalist episteme extends far beyond the field of 
philosophical inquiry to encompass the practice of listening, i.e., the activity of 
reading and of criticism. In other words, Lévinas’s non-ontological ethical 
philosophy provides a model for conceptualizing the ethos of those 
contemporary narratives that do not voice the prescriptive norms of rationality 
typical of the ethical discourse of modernity. In pragmatic terms, this ethical 
philosophy equates with a labor of reading no longer confined to a description of 
the logocentric truth of the “said,” but also answers the summons of the text by 
articulating those ethical moments of interruption belonging to the order of 
“saying.” In this context, the narrative model proposed by Adam Zachary 
Newton in his recent Narrative Ethics is most important.*' From the premise that 
“narrative prose fiction (particularly in texts leading up to and away from 
modernism)” (25) implies a relational system based upon the uniqueness of the 
ethical situation, Newton proposes a Lévinas-inspired triadic model based upon 
“an interactive rather than a legislative order” (13). This model comprises a 
narrational, a representational, and a hermeneutic ethics. “Narrational ethics” 
articulates for Newton a moment of “saying” by activating “the dialogic system 
of exchanges at work among tellers, listeners, and witnesses, and the 
intersubjective responsibilities and claims which follow from acts of storytelling 
(18). “Representational ethics” is associated by Newton to the otherness of 
Lévinas’s “face” and therefore is concerned with the problematic issue of 
recognition, with “an anagnorisis that extends beyond the dynamics of plot to 
the exigent and collaborative unfolding of character, the sea change wrought 
when selves become either narrating or narrated [...]” (25). The last category, 
“hermeneutic ethics,” encompasses both “narrational” and “representational” 
ethics. Since, as Newton claims, “textual interpretation comprises both private 
responsibilities incurred in each singular act of reading and_ public 
responsibilities that follow from discussing and teaching works of fiction” (19), 
“hermeneutic ethics” accounts for both the readers’ interpretation of the text and 
for the social performative of the narrative act. In short, “hermeneutic ethics” 
refers to the displacement of narrated, fictionalized ethics, from the self- 
enclosed space of the aesthetic to that of social reality. 

Turning back to Consolo’s novel from the framework provided by 
Lévinas’s “amphibiology” and Newton’s triadic narratological model, it now 
becomes possible to describe the polyphonic and inconclusive narrative 


*! For additional treatments of the impact of Lévinas’s thought for the theory and practice 
of reading, see also Eaglestone, Ethical Criticism, particularly the section “Conclusion 
Interpretation Continual Interruption” (175-79), Critchley’s “C/6tural Reading II: Wholly 
Otherwise: Lévinas’s Reading of Derrida” 145-87. 


Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 129 


discourse of // sorriso not simply as one more symptom of an epistemological 
malaise, but as an example of how an ethical moment of signification can be 
expressed after the de-legitimizing effects of post-modernity. 

As I have previously indicated, although the nine chapters of // sorriso 
extend from September 12, 1852, to December 18, 1860, neither the treatment of 
temporality, nor that of voice, provide the reader with a stable, linear 
representation. While chronological frameworks are violated by conspicuous 
anachronies, major events are narrated through different perspectives and points 
of view. Yet, it is precisely in the interruptions of a continuous flow of narration 
and in the dialogic patterns of representation that readers can locate an ethical 
moment of “saying.” To tell it in Newton’s terms, it is in the violation of 
classical form that “the intersubjective responsibilities and claims which follow 
from acts of storytelling” (18) can be disclosed. 

From my previous summary, we might recall how, in the first chapter of // 
sorriso, a conspicuous set of anachronies is produced by an analeptic shift from 
the fall of 1852 to the appendices dated 1840 and 1842 respectively. By way of a 
third-person narrative the reader learns that on September 12, 1852 Mandralisca 
travels by boat from Tindari to Cefalu with the portrait of Antonello da Messina. 
In the course of the journey, he not only fails to recognize in the face of an 
anonymous sailor the revolutionary Interdonato but, more significantly, remains 
unaffected by Interdonato’s explanation of the “mal di pietra” affecting the 
Southern miners. Then, on October 27, 1852, the reader meets Mandralisca 
again. This time, the Baron is unveiling his latest piece of art to his circle of 
nobility. As exquisite food is being served, a mob of peasants assembles in front 
of the palace to beg for some remains. Except for the baroness, who eventually 
orders her servant Rosalia to bring a plate of food to the beggars, neither the 
host, nor his guests seem troubled by the occurrence. In fact, the evening 
proceeds with the unveiling of the portrait by Messina before closing abruptly 
with two appendices. Written by Mandralisca himself on the subject of Sicilian 
malacology, these disorienting segments are much more than casual inclusions. 
They reflect back onto the narrative that precedes them, while summoning us 
readers, to become witness to the futility of pursuing a knowledge that has 
become completely autonomous from the historical process. In more Lévinasian 
terms, by revealing Mandralisca’s aesthetic existence, the appendices place 
scholarly life on trial and therefore function as examples of a “saying” that 
disrupts, through the creation of a differential pattern of meaning, not only the 
“said” of the narrative that precedes it, but also the complacency of all 
intellectual pursuits and callings, including those of readers. In this context, 
Mandralisca’s ritual unveiling of his latest acquisition is quite significant, as the 
piercing eyes of the “Sailor” gaze upon all those who have abdicated social 
responsibility, regardless of their spatial location: 


Le piccole, nere pupille scrutavano dagli angoli degli occhi e le labbra appena si 


130 Norma Bouchard 


stendevano in un sorriso. Tutta l’espressione di quel volto era fissata, per sempre, 
nell’increspatura sottile, mobile, fuggevole dell’ironia, veio sublime d’aspro pudore con 
cui gli esseri intelligenti coprono la pieta. Al di qua del lieve sorriso, quel volto sarebbe 
caduto nella distensione pesante della serieta e della cupezza, sull’orlo dell’ astratta 
assenza per dolore, al di 1a, si sarebbe scomposto, deformato nella risata aperta, 
sarcastica, impietosa o nella meccanica liberatrice risata comune a tutti gli uomini. // 
personaggio fissava tutti negli occhi, in qualsiasi parte essi si trovavano, con i suoi occhi 
piccoli e puntuti, sorrideva a ognuno di loro, ironicamente, e ognuno si senti come a 
disagio. 

(20; emphasis added) 


Analogous creations of meaning through the fracturing of the “said” by the 
“saying” are also at work in the second chapter. A long narrative segment titled 
“L’albero delle quattro arance” describes the visit of Interdonato to Mandralisca. 
Dressed at first as a merchant, Interdonato has a conversation with two 
constables, Chinnici and Bajona. Then, he visits Mandralisca and pleads for his 
involvement in the uprising of Cefalu' of 1856. Despite a momentary awareness 
that the historical process is a succession of violent acts — “lotta per la 
pagnotta, guerra bestiale dove il forte prevale e il debole soccombe” (37) — 
Interdonato’s vision of the uprising is that of a peaceful advent of a more 
democratic society: “Ma gia é la vigilia del Grande Mutamento: tutti i cefali si 
disporranno sullo stesso piano e la pagnotta la dividerranno in parti uguali, senza 
ammazzamenti, senza sopraffazioni animalesche. E cefalo come Cefalu. vuol 
dire testa; e testa significa ragione, mente, uomo [...] Vuoi vedere che da questa 
terra? [...] (37). However, as in the case of the first chapter, this chapter also 
closes abruptly with the inclusion of an appendix from the 20" century. Written 
by the historian Francesco Guardione and dated November 25, 1907, the 
appendix functions as an ethical supplement to the speech of Interdonato. It 
provides an account of the failed uprising of Cefall in 1856 and, once again, 
summons reader to witness the pitfalls of the historical division between classes. 
Such division encompasses constables, like Bajona and Chinnici, who joined the 
repression and were instrumental in bringing about the arrest of patriots such as 
Spinuzza, Maggio, and the Botta Brothers. However, the division also extends to 
the class of intellectuals, like Interdonato himself, whose idealism prevented a 
realistic assessment of the motivations that drew peasants into revolutionary 
uprisings. The second and last appendix that closes the second chapter merits 
even further attention. As an excerpt from Abba’s Noterelle d’uno dei Mille, it 
provides a poetic, almost chivalric version of the Marsala landing of May 1860 
and of the arrival of the Garibaldini. In this sense, then, the appendix is close to 
the vision of the uprising of Cefalu as refracted through the voice of Interdonato. 
However, since the excerpt is introduced just before the third chapter, readers 
witness a striking contrast between the patriotic fervor that animates upper class 
intellectuals, like Abba himself, and a Southern reality of abuse and violence, 
madness and brutality. This reality concretizes in the third chapter, titled ‘“Morti 


Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 131 


Sacrata.” Here, amidst the description of a land scorched by drought and 
devastated by famine, a mad hermit who first murders and then rapes a young 
woman, announces an upcoming massacre: ‘“* — Triste presentimento d’amari 
accadimenti mi striscia nel cuore e nel cervello, — declama nel silenzio. — 
Sento odore di sangue ferro fuoco [...] Morte” (67). 

The relational ethics binding not only tellers, such as Interdonato and the 
peasants, but also listeners, including Mandralisca and us, as readers of // 
sorriso, becomes solidified, by the fourth chapter, in the self-discovery of 
Mandralisca. Nonetheless, Mandralisca’s coming to consciousness does not 
result in a return to a modern form of ethical thought and the universalizing 
system of a-priori evaluations upon which this thought is predicated. To reprise 
Newton’s Lévinas-inspired model, the narrative unfolds into a “representational 
ethics” and dramatizes how, in a time of weakened hermeneutics, the ethical 
relation can exist only as a pre-rational submission to the Other. As we shall see, 
this submission not only precedes all judgements and evaluations, but leads 
Mandralisca into a process of complete substitution for the Other. This process 
begins on May 15, 1860, when Mandralisca, on a journey to collect additional 
specimens for his scientific studies, travels to the town of Alcara Li Fusi and 
witnesses the aftermath of the massacre that has taken place during the riot. The 
riot itself, however, is not directly represented in the novel.” Although it is the 
most significant event of // sorriso, the narrative rapidly shifts from a third- 
person account of peasants getting ready to revolt, to the voice of Mandralisca. 
From being the object of a third-person narration, Mandralisca becomes, at this 
point of the novel, a subjectivity that inscribes itself into space by way of three 
first-person writings. These comprise a letter to Interdonato, a description of the 
scene of the massacre, and an account of the journey into the prison-labyrinth 
where twelve rioters have been held. Since such shift in discursive pattern is 
most significant to my assessment of the ethical significance of Consolo’s novel, 
Mandralisca’s writings deserve close attention. 

Mandralisca begins his letter to Interdonato by disclaiming all legislative 
ethical judgements. The object of his writing, he states, is neither a pleading to 
spare nor a justification to condemn the rioters. It is an account of events that he 
is producing on behalf of those who, being speakers of dialect only, have been 
denied a voice in the national language of official recorded memory: 


[...] questa memoria non suoni invito istigativo a far pendere 1 piatti della bilancia della 
Giustizia sacra da una parte o dall’altra, ma sia intesa quale mezzo conoscitivo 
indipendente, obiettivo e franco, di fatti commessi da taluni che hanno la disgrazia di non 


2 While the third and fifth chapters, dated May 13 and May 16 respectively, mention the 
landing in Marsala and even account for the peasants’ reaction to the news of Garibaldi’s 
arrival, the fourth chapter, dated May 15, 1860, is dedicated to the description of 
Mandralisca’s journey to secure specimens for his scientific studies. 


132 Norma Bouchard 


possedere (oltre a tutto il resto) il mezzo del narrare, a voce o con la penna, com’io che 
scrivo, 0 Voi, Interdonato, o gli accusatori 0 contro parte o giudici d’essi imputati 
abbiamo il privilegio. E cos’é stata la Storia sin qui, egregio amico? Una scrittura 
continua di privilegiati. 

(96) 


Then, Mandralisca begins a long meditation on the legitimacy of his words 
as a speaker representing the events of Alcara. He would like to narrate with the 
same immediacy as one of the participants of the riots would have done it: “E 
narrar li vorrei siccome narrati li averia un di quei rivoltosi protagonisti 
moschettati [...]” (96). However, as he is about to produce his account, he 
realizes that even if he were able to write in a most transparent, language — 
“siccome il dagherrotipo fissa di noi le sembianze” (97) — he would fail 
because of a lack of a common ground between his words and those of the 
rioters. This ground, which Mandralisca describes as “la chiave, il cifrario” (97), 
is a shared frame of reference. As such, it extends far beyond the commonality 
of a linguistic code to encompass the entirety of life’s experience, including the 
ethical sphere. This is a sphere that, being marked by the irreducible alterity of 
the Other, invalidates the assumptions of universally valid systems of values and 
criteria for moral behavior: 


[...] oltre la lingua, teniamo noi la chiave, il cifrario dell’essere, del sentire e risentire di 
tutta questa gente? Teniamo per sicuro il nostro codice, del nostro modo d’essere e di 
parlare ch’abbiamo eletto a imperio a tutti quanti [...]. E quindi noi diciamo Liberta, 
Egualita, Democrazia, riempiamo d’esse parole fogli, gazzette, libri, lapidi, pandette [...]. 
E gli altri, che mai hanno raggiunto i diritti pil sacri e elementari, la terra e il pane, la 
salute e amore, la pace, la gioja e l’istruzione, questi dico, e sono la piu parte, perché 
devono intender quelle parole a modo nostro? Ah, tempo verra in cui da_ soli 
conquisteranno quei valori, ed essi allora li chiameranno con parole nuove vere per loro 


[...] vere perché i nomi saranno intieramente riempiti dalle cose. 
(97-98) 


Nonetheless, the demise of modern ethical thought suggested by 
Mandralisca’s realization that universally valid systems of values do not exist, 
does not result in passivity. Since the ethical imperative persists under the 
treacherous surface of language and the ever contingent frames of our 
representations — “Perché, a guardar sotto, sotto la lumaca intendo, c’é la terra 
vera, materiale, eterna” (100) — Mandralisca accepts a condition of total 
responsibility for a group of people that eludes his rational understanding. In 
Lévinasian terms, he submits to a primordial ethical experience with the “face” 
of a mysterious, non-totalizable Other. Further, because this experience precedes 
all acts of objectification, including the formulation of judgements and 
evaluations, Mandralisca declares that he will confine his writing to a 
description of the outcome of the riot of Alcara: “Degli esiti soltanto m’era 


Consolo, Lévinas, and the Ethics of Postmodernist Storytelling 133 


agevole parlare, e J/icito [...]” (103; emphasis added). Titled “Memoria” and 
structured like a Dantesque journey, this description carries the reader to the 
villages where the massacres have occurred. Amidst a landscape of putrefying 
bodies, Mandralisca recalls the imprisonment of the revolutionaries and the final 
speech of a colonel. The speech informs the population that those who have 
been arrested are nothing but monstrous traitors who have betrayed the 
unification of Italy and the sacred names of Garibaldi and Vittorio Emanuele for 
acts of personal revenge. Then, Mandralisca moves on to relate to Interdonato 
his visit to the prison. Built under the palace of the nobleman Maniforti, it is a 
labyrinth shaped as a snail-shell. Meandering through its passageways, 
Mandralisca discovers the writings of the prisoners.” Ephemeral signs, receding 
echoes, these writings exist only as traces of charcoal, as markings of dust. And 
it is at this juncture of the novel that Mandralisca completely effaces his own 
subjectivity to substitute for the Other. Hence, from his disembodied voice, 
twelve narratives emerge. Included as the ninth chapter of // sorriso, they tell 
story of hunger and grief, of violence and revenge, but also of social justice and 
fair distribution of land. By so doing, they counter, through an ethical moment 
of “saying,” the “said” version of the facts of Alcara Li Fusi recorded in official 
discourse. 

Consolo’s novel, however, does not close with the prisoners’ voices, but 
with three archival documents. Functioning as instances of the “said,” the 
documents put an abrupt end to the “representational ethics” of the ninth chapter 
by objectifying alterity to the order of Being and of the same. As we might 
recall, while the first document identifies the rioters as the guilty ones — “i 
ladri, gli assassini, gl’incendiarii” (139) — the second one is the death certificate 
of the rioter Sirna. As the clerk records the manner of his passing--"pena di 
fucilazione” (140)--he also informs the reader of having had to sign in for 
Sirna’s witnesses. Confined outside the symbolic order — “Avendo detto li 
testimoni dichiaranti di non saper scrivere” (140) — the witnesses are again the 
objects of the logocentric system of representation of the “said.” The third and 
last document is a “proclama.” Voiced by Mordini on October 15, 1860, it joins 
the process of objectification of the other previous texts. It announces the 
“plebiscito” of October 21, 1860 and exhorts Sicilians to become loyal subjects 
to the new Italian state: “Ad affrettare il compimento dei vostri destini, io scelsi, 
or son pochi di, una via che altri popoli d'Italia avevano percorsa [...]. E la scelsi 
perché aveva |’approvazione del Dittatore, perché guidava ad un patto solenne di 
conciliazione e di pace [...]. Qui si tratta di fare con la concordia la patria” (141). 
Collectively considered, then, these archival documents replay the historical 
destiny that awaited the Southern masses in the years of Risorgimento and its 


3 | would like to point out that the 1976 edition of the novel reports the twelfth writing in 
Southern dialect. Regrettably, the 1997 edition of the novel includes a translation into 
Italian, thereby reducing the effect of alterity of the text (// sorriso 158-59). 


134 Norma Bouchard 


aftermath. However, it is important to bear in mind that they remain embedded 
in a context where the alternative “truth” of an ethical “saying” has been 
allowed to resonate, where the voice of the Other has been, at least for a 
moment, saved from erasure. Hence, the novel’s open-ended epilogue ultimately 
forces us, as readers, to reflect upon the past and to question the legitimacy of 
our present collective life. Named by Lévinas the third party, or /e tiers,“ 
collective life is a space where the responsibility of one-for-the-Other is 
enlarged to encompass the plurality of beings of a community. And precisely 
because, for Lévinas, the community is the site where our social and political 
formations must be constantly put under scrutiny for an on-going democratic 
project,” Lévinas ultimately joins Consolo in locating in our storytelling the 
possibility for imagining a better future. In the words of Fuga dall’Etna, “E una 
lotta, quella della letteratura, contro il potere, che cerca sempre di cancellare la 
nostra memoria per non farci avere consapevolezza del presente e non farci 
immmaginare il futuro” (28). 

To be sure, this process of imagination will be an uneasy and uncanny one, 
since all calls to responsibility for the Other will ultimately erode and subvert 
our relation to “truth.” Nonetheless, as Eaglestone has commented in reference 
to Blanchot’s The Writing of the Disaster, it “is in these moments when our 
sense of our selves and our relation to the /ogos is interrupted and put into 
question that the ethics of literature are at their clearest” (175). 

In conclusion, Vincenzo Consolo’s // sorriso. dell’ignoto  marinaio 
expresses an ethical moment despite a time of weakened hermeneutics. 
However, this ethical moment is no longer the product of the conceptual 
framework of modernity and therefore can not rest upon a /ogos that would be 
revealed in the language of “truth” articulated by the fictional text. As Consolo 
suggests in the interview Fuga dall’Etna, the reciprocity between ethics and 
novelistic discourse depends upon a narrative and linguistic experimentation. 
When read from the framework of Lévinas’s post-rationalist discourse on ethics 
and Newton’s  Lévinas-inspired model of  narratology, Consolo’s 
experimentation constitutes an encounter binding tellers and listeners, including 
us, as readers and critics of narrative. Such an encounter ultimately foregrounds 
how the ethics of contemporary life and of postmodernist stories that give a 
meaning to it depend upon intersubjective relations of responsibility and 
obligation towards the alterity of all the others, and especially upon the 
willingness to fulfill our duties before demanding our rights. 


University of Connecticut, Storrs
