Steven Shankman 





Ethics, Transcendence, and the Other: 
Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 


What do we mean when we say “the Other’? In contemporary critical theory, 
and particularly in postcolonial theory, “the Other” generally refers to the person 
who has been marginalized by the dominant power structure: the woman, the 
person of color, the disadvantaged and powerless. The word has a negative 
valence. Let us call this alterity 2. Alterity, in this sense, is even at times 
transformed into a verb: to “other” someone is to turn that person into a thing, an 
object, to dehumanize her or him. In this essay, | wish to recover a different 
meaning of the word Other, one that is indebted to the philosophical work of 
Martin Buber and particularly Emmanuel Lévinas. In this tradition, the term 
otherness — alterity — has a positive valence. Let us call it a/terity 1. To speak 
in the language of the phenomenological tradition, alterity in this sense is that 
which precisely evades conceptualization, it is the otherness of the other subject 
that conceptualization would of itself automatically violate, since 
conceptualization entails knowing and hence the assimilation of alterity by the 
ego that seeks to know. Otherness, in this Lévinasian sense, is an occasion for 
the transcending of the ego. It is the face of the Other that occasions this 
transcendence of the ego and that demands my responsibility. 

At first glance, it would appear that the topic of ethics in Milione of Marco 
Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili is an unpromising one. Alterity, for Marco 
Polo, is experienced largely as a quest for the objectively different. And there is 
barely a trace of personality, or of an active subjectivity, in Marco Polo himself. 
He is hardly a subject in the sense of someone whose deepest convictions are 
open to question, although he is certainly curious about cultural difference, in a 
rather detached manner. But this curiosity almost never appears to threaten the 
stable center of Polo’s consciousness. We almost never sense that his deepest 
convictions are shaken by the vast differences between the assumptions and 
practices of his own medieval-European-Christian culture and the attitudes of 
the inhabitants of the Asian continent that he observes and catalogues. As John 
Larner has recently shown in Marco Polo and the Discovery of the World, the 
book Milione is best understood as a serious and remarkably learned and novel 
work of positivist geography. There are moments in Marco Polo’s text, however, 
that gesture towards transcendence in the ethical, Lévinasian sense, moments 
that reveal the possibility of the beginning of a dialogue with the Other. It is this 
very possibility of dialogue, and hence of sensing the trace of the transcendent 


Annali d’Italianistica 19 (2001) 


138 Steven Shankman 


otherness of the other person or other subject, that haunts the text of Calvino’s 
novel, which focuses on the relationship between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan 
that is derived from Milione. That breakthrough is constantly frustrated in 
Calvino’s text, however; and while it never in fact occurs, the author does offer 
some suggestions as to why that dialogue is perpetually aborted. The reasons for 
that abortion stem directly from Calvino’s critique of the quest for alterity, or 
otherness, in the sense of an objectively quantifiable difference from the self: the 
precise sense | have labeled, above, as alterity 2, but which is, in truth, not 
alterity at all. 

Throughout most of Milione, Marco presents himself as a Christian and 
appears to accept the truth of the Christian religion in a conventional, unprobing 
manner. This is certainly no great spiritualist text. The truth of Christianity 
generally resides, for Marco Polo, in its bare and positivist truth or falsity. At the 
beginning of the book, Marco tells of his father’s first visit to the Great Khan’s 
kingdom. It turns out that the Khan is not hostile towards the Christian faith. 
Indeed, he demonstrates curiosity about it. But the curiosity has to do mostly 
with the truth or falsity of the Christian religion, rather than with its ethical or 
experiential dimension. If Christianity happens in fact to be true, the Khan wants 
to hedge his bets and get on board. He thus orders Marco’s father and uncle to 
carry a letter to the Pope, as Marco (via his storyteller, Rusticiano, or 
Rustichello) relates: 


He begged that the Pope would send as many as an hundred persons of our Christian 
faith; intelligent men, acquainted with the Seven Arts, well qualified to enter into 
controversy, and able to prove by force of argument to idolators and other kinds of folk 
[a les ydules et a les autres”], that the Law of Christ was best [“la loi cristiene est 
meior’], and that all other religions were false and naught; and that if they would prove 
this, he and all under him would become Christians and the Church’s liegemen. Finally 
he charged his Envoys to bring back to him some Oil of the Lamp which burns on the 
Sepulchre of our Lord at Jerusalem. 

(Travels |. 13-14; Milione VIII. 9-10)! 


' The text of Milione that I principally cite (by volume and page number) is the venerable 
English translation by Sir Henry Yule. I choose this version, in part, because it will be 
more readily understood by readers of this essay than the early fourteenth-century text 
written in Franco-Italian, which I sometimes cite (by section and line numbers) along 
with Yule’s English, and which “most scholars [...] take to be the earliest and closest to 
the original” (Larner 184). Yule is translating M. G. Pauthier’s Livre de Marco Polo 
citoyen de Venise, conseiller privé et commissaire impérial de Khoubilai-Khadn (Paris, 
1865); his version does not always correspond with the earlier Franco-Italian text. The 
textual history of Milione is complex and uncertain. Indeed, as Spence has noted (3), “we 
do not have the original manuscript, but only copies of a lost original, amended copies of 
those copies, and translations or abbreviations of copies. Nor do we even know with 
certainty the language of the ‘first’ version. Most probably it was in Venetian or 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 139 


Hence, for Kublai Khan — and for Marco, as well, who approvingly tells this 
story — Christianity is reduced to the truth or falsity of literal belief. Elsewhere 
in the text, as in the story of the miraculously moved stone that stood at the foot 
of the pillar of a Christian church in Samarkand (I. 183-87), the truth of 
Christianity is likewise sought through the examination of visible evidence. 
Ethics is beside the point. 

As another instance of the unshakability of Marco’s Christian beliefs, 
despite the extent of his travels, consider the place of Buddhists in his text, 
whom he consistently refers to as “Idolators” [‘tydules” Milione VIII. 8]. In 
Chapter CIV, Marco observes the great charity (‘il fait grant charité as povres 
gens” Milione CIV. 1) that the Emperor displays to the poor. Even when Marco 
has the opportunity of seeing, in Buddhist charity, an equivalence to this prime 
Christian virtue, his doctrinal certainties manage to block the way towards a 
transcendence of the ego. After praising the Khan for his almsgiving, he notes 
that the “Tartars, before they were converted to the religion of the Idolators, 
never practised almsgiving” (I. 445). Previously, the Mongols had interpreted 
the lowly fortunes of the destitute as evidence of God’s scorn for them, but “the 
sages of the Idolaters [...] told the Great Kaan that it was a good work to provide 
for the poor, and that his idols would be greatly pleased if he did so. And since 
then he has taken to do for the poor so much as you have heard” (I. 445-446). It 
is clear, according to the narrator in this passage, that even those who selflessly 
do good for others may still, nonetheless, be described as “idolators.” This 
statement indicates that Marco’s Christianity is, in effect, itself a version of 
“idolatry” in that sense that his is a religion that has cut loose from its central 
message of the necessity of self-sacrifice and of charity towards others. Marco, 
unwittingly and paradoxically, renders Christianity just one more form of 
idolatry when, as a professed Christian, he in effect turns his back on the Other 
by uncharitably labeling charitable Buddhists “idolators.” 

There is at least one moment in the text, however, when Marco leaves 
behind his religious provincialism. At the age of eighty-five, the Great Khan 


‘Lombard’ dialect, subsequently translated into Italianate French, and thence into Latin.” 
Even the meaning of the title of the book, Milione, by which it is known in Italian, is a 
matter of debate. Larner believes that, after Marco was released from the prison in Genoa 
in which he allegedly dictated his narrative to Rustichello (or Rusticiano), Marco’s 
family “had bought a palace called Milione. [...] Roberto Gallo argued that the name 
‘Milione’ is a corruption of ‘Vilione’ and that it was the palace of the Vilione family [...] 
which the Polos bought. Though not all that large, it was built around a courtyard and had 
a tower. Marco’s uncle, the elder Marco, and his descendants took the name *Milion’ as a 
surname; and in the course of time the word came to be applied to Marco himself as a 
nickname. Jacopo d’Acqui writes of him as ‘Marco who is called “Milono” [sic] which is 
how the Venetians says “riches of a thousand pounds.’” Eventually the word ‘Milion’ 
was used in some manuscripts as a title for the Book” (44). 


140 Steven Shankman 


must confront the rebellion of one of his liegemen, his uncle Nayan, who is 
(rather remarkably) fifty-five years his junior. Nayan was “a baptized Christian,” 
and of course the Khan is not. Since Marco’s belief in the truth of Christianity 
seems so unshakable, it is worth noting that his sympathies, in this contest, lie 
with the pagan Great Khan rather than with the Christian Nayan. Indeed, Marco 
sees the Khan, in this instance, as the great defender of Christianity. As a 
baptized Christian, Nayan had carried a great cross to battle with him, but was 
defeated nonetheless. After the battle, many non-Christians — “‘saracing, ydres 
et juif, et maintes autres jens que ne creoent en Dieu” (“Saracens and Idolaters 
and Jews, and many others that believed not in God” I. 343; LXXX) — taunted 
the Christians. Kublai Khan bade the Christians be of good heart, 


for if the Cross had rendered no help to Nayan, in that It had done right well; nor could 
that which was good, as It was, have done otherwise; for Nayan was a disloyal and 
traitorous Rebel against his Lord, and well deserved that which had befallen him. 
Wherefore the Cross of your God did well in that It gave him no help against the right. 
(se la crois dou vostre dieu ne a aidiés Naian, elle a fait grant raisonz; por ce que elle est 
bone ne devoit faire se bien non et droit. Naian estoite desliaus et traitres que venoit 
contre son seignors et por ce est grant droit de ce que li est avenu, et la crois dou vostre 
dieu fist bien, se elle ne |’aide contre droit.) 

(1. 344; LXXX. 8-9) 


The narrator here associates Christianity with justice. He suggests that the 
“pagan” Kublai Khan can be more just, and hence more truly Christian, than the 
baptized Nayan. 

Why is the Khan so interested in defending Christianity, and what does this 
have to do with the transcendence of the ego and with the move towards ethics? 
And what does all this have to do with alterity, and specifically with the 
difference between what I have called a/terity ] and alterity 2? Could it be that it 
is precisely Kublai Khan’s affection for Marco Polo, who is so culturally 
different from himself, that encourages him to want to see, in Marco’s Christian 
creed, why Marco himself (and the world he represents) so values this particular 
religion? Milione records the traces of the Khan’s affection for Marco Polo and 
of Marco’s admiration for the ethics of a “pagan” who, remarkably, can act 
more justly than a baptized Christian. 

As Jonathan Spence remarks, Calvino’s novel Le cittd invisibili takes as its 
starting point the relationship between Marco and the Khan (237). There is only 
a brief mention made in Milione of the affection the Khan feels for Marco. The 
book is mainly a catalogue of the wonders witnessed, in a generally sober 
manner, by Marco. The tone of sober observation is relieved , however, not only 
by Marco’s passionate admiration of the Khan, which I have just discussed. It is 
relieved, as well, by the narrator’s recording of the Khan’s affection for Marco. 
Early in the book, Kublai Khan had sent Marco out on a state visit: 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 141 


When Mark returned from his ambassage he presented himself before the Emperor, and 
after making his report of the business with which he was charged, and its successful 
accomplishment, he went on to give an account of the pleasant and intelligent manner of 
all the novelties and strange things that he had seen and heard insomuch that the Emperor 
and all such as heard his story were surprised, and said: “If this young man live, he will 
assuredly come to be a person of great worth and ability.” 

(Quant Marc fu retorné de sa mesajarie, el s’en vait devant le grant kan et li renunse toute 
le fait por coi il estoit alés — et l’avoit achevee moult bien — puis li dit toutes le novités et 
toutes le coses qu’il avoit veu[¢] en cele voie, si bien et sajemant que le grant kan, et cel¢ 
tuit que l’oient, en unt grant mervoie, et distrent entr’aus; se cest jeune vif por aajes il ne 
puet falir qu’il ne soit home de grant seng et de grant valor.) 


Thereafter Messer Marco abode in the Kaan’s employment some seventeen years, 
continually going and coming, hither and thither, on the missions that were entrusted to 
him by the Lord. [...] And, as he knew all the sovereign’s ways, like a sensible man he 
always took much pains to gather knowledge of anything that would be likely to interest 
him, and then on his return to Court he would relate everything in regular order, and thus 
the Emperor came to hold him in great love and favour. [...] So the Emperor became ever 
more partial to him, and treated him with the greater distinction, and kept him so close to 
his person that some of the Barons waxed very envious thereat. 
(Et le grant kan li plasoit tant l’afer de meser Marc que il le vo<lo>it grant bien et li fasoit 
si grant onor et le tenoit si pres de soi que les autres baron en avent grant enoie.) 

(1. 30-31; Milione XVII. 1-2; 7-8) 


Marco Polo has won the affection of the Great Khan, in large part, on the basis 
of his special ability to represent, to the Khan, the nature and extent of his 
empire. 

In Milione, then, Marco represents the empire to the Emperor, and he does 
so in skillful and no doubt flattering ways. In this sense, Marco’s tales hardly 
offer opportunities for the Khan to experience the transcendence of the ego that | 
am associating with ethics, in the Lévinasian sense. But for Calvino the 
relationship between Marco and the Khan more seriously flirts with the 
possibility of true dialogue; it hovers on the brink of the transcendence of the 
egos of the participants, but that transcendence never quite happens. Why? 

The problem, as Calvino analyzes it, lies in the nature of a language that 
remains within the bounds of representation rather than in its capacity to 
welcome the Other, to address the Other. As Lévinas writes in Totalité et infini 
(65), “La prétention de savoir et d’atteindre |’Autre, s’accomplit dans la relation 
avec autrui, laquelle se coule dans la relation du language dont l’essentiel est 
l’interpellation, le vocatif’ (“The claim to know and to reach the other is 
realized in the relationship with the Other that is cast in the relation of language, 
where the essential is the interpellation, the vocative” Totality and Infinity 69). 
Calvino is frequently concerned with critiquing the Western subject’s exclusive 
reliance upon the purely intentional consciousness, as embodied in his character 


142 Steven Shankman 


Palomar.” This mode of viewing reality in its purely objective mode — the 
privileged mode of consciousness since the Renaissance, culminating in 
Descartes — has a devastating effect on interpersonal relations, as the example of 
Calvino’s Palomar demonstrates. Influenced by © structuralism and 
poststructuralism (as well as poststructuralism’s eighteenth-century harbinger, 
Laurence Sterne), in Le citta invisibili Calvino engages in a skeptical critique of 
language as a bearer of ultimate truth. Calvino, as a reader of Derrida, has 
learned poststructuralism’s lessons well (Hume 12-13). But Calvino’s text may 
take us back, before and beyond Derrida, to Derrida’s teacher, Emmanuel 
Lévinas, for whom language in its discursive or representational mode is a 
“said” that — unless recalled to its “saying” — will in fact betray ethics, or what I 
have called the fitting response to a/terity /. For the meaning of language, for 
Lévinas, is precisely its ‘“‘interpellative” nature, its speaking in the vocative. 
Language, in this ethical, Lévinasian sense, is an invitation to the Other, the 
basis and the beginning of dialogue, of which there is the faintest trace in 
Milione, but whose traces are more visible in Calvino’s Le citta invisibili. 

In Calvino’s lyrical novel, which the author himself referred to as a “poema 
d’amore,” we thus do not find a true dialogue between Marco Polo and Kublai 
Khan, but what we do discover is the trace of the Other. Indeed, in the first 
section — which, as is the case with all these framing sections of the nine 
chapters of the book, is nameless, perhaps thus signifiying the very 
unrepresentability of the Other — of the first of the nine chapters of the book, 
Calvino alludes to the themes we have just been discussing: 


Non é detto che Kublai Kan creda a tutto quel che dice Marco Polo quando gli descrive 
le citta visitate nelle sue ambascerie, ma certo |'imperatore dei tartari continua ad 
ascoltare il giovane veneziano con pit curiosita e attenzione che ogni altro suo messo 0 
esploratore. Nella vita degli imperatori c’é un momento, che segue all’orgoglio per 
l'ampiezza sterminata dei territori che abbiamo conquistato, alla malinconia e al sollievo 
di sapere che presto rinunceremo a conoscerli e a comprenderli; un senso come di vuoto 
che ci prende una sera con |'odore degli elefanti dopo la pioggia e della cenere di 
sandalo che si raffredda nei bracieri; una vertigine che fa tremare I fiumi e le montagne 
istoriati sulla fulva groppa dei planisferi, arrotola uno sull’altro i dispacci che ci 
annunciano il franare degli ultimi eserciti nemici di sconfitta in sconfitta, e scrosta la 
ceralacca dei sigilli di re mai sentiti nominare che implorano la protezione delle nostre 
armate avanzanti in cambio di tributi annuali in metalli preziosi, pelli conciate e gusci di 


° For a skeptical perspective on the efficacy of dialogue similar to that found in Le citta 
invisibili (first published in 1972), see Palomar (published in 1983), * II fischio del 
merlo” (“The Blackbird’s Whistle”), Romanzi e racconti I1.891-96. Calvino’s questioning 
whether or not birds can hold conversations recalls Laurence Sterne’s famous passage on 
the starling in A Sentimental Journey through France and Italy (94-102), particularly the 
chapter entitled “THE PASSPORT/THE HOTEL DE PARIS” (94-97). 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 143 


testuggine: é il momento disperato in cui si scopre che quest'impero che ci era sembrato 
la somma di tutte le meraviglie é uno sfacelo senza fine né forma, che la sua corruzione é 
troppo incancrenita perché il nostro scettro possa mettervi riparo, che il trionfo sui 
sovrani avversari ci ha fatto eredi della loro lunga rovina. Solo nei resoconti di Marco 
Polo, Kublai Kan riusciva a discernere, attraverso le muraglie e le torri destinate a 
crollare, la filigrana d'un disegno cosi sottile da sfuggire al morso delle termiti. 
(Kublai Khan does not necessarily believe everything Marco Polo says when he describes 
the cities visited on his expeditions, but the emperor of the Tartars does continue 
listening to the young Venetian with greater attention and curiosity than he shows any 
other messenger or explorer of his. In the lives of emperors there is a moment which 
follows pride in the boundless extension of the territories we have conquered, and the 
melancholy and relief of knowing we shall soon give up any thought of knowing and 
understanding them. There is a sense of emptiness that comes over us at evening, with the 
odor of the elephants after the rain and the sandalwood ashes growing cold in the 
braziers, a dizziness that makes rivers and mountains tremble on the fallow curves of the 
planispheres where they are portrayed, and rolls up, one after the other, the despatches 
announcing to us the collapse of the last enemy troops, from defeat to defeat, and flakes 
the wax of the seals of obscure kings who beseech our armies’ protection, offering in 
exchange annual tributes of precious metals, tanned hides, and tortoise shell. It is the 
desperate moment when we discover that this empire, which had seemed to us the sum of 
all wonders, is an endless, formless ruin, that corruption’s gangrene has spread too far 
to be healed by our scepter, that the triumph over enemy sovereigns has made us the 
heirs of their long undoing. Only in Marco Polo’s accounts was Kublai Khan able to 
discern, through the walls and towers destined to crumble, the tracery of a pattern so 
subtle it could escape the termites’ gnawing.) 

(Romanzi e racconti Il. 36/1; Invisible Cities 5-6)° 


Here, at the very beginning of the work, we have a marvelous description of the 
spiritual emptiness (vwoto) that accompanies the recognition, by the imperial 
consciousness, of the specter of transcendence that continually eludes its endless 
and finally pointless intentionalist grasping. The intentional consciousness can 
apprehend only that which it represents, but this representation is not other. It is 
the very fact that the intentional consciousness grasps reality in the mode of 
representation that keeps the Khan’s consciousness bound within its imperial 
sameness. What haunts his consciousness in the evenings that follow daily 
conquest, “con l’odore degli elefanti dopo la pioggia” (“with the odor of the 
elephants after the rain”), is his unfulfilled longing for the truly other, or what 
Martin Buber refers to as the “between” (Zwischen) in which a “meeting” occurs 
between persons, thus turning — for the subject — an “it” into a “Thou.” At this 





> Calvino places in italics the sections of his novel that begin and conclude each chapter, 
and I shall honor his intentions by preserving the italics. It is precisely in these sections 
that the reader encounters the dialogues between Marco Polo and Kublai Khan. 


144 Steven Shankman 


twilight moment, a sense of emptiness (vwoto) and dizziness (una vertigine) sets 
in. The Khan recognizes that he grasps his vast empire only in the mode of its 
representation, i.€., as “i fiumi e le montagne” (“rivers and mountains’) that his 
“vertigine” makes tremble, “istoriati sulla fulva groppa dei planisferi’ (“on the 
fallow curves of the planispheres where they are portrayed’). The malincolia 
(melancholy) that the Khan feels is precisely the longing for the Other, in the 
sense in which it is understood by Buber and Lévinas. The Khan recognizes that, 
despite his extraordinary power and the vastness of his conquered territories, his 
own subjectivity is woefully incomplete. Only through openness, through a 
welcoming, in the mode of passivity, of the face of the Other can the 
consciousness be disrupted by transcendence. 

The activist, imperial conqueror is pleasantly reduced to a passive listener 
in the presence of his ambassador Marco Polo, the literal truth of whose accounts 
the Khan does not necessarily believe. What attracts the Khan to Marco is not the 
objective contents of his ambassador’s narrated representations of the vast 
reaches of the Khan’s empire, but rather what Lévinas calls “proximité.” As 
Alphonso Lingis explains in the introduction to his translation of Lévinas’s 
second great philosophical work (after Totalité et infini), Autrement qu’étre ou 
au-dela de l’essence, “proximity” is the closeness one feels in the presence of a 
neighbor, for whom one is infinitely responsible: “The other, my neighbor [/e 
prochain] concerns me, afflicts me with a closeness [proximité] closer than the 
closeness of entities” (Otherwise than Being xxv). In the words of Lévinas 
(Autrement qu’étre 178), “La proximité est fraternité avant l’essence et avant la 
mort; ayant un sens malgré I’étre et le néant, malgré le concept” (“Proximity is 
fraternity before essence and before death, having a meaning despite being and 
nothingness, despite concepts” Otherwise than Being 139). It is precisely this 
“proximity [...] closer than the closeness of entities” that the Khan senses in the 
trace (“la filigrana”) of the Other who is Marco Polo: ‘Solo nei resoconti di 
Marco Polo, Kublai Kan riusciva a discernere, attraverso le muraglie et le torri 
destinate a crollare, la filigrana d'un disegno cosi sottile da sfuggire al morso 
delle termiti’ (“Only in Marco Polo’s accounts was Kublai Khan able to discern, 
through the walls and towers destined to crumble, the tracery of a pattern so 
subtle it could escape the termites’ gnawing”). The permanence that the Khan 
seeks in the interminable expansion of his empire can, perhaps, only be 
approximated in his openness to a dialogue with the Other. Only this has the 
capacity ‘“‘sfuggire al morso delle termiti’ (“to escape the termites’ gnawing’’). 

It is thus perhaps not the content of what Marco Polo has to say, but rather 
his presence as a potential interlocutor, that the Khan finds so inspirational about 
him. Marco Polo becomes for the Emperor, in brief, the catalyst through which 
reality is, at least approximately, transformed from an “it” into a “Thou,” thus 
allowing for the possibility of a Buberian “meeting” to take place. In the 
dialogue that concludes the first chapter, it is Marco Polo who is able to 
represent the empire to the Emperor in a manner that, at least potentially, 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 145 


transcends the limitations of knowing in the phenomenal, Kantian sense. Kant 
had divided objects of knowledge into noumena and phenomena, an opposition 
that precludes our ability to know the noumena, or things-in-themselves 
(Critique of Pure Reason 266ff.). The Khan is a foreigner to his subjects. 
(Indeed, as a Mongol, he is literally a foreigner even to the Han Chinese, the 
dominant ethnic group of traditional China and the group from which emperors 
had traditionally been culled.) There is no chance of a “meeting” between the 
Emperor and his subjects, since the Khan’s “ambasciatori erano persiani 
armeni Ssiriani copti turcomani’’ (“ambassadors were Persians, Armenians, 
Syrians, Copts, Turkomans’’), and since “/’imperatore é colui che é straniero a 
ciascuno dei suoi sudditi’ (“the emperor is he who is a foreigner to each of his 
subjects”), “solo attraverso occhi e orecchi stranieri l’impero  poteva 
manifestare la sua esistenza a Kublai” (“only through foreign eyes and ears 
could the empire manifest its existence to Kublai” \\. 373; Invisible Cities 21). In 
a strictly Kantian sense, the pristinely noumenal quality of his empire is 
faithfully conveyed by these foreign ambassadors, who in “/ingue 
incomprensibili al Kan [...] riferivano notizie intese in lingue a loro 
incomprensibili (“languages incomprehensible to the Khan [...] relayed 
information incomprehensible to them” II. 373; Invisible Cities 21). 

While this method of communication is ontologically rigorous, it is finally 
inhuman. In Lévinasian terms, the language of the Khan’s foreign ambassadors 
is a language that consists of ‘“‘saids” that have been completely severed from 
any prelinguistic saying that would constitute an invitation to meeting and to 
dialogue: a language severed from ethics. Hence, in this incomprehensible 
language of the foreign ambassadors, the potential ““Thous” are lumped in with 
the “‘its” in a catalogue whose alleged objectivity occludes the cruelty it narrates: 
the ambassadors tell of “/e cifre introitate dal fisco imperiale, i nomi e i 
patronomici dei funzionari deposti e decapitati, le dimensioni dei canali 
d’irrigazione che i magri fiumi nutrivano in tempi di siccita” (“the revenues 
received by the imperial treasury, the first and last names of officials dismissed 
and decapitated, the dimensions of the canals that the rivers fed in times of 
drought” II. 373; Invisible Cities 21). In a Sternean (and Rabelasian) gesture, 
Calvino has Marco Polo, who at this moment in the narrative is ignorant of the 
languages of the people he has visited in the Khan’s empire, communicate with 
the Khan through gesture alone. The Khan finds these “segni” (“signs”) much 
more comprehensible than the ambassadors’ languages; but his dialogue with 
Marco, for the Khan, is still conducted in the language of ontology rather than 
ethics: namely, of signs and emblems rather than of persons. “// giorno in cui 
conoscero tutti gli enblemi” (“On the day when I shall know all the emblems”), 
the Khan asks Marco, “riusciro a possedere il mio impero, finalmente?” (“shall 
I be able to possess my empire, at last?”). On that day, Marco implies in his 
response, the Emperor will no longer be human: “quel giorno sarai tu stesso 
emblema tra gli emblemi” (“On that day you will be an emblem among 


146 Steven Shankman 


emblems” Il. 374; Invisible Cities 22-23). 

Eventually Marco Polo learns the languages that enable him to 
communicate his findings to the Emperor, and the merchant even becomes adept 
at speaking the Khan’s native Tartar tongue. But what continues to intrigue the 
Emperor is precisely the ways in which Marco Polo’s language is not restricted 
— just as the novel itself is not — to the purely descriptive, representational mode. 
For what made the words of Marco Polo, the Emperor’s ‘“‘inarticolato 
informatore” (“inarticulate informer’), particularly precious to the Khan was ‘“‘/o 
spazio che restava loro intorno, un vuoto non riempito di parole” (“the space 
that remained around it, a void not filled with words’ II. 386; Invisible Cities 
38). 

For Lévinas’s theory of the face and its relation to ethics, one of the most 
suggestive parts of Calvino’s novel is the second section, entitled “Le citta e i 
morti,” of Chapter Six. It is the epiphany of the human face that, for Lévinas, 
breaks the spell of presence and requires my ethical response. Most of Marco 
Polo’s descriptions of the cities he visits are all too visible. He speaks of 
patterns, dimensions, fantasies, but rarely of persons. !t is in the city of Adelma 
that the alterity of the human face almost breaks through the trance of presence 
and of fantasy — but not quite. Marco is unable to respond to the alterity of 
each unique face of the inhabitants of this city. The faces are reduced to 
phenomena that appear. They are not traces of an alterity that would disrupt 
presence. Each face that Marco sees, rather than being the occasion for 
transcendence into the ethical sphere, is rather an instance of a phenomenal 
reality that he readily assimilates (or wishes to assimilate), since each face he 
encounters reminds him of a face that he has already seen. For once in his novel, 
Calvino’s narrator in his travels confronts the reality of human suffering. Marco 
observes: 


Mi turbo la vista d’un malato di febbri rannicchiato per terra con una coperta sulla testa: 
mio padre pochi giorni prima di morire aveva gli occhi gialli e la barba ispida come lui 
tal quale. Girai lo sguardo; non osavo fissare pil nessuno in viso. 

(I was upset by the sight of a fever victim huddled on the ground, a blanket over his head: 
my father a few days before his death had yellow eyes and a growth of beard like this 


man. | turned my face aside; I no longer dared look anyone in the face.) 
(II. 438: /nvisible Cities 94) 


Marco Polo fears confronting alterity in the Lévinasian sense; he fears 
confronting a unique Other who demands his ethical response. If he continues to 
look at these faces, who at first uncannily resemble faces he has known, he 
worries that when “le somiglianze si dissolvano e appaino facce estranee, 
apportatrici d’angoscia” (“resemblances will dissolve, alien faces will appear, 
bearing anguish” II. 438; /nvisible Cities 94). Hence, Marco decides it is best for 
him not to look at all. He has reached a stage in his life, he comments, when “la 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 147 


mente si rifiuta d’accettare altre fisionomie, altre espressioni” (“the mind refuses 
to accept more faces, more expressions” II. 438; /nvisible Cities 95). Every new 
face simply recalls previous faces he has known, and these faces stare at him 
demanding a response that he finds burdensome. 

Calvino’s Marco Polo thus resists the epiphany of the face. The Emperor, 
who is in search of the essence of his empire, likewise resists transcendence in 
the ethical, Lévinasian sense. Although his attempted dialogue with Marco Polo 
contains the traces of a confrontation with the Other, that dialogue is constantly 
undermined by his search for essences that can be known, represented, and thus 
possessed. Towards the end of the novel, the Emperor suggests that, rather than 
have Marco Polo relate to him what he has seen of the Khan’s empire in his 
travels, he and Marco should play chess. Marco will then attempt to convey to 
the Khan the nature and essence of his empire by means of the pieces the two 
will move along the chessboard and the patterns thus created. By contemplating 
“questi paesaggi essenziali” (“these essential landscapes”), the Khan will thus 
be able to reflect “su/l’ordine invisibile che regge le citta, sulle regole cui 
risponde il loro sorgere e prender forma e prosperare” (“on the invisible order 
that sustains cities, on the rules that decreed how they rise, take shape and 
prosper’), and at times he thought he was “su/ punto di scoprire un sistema 
coerente e armonioso che sottostava alle infinite difformita e disarmonie” (“on 
the verge of discovering a coherent, harmonious system underlying the infinite 
deformities and discords”) of the actual empire (Il. 462; /nvisible Cities 122). 
He now even feels it unnecessary to send his ambassador Marco Polo out on any 
future missions, since the pattern set by the movement of the chess pieces can 
more efficiently reveal the essential. As the narrator observes, 


A forza di scorporare le sue conquiste per ridurle all’essenza, Kublai era arrivato 

all'operazione estrema: la conquista definitiva, di cui i multiformi tesori dell'impero non 

erano che involucri illusori, si riduceva a un tassello di legno piallato: il nulla... . 

(By disembodying his conquests to reduce them to the essential, Kublai had arrived at the 

extreme operation: the definitive conquest, of which the empire's multiform treasures 

were only illusory envelopes. It was reduced to a square of plane wood: nothingness. . . .) 
(II. 462; Invisible Cities 123) 


Almost as if he were reading Lévinas, Calvino here — by implication — is critical 
of what is occluded by the single-minded emphasis upon the search for 
representation and essence in the rigorous ontological sense. We must wait for 
the conclusion of the work before what is missing is revealed to be precisely 
ethics, which, in Lévinas’s view, must always precede ontology. The emphasis 
here is on how the single-minded search for essence creates a dangerously 
autonomous game in which the human is replaced by essentialized wooden 
figures being moved about in a chess match. Humans are here reduced to pawns 
in a game. As the Khan observes, he may have mastered the game of 


148 Steven Shankman 


representing and knowing essences, “ma adesso era il perché del gioco a 
sfuggirgl’’ (“but now it was the game’s purpose that eluded him” II. 462; 
Invisible Cities 123). What is “il perché del gioco,” “the game’s purpose’? 
Linguistic signs, in Derridean fashion, point only toward themselves, in an 
infinite regress of self-reflexiveness in Calvino’s text. The chess game, which 
the Khan hopes will reveal the essential, opens up only on the self that 
represents phenomena to itself and that perceives essences. More specifically, 
the chess game reveals the materiality of its own failed efforts at gesturing 
towards what is truly other, or what I have called alterity /. 

What do I mean by “the materiality of its own efforts at gesturing towards 
what is truly other’? Here we need to return to Lévinas’s notion of the 
difference between the “saying” (/e dire) and the “said” (/e dit). Language, for 
Lévinas, has the capacity to welcome the Other, but only in what he calls its 
“interpellative” or vocative mode, i.e., when language is addressed to the Other 
as an invitation to dialogue. The language of the “said” is, in distinction, 
impersonal and carries with it the danger of freezing the “saying” within an 
inhumanly autonomous discourse that has lost its moorings in the “saying.” The 
danger is that the “saids”’ may have so cut loose from the intersubjective 
encounter that all we are left with is an impersonal discourse that refers only to 
itself. Lévinas’s student, Jacques Derrida, is the relentless analyst of this kind of 
self-referential discourse that is revealed to have nothing human at its center. All 
semiotics, with its adherence to the irreducibility of the signififying aspect of 
language in the mode of representation, is subject to this kind of deconstructive, 
Derridean analysis. Lévinas, however, is interested in analyzing these “saids” in 
relation to a “saying” that is, as he terms it, prelinguistic, which is another way 
of stating that ethics must precede ontology.” 

In Le citta invisibili, Calvino, as did Sterne before him, points out the 
endlessly self-reflexive nature of the language of “‘saids,” even as this language 
gestures towards essences. As I have mentioned, the Khan believes that the 
chess game managed “scorporare le sue conquiste per ridurle all’essenza” (“to 
disembody his conquests in order to reduce them to the essential’). Marco Polo 
then immediately calls into question the ontological efficacy of these gestures 
towards essence by focusing on the irreducible materiality and particularity of 
the signs that the Khan had taken as representing and revealing the essential: 


“La tua scacchiera, sire, é un intarsio di due legni: ebano e acero. Il tassello sul quale si 
fissa il tuo sguardo illuminato fu tagliato in uno strato del tronco che crebbe in un anno 
di siccita: vedi come si dispongono le fibre? Qui si scorge un nodo appena accennato:

Autrement qu'étre 6-9. Unlike the game of chess in Calvino’s novel, for Lévinas “Le dire 
précisément n’est pas un jeu” (“Saying is not a game” Autrement qu’étre 6; Otherwise 
than Being 5). 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 149 


una gemma tento di spuntare in un giorno di primavera precoce, ma la brina della notte 
l'obbligo a desistere. [...] Ecco un poro pitt grosso: forse & stato un nido d'una larva; 
non d'un tarlo perché appena nato avrebbe continuato a scavare, ma d'un bruco che 
rosicchio le foglie e fu la causa per cui |'albero fu scelto per essere abbattuto.... Questo 
margine fu inciso dall'ebanista con la sgorbia perché aderisse al quadrato vicino, pitt 
sporgente ...”’ 

“Your chessboard, sire, is inlaid with two woods: ebony and maple. The square on which 
your enlightened gaze is fixed was cut from the ring of a trunk that grew in a year of 
drought: you see how its fibers are arranged? here a barely hinted knot can be made out: 
a bud tried to burgeon on a premature spring day, but the night's frost forced it to desist. 
[...] Here is a thicker pore: perhaps it was a larvum’s nest; not a woodworm, because, 
once born, it would have begun to dig, but a caterpillar that gnawed the leaves and was 
the cause of the tree's being chosen for chopping down.... This edge was scored by the 


wood carver with his gouge so that it would adhere to the next square, more protruding 
5 


(Il. 469; /nvisible Cities 131) 


The Khan — in his imperial desire to know, to represent, and possess his vast 
empire — had dreamed of a realm of essences detached from the messy 
particularity of the living and of the human. Marco Polo is now disabusing him 
of this fantasy. He is doing so by fluently speaking to the Khan in the Emperor’s 
own Tartar language and by drawing the Emperor’s attention to the materiality 
of those signs that are supposed to represent abstract essences. 

I have been suggesting that Calvino, in Derridean and Sternean fashion, 
deconstructs a language that imagines it can articulate essences in an objective, 
impersonal manner. Such utterances are revealed to be always burdened by 
temporality, history, and materiality. But Calvino is not content with this 
deconstructive analysis. He is moving, | would suggest in conclusion, in a 
genuinely Lévinasian direction by suggesting that what is occluded in this 
search for essences is precisely ethics, or what Lévinas refers to as the 


> McLaughlin remarks on how this passage comments upon the very medium of language 
as a signifier (107): “Again there is an important echo in Polo’s words to describe the 
wood: ‘vedi come si dispongono le_fibre? Qui si scorge un nodo appena accennato (you 
see how its fibres are arranged? Here a barely hinted knot can be made out)’ [...]. The 
metaphor of wood confirms, if confirmation were needed, that again Calvino is also 
discussing here the medium of language. The words used recall the important passage in 
the 1963 letter to Mario Boselli about his own style [...]: ‘La pagina [...] € un spaccato di 
un legno, in cui si possono seguire come corrono le fibre, dove fanno nodo, dove si 
diparte un ramo” (“The written page [...] is like a cross-section of a piece of wood, in 
which one can follow the lines of the fibres, see where they form a knot, where a branch 
goes off’). As McLaughlin notes, in this chapter Calvino “develops the then fashionable 
metaphor of chess and its rules, used by structuralists, particularly Saussure, to describe 
the arbitrariness of the signifier in its relation to the signified.” On these issues see also 
Cannon. 


150 Steven Shankman 


transcendent otherness of the other person. In the final dialogue of the novel, 
Calvino names dialogue itself as the utopian place where a positive future can be 
born. As in Plato’s famous dialogue, The Republic, so in Calvino’s text (which 
is about the utopian city, as was Plato’s) it is clear that the city is a metaphor, or 
a sign, that describes the human soul. In this last section of the novel, the Khan 
brings out his atlas and asks Marco Polo about his view of the future, based on 
all the marvelous cities he has seen, so many of which he has described to the 
Emperor. He admits to the Khan that we perhaps never escape our own 
subjective prisons: “Chi comanda al racconto non é la voce: é l’orecchio” (“It is 
not the voice that demands the story: it is the ear” \\. 473; Invisible Cities 135). 
Even if we engage in dialogue and listen to our interlocutor, Marco suggests, we 
may hear only what we want to hear, and what we want to hear will shape the 
way our interlocutor speaks and even what he says, especially if he is a powerful 
emperor whom we, in his employ, might be tempted to flatter or at least please. 
Still, there is always the hope that we can escape from the prison of our purely 
subjective consciousness, for the possibilities of a positive future can be found 
in the pursuit of the invisible city that consists in 


il dialogo di due passanti che s’incontrano nel viavai [...] di segnali che uno manda e 
non sa che li raccoglie. Se ti dico che la citta cui tende il mio viaggio é discontinua nello 
spazio e nel tempo, ora pitt rada ora pit densa, tu non devi credere che si possa smettere 
di cercarla. 

(the dialogue of two passersby meeting in the crowd [...] of signals one sends out, not 
knowing who receives them. If I tell you that the city toward which my journey tends is 
discontinuous in space and time, now scattered, now more condensed, you must not 


believe the search for it can stop.) 
(Il. 497; Invisible Cities 164) 


This city can be an inferno, it is true, but if we listen to the Other and thus begin 
to engage in dialogue, the city need not be hellish: 


L'inferno dei viventi non é qualcosa che sara; se ce n’é uno, é quello che é gia qui, 
l'inferno che abitiamo tutti i giorni, che formiamo stando insieme. Due modi ci sono per 
non soffirne. Il primo riesce facile a molti: accettare |'inferno e diventarne parte fino al 
punto di non vederlo piu. Il secondo é rischioso ed esige attenzione e apprendimento 
continui: cercare e saper riconoscere chi e cosa, in mezzo all'inferno, non é inferno, e 
farlo durare, e dargli spazio. 
(The inferno of the living is not something that will be; if there is one, it is what is 
already here, the inferno where we live every day, that we form by being together. There 
are two ways to escape suffering it. The first is easy for many. accept the inferno and 
become such a part of it that you can no longer see it. The second is risky and demands 
constant vigilance and apprehension. seek and learn to recognize who and what, in the 
midst of the inferno, are not inferno, then make them endure, give them space.) 
(11.497-98: Invisible Cities 165) 


Milione of Marco Polo and Calvino’s Le citta invisibili 151 


Calvino is here clearly drawing on Sartre, who famously remarked, in his play 
Huis clos, “Venfer, c’est les Autres” (“Hell is other people” 62). For Sartre, the 
Other is a threat to the integrity of the self.° For Lévinas, however, the Other is 
that which calls me out of my self-enclosed world and for whose uniqueness | 
am uniquely responsible. In making a plea that the subject allow the Other 
“spazio,” Calvino is asking that we acknowledge the transcendent otherness of 
the other person and that we resist the imperial assimilation of the Other into our 
own consciousness. It is a risky business (‘‘é rischioso’’), but it is an opening 
towards a dialogue whose traces are visible in Calvino’s Le citta invisibili. 

What, then, is an invisible city? Is it a city of pure imagination, a city of 
which we always wish to hear once we have tired of the exotic city that we have 
just visited or that has just been described to us, and that has perhaps just lost its 
exotic charm? Is it a city of pure essences that have finally escaped from the 
burdens of matter and time? Or is it rather the trace (“la filigrana”’) of the 
eternal or the permanent to which we are open when we allow the Other to 
endure and give him space? Are the invisible city and ethics one and the same? 


University of Oregon, Eugene
