Angela M. Jeannet 





A Matter of Injustice: 
Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 


1. Introduction 
The debate on what relationship does or does not obtain and should or should 
not obtain, between writing and experience has been going on for centuries: an 
indication of how important the issue is for readers and writers alike. We are all 
faced with ethical questions in our everyday lives, and the written word is very 
much part of our existence.’ Italian authors throughout the centuries have 
consistently addressed the connection between creativity and pragmatic 
concerns, and have given large space in their writings to the issue of the 
individual’s relationship to society. The question whether literature has 
responsibilities has received a positive answer in their writing practice from the 
moment the Italian tongue became a written medium. As a matter of fact, the 
very question of what could be an “Italian language” has traditionally been 
couched in sociopolitical terms. 

Literature finds itself in an ambiguous position when it comes to theoretical 
discourses such as yesterday’s debates about commitment (or engagement, or 
impegno), or today’s reflections on ethics and postmodernism,’ because 


' Richard Kearney’s States of Mind is one of several recent collections of essays by 
contemporary thinkers, who approach such issues from a variety of viewpoints. As I put 
the finishing touches to this article, | read the announcement of a very timely publication, 
Massimo Lollini’s // vuoto della forma. Here the author traces the evolution of the 
concept of writing as witnessing and search for truth, through several moments in the 
Western, and largely Italian, tradition. Poets, dramatists, and fiction writers make the 
most incisive statements on this topic; for confirmation, I must refer the reader especially 
to two pages (218-19) of José Saramago’s Manuale di pittura e calligrafia, his first 
major, and exemplary, work of fiction. 

* By now, the terms “postmodern,” “postmodernity,” and “postmodernism” have a long 
and complex, even contradictory, history. Canonical texts include those written by Ihab 
Hassan, Linda Hutcheon, Andreas Huyssens, Fredric Jameson, E. Ann Kaplan, Jean- 
Francois Lyotard, and Andrew Ross. I would add to that customary list a few pages from 
Pertile’s introduction to The New Italian Novel, and from Biasin’s 1991 article (173-74), 
which underline the special connotations of “modernity” and “postmodernity” in the 
context of Italian culture and literature. Fundamental in Italian studies is the ground- 
breaking volume 9 of Annali d’Italianistica (1991) on the modern and postmodern, 
including Cervigni’s introductory essay. 


Annali d'Italianistica 19 (2001) 


154 Angela M. Jeannet 


literature uses language as its medium. Language is certainly not reserved for 
literary expression or even for written usage, therefore it is hopelessly emmeshed 
in what we indicate, perhaps vaguely, as “our experience.”” How can commitment 
be manifested in the writing practice, by the choice of themes and occasions, or 
the construction of characters, or even the very texture of writing? To analyze 
the ethical imperative from this perspective is not an easy task, but it continues 
to engage whoever is interested in what we call literature. It is precisely by 
taking my point of departure from the construction of a singular language that I 
can begin to explore the interweaving of the ethical dimension and the creative 
imagination in Antonio Tabucchi. It is not my intention to address what the 
man/citizen Tabucchi may say or write, as for instance in his La gastrite di 
Platone, or Gli Zingari e il Rinascimento, or what he asserts in his interviews as 
one of the most prominent intellectuals in today’s Italy, because those are in part 
public relations phenomena. Rather, I intend to examine what emerges from his 
fiction, particularly his most recent publications. 

The first choice a writer makes, especially an Italian writer, is the choice of 
language. Tabucchi has elaborated a version of standard Italian that is at the 
same time agile and cultivated, playful, colorful, and enlivened by an occasional 
roughness, or an intercalation borrowed from everyday speech. His language is 
anchored in the body, whose functions are essential to the development of the 
narrative. That is especially true for the digestive function, which was 
traditionally considered the least noble among those carried out by the various 
body systems. Its fields of action, including food and cooking, are ever present 
in Tabucchi’s fiction. Gian Paolo Biasin rightly asserts that, for instance, the 
descriptions of Portuguese dishes included in several novels produce a reality 
effect, turn the foods themselves into elements of the narrative, and ironically 
evoke the recipes in the sacred texts of the culinary tradition (“Altri cibi, altre 
istanze,” Le periferie). In Tabucchi’s latest novel, La testa perduta di 
Damasceno Monteiro, the entire first chapter focuses on elementary physical 
needs. As a matter of fact, Manolo the gypsy reaches a sense of harmony with 
the universe precisely when, in the early morning light, he meditatively abandons 
himself to the relief of a very familiar urge. It is important to note that foods, as 
well as those needs and actions that involve the body in all its materiality are 
given to us as words. Experience at its most basic level is translated into writing 
through the manipulation of a given linguistic system. For Tabucchi, one such 
system is the Italian language, and another is the Portuguese, which he once 
described as a concrete place, his “luogo di affetto e di riflessione” (Requiem 7), 
no doubt echoing Fernando Pessoa’s expression: “La mia patria é la lingua 
portoghese” (// libro dell'inquietudine 252). Tabucchi’s writing, as it is obvious 
even from the most superficial reading, is woven with elements taken from 
various languages in which human beings have translated their experience of the 
body. But the Tuscan expressions we find in Tabucchi’s texts of the 1970s, or 
the many Portuguese expressions he used later, are not exoticisms or distancing 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 155 


elements. On the contrary, they are the means by which Tabucchi anchors the 
written in the corporeal. They serve to remind us that his writing is connected to 
specific circumstances, places, and experiences. They make explicit the 
commitment to translate, indeed transfer, the immediate presence of the world 
into the chosen symbolic system, through the operations of the senses and the 
brain that seize and decode it. The commitment that storytelling makes, then, is 
to reveal, make accessible, and guarantee that presence. The alchemic gamble 
that has always been at the core of the game called writing, even at its most 
serious level, becomes an essential part of Tabucchi’s practice. 


2. The body 

Citing Tabucchi’s words in one of his critical studies on Fernando Pessoa (Un 
baule pieno di gente 88), Biasin adds: “Il cibo in Requiem ha la funzione di far 
risaltare ‘la naturalis salus dell’accettazione fenomenica del reale contrapposta 
al brivido metafisico dell’interrogazione sul reale stesso’” (Biasin, Le periferie 
140). The terminology Tabucchi uses in his essay is important because it refers 
to a contrapposition found in his own narrative, and also because it evokes a very 
specific current of thought. While in the 1980s Tabucchi had moved toward what 
Laura Lepschy defines “a more metaphysical view of life” (The New Italian 
Novel 203), his books of the 1990s revisit a universe in which materiality is 
foregrounded. The dominance of the phenomenological is made evident by the 
attention given to the corporeal changes, however minimal, in Tabucchi’s 
characters. The body’s daily life, its moments of euphoria, enjoyment, food 
intake, insomnia, sleepiness, sweating, and so on, provide the atmosphere, 
situations, images, and metaphors that are specific to his narrative. The texts 
always express a profound empathy with the ways the characters physically live 
the events; the writer’s imagination is compassionate and whimsical at the same 
time. One example of the complex bond connecting life experience and fantasy 
is an episode in Requiem (58-63), in which the narrator’s father, although dead at 
a mature age because of an illness that left a deep scar in his son’s memory, 
appears as a handsome youth in the son’s dream, freed of the anguish that still 
torments the narrator. Also in Requiem, remorse is compared to a most ordinary 
human affliction, a virus: “[...] penso che l’herpes sia un po’ come il rimorso, se 
ne sta addormentato dentro di noi e un bel giorno si sveglia e ci attacca, poi 
torna a dormire perché noi siamo riusciti ad ammansirlo, ma é sempre dentro di 
noi, non c’é niente da fare contro il rimorso” (79). 

It is to be expected that for any writer who privileges the world of 
phenomena the “scandal” of the body’s death — to borrow an expression dear to 
French Existentialists — would be a central concern; and indeed it constitutes a 
steady thematic undercurrent in most of Tabucchi’s fiction (Geerts). But death 
seldom comes quietly in his stories; rather, it is the consequence of various forms 
of violence, so that in the majority of cases the dead are still young. Most 
important, the violence represented by death, although at times “natural,” 


156 Angela M. Jeannet 


whether caused by a fatal illness or an accident, often has a political origin. 
However, in the heroic depictions of different Resistances, from the 1930s to the 
1950s, one always knows which characters caused death; whether good or bad, 
they are readily identifiable. Here, violent deaths are part of obscure agendas, 
and a means by which power is acquired and maintained by faceless 
executioners; responsibility for them is not claimed by any authority; in fact, they 
are meant to remain mysterious, as a pervasive form of intimidation and 
silencing. 

Death due to political violence, as a manifestation of the power one has over 
the other, is at the center of much Western writing since at least the eighteenth 
century. The volumes that illustrate the positions of thinkers and writers on 
torture, assassination, and the death penalty, those practices familiar to all more 
or less “legitimate” political forces, fill the shelves of our libraries. The critique 
of political and state violence in our culture proceeds from two main sources. On 
the one hand, there are the voices of those who represent secular attitudes 
inspired by, and descended from, the Enlightenment; on the other hand, there are 
the voices speaking from the perspective of ethical principles based on religious 
faiths. Twentieth-century thinkers, who have mostly turned their sights toward 
the phenomenological, have expressed themselves in different ways, depending 
on their interpretations of modernity, existentialism, and postmodernism.’ 
Tabucchi’s fiction, deeply rooted in its cultural soil in this respect as well as in 
others, is a meditation on death, including death inflicted by the violence that is 
exerted “in nome di fraintesa giustizia” (L ’angelo nero 73). 


3. Guilt, trials, and confessions 

We know that literature is nourished by literature. For Tabucchi’s generation, 
those European authors, especially French, who published between the 1920s 
and the 1950s had a determinant impact. They placed at the center of their 
narratives a question that used to be called with some pomposity “the human 
condition.” Their characters spoke in striking ways of their sense of 
disconnectedness and, paradoxically, their feeling of responsibility, that followed 
the discovery of human freedom. They made no concessions to old rhetorical 
patterns, favoring a style that was spare, direct, and usually paratactical. Also, 
writers like Kafka, Sartre, and Camus privileged an image that kept occurring 
obsessively in their writing, in order to represent the conflict that took place 
inside the human conscience, the discomfort of choices to be made, and the need 
for an arena where such issues could be debated. That recurring image was the 
courtroom. At times, the evaluation of multiple truths took place during a trial; at 
other times, the protagonist vainly sought those who were supposed to judge 
him; and confessions to visible or invisible listeners acquired the tones of 


> Lévinas, Foucault, and Derrida are three major voices in the ongoing dialogue on ethics 
in today’s world. See also Zygmunt Bauman’s works on this topic. 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 157 


depositions. 

The judicial metaphor served many purposes: to foreground the inevitability 
of ethical choices; to reflect on the definition of human and civic responsibility; 
to place face to face different interpretations of the concept of justice; to explore 
the meanings given to words referring to human connections; and finally to 
analyze and perhaps exorcise the widening pessimism engulphing the twentieth 
century. In those tribunals, the just and the unjust could sit on either side of the 
courtroom. Innocence and guilt were equally inadequate to define the accused, 
who did not always know what the accusations were, but carried the burden of a 
guilt they felt was at the same time buried away and inescapable. The experience 
all human beings shared was the basis for a concept of solidarity that was 
supposed to overcome the disconnected presence of individuals in the world. No 
metaphysical dimension offered any hope of transcendence, all gods having been 
undone as much (one must assume) by the collapse of belief as by the worldly 
entanglements of all religious confessions. 

Since intertextuality weaves together many threads, the memory of other 
stories about justice and courtrooms is ever present in the fiction of Tabucchi. | 
think of Carlo Collodi, an Italian writer who is the epitome of fantastic realism 
and who is finally being rescued from the narrow confines of so-called children’s 
literature. In Tabucchi, there is a “dream of a dream” (Sogni di sogni 47-49) that 
is a homage to Collodi.’ Much earlier, in a novel crammed full of events, 
Tabucchi had resurrected some Collodian animal characters in a scene of justice 
perverted (// piccolo naviglio 175-83). Looking at the judge presiding at the 
trial, “Capitano Sesto capi senza possibilita di errore che esso era il gorilla di 
Pinocchio della sua infanzia” (175). Tabucchi’s fiction orchestrates with very 
different instruments the “primal” experience of the marionnette Pinocchio, that 
moment when human beings have for the first time the revelation of injustice, 
accompanied by a frustrated hunger for its opposite. Just as in Collodi, there 
lives in Tabucchi the culture of a harsh yet much loved Tuscany 
(“un’immaginaria maremma” L’angelo nero 9), irreverent, ironical, bitter, and 
ready to act, although without illusions about action’s outcome. Again, it is 
language that brings to light the writer’s points of reference and choices: Piazza 
d'Italia and I! piccolo naviglio are totally immersed in the Tuscan tradition of 
storytelling. But even later, Collodi’s presence returns either in characteristic 
expressions, or in magically evoked images (‘“lorsignori,” Reguiem 45 and 120, 
and passim; Damasceno Monteiro 211). 


4. The angel with black wings 
L’angelo nero appeared in 1991, and Requiem, in its original Portuguese 


* The biographical note at the end of the volume is clearly by Tabucchi, as the final 
sentences reveal: “Fu perseguitato dai reumatismi, dalle manie e dall’insonnia. Dette vita 
immortale a un burattino di legno” (Sogni di sogni 82). 


158 Angela M. Jeannet 


version, in the same year; Sogni di sogni in 1992, Sostiene Pereira in 1994, and 
La testa perduta di Damasceno Monteiro (which | will henceforth indicate as 
Damasceno Monteiro) in 1997. They follow one another very closely, revisiting 
history’s nightmares that Tabucchi had first evoked in Piazza d'Italia’ and II 
piccolo naviglio. Each book returns to fundamental thematics: the uninterrupted 
exchange between past and present, the crushing power of violence, resistance to 
it, and the experience of injustice. Other themes also emerge in Tabucchi’s later 
works to complicate the picture: a sense of guilt for one’s actions; an even 
greater remorse for omissions big and small; the temptation to yield to a desire 
for quiet living; and the creeping disillusionment that counsels inaction. As his 
thematics deepens, a shift occurs in his writing approach: from the thick 
amassing of images, sensations, and folk storytelling techniques, which are 
characteristic of the early novels, to a spare and rarefied atmosphere where few 
characters, at times only two, engage in a dialogue. 

A short story entitled “I] battere d’ali di una farfalla a New York pud 
provocare un tifone a Pechino?” (L’angelo nero 71-90) first proposes a scene 
where a grey-haired character facing an unnamed interrogator unwillingly makes 
a deposition. The inquisitor demands a confession couched in bureaucratic 
language, “io sottoscritto, cognome e nome...” (73), and uses the first person 
plural to refer to himself; whether to suggest a plurality of authoritarian figures, 
or to employ a pluralis maiestatis to stress his own power is never made clear. 
The grey-haired man is trying to evade the questioning, but the interrogator is 
very direct: “[...] la parte dello sfacciato impudente, ma sommerso dal rimorso, 
deve farla con i giudici; con noi deve essere umile, anzi, umilissimo” (74). Little 
by little, prodded by the questioner, who seems to know already every move and 
action the accused made many years before, the grey-haired man begins to 
provide details of an assassination in which he had participated “per la giusta 
causa” (78). But it soon becomes evident that the questioning is a ruse. The 
accuser is simply interested in the other man’s acknowledgement of his own 
responsibilities, for the questioner is keenly aware that the story has been 
festering for years inside the other man, in a remote corner of his memory. 
Suddenly, there is a shift in the dialogue, and the grey-haired man becomes 
anxious to tell someone what had happened, for the first time in his life (82). The 
narration that follows places the accused in the role of a witness, perhaps an 
informant, and the accuser in the role of a defense attorney, who suggests the 
appropriate behavior: “[...] meno freddezza, pit: passione. E lacrime. Insomma, 
il tormento. La nostra € una faccenda fondata sul pentimento, non se lo 
dimentichi” (85). Beyond the cat-and-mouse game and the allusions to the 
double hypocrisy of accused and accuser, there is no closure to the story. The 
ending is rather banal, with the inquisitor, a gentleman improbably dressed in a 


* “So che questo libro é le mie radici, di uomo e di scrittore. Tutto torna 0 niente torna” 
(Piazza d'Italia , 2nd edition, 7). 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 159 


sky-blue suit, suggesting that his code-name may be Dr. Conscience (90). By 
asking the question that the title of the story forms, and making reference to the 
mathematical concepts of fractals and catastrophe theory, the interrogator coldly 
and logically presents individual responsibility as one facet of universal 
interdependence. Action has a value because it causes unforeseeable, but no less 
real, repercussions. We are far from the dramatic, earnest tones used in the 
reconstructions of middle- and low-bourgeois milieus to which earlier writers 
had accustomed us. Tabucchi’s voice has an ironic tone; he favors dream-like 
situations, ghostly confrontations, and at times a melancholy playfulness made 
pungent by sarcastic allusions. 

The short story that follows in the same collection, “La trota che guizza fra 
le pietre mi ricorda la tua vita,” looks with greater specificity to the relationship 
between literature (poetry, to be precise) and the world. The volume that 
contains the story has a preface ending in a declaration at the same time 
redundant and ambiguous: “[...] il titolo di questo libro — the preface says — 
appartiene a Eugenio Montale, che prima di me si é imbattuto in un angelo con 
le ali nere. E un titolo che vuol essere un omaggio” (10). It is no mystery that the 
presence of Montale’s voice is pervasive in much Italian writing between the 
1930s and the 1960s. Even in Tabucchi’s earlier novels we find allusions and 
outright quotations. Just to cite the most obvious, in // piccolo naviglio there is a 
Montalean epigraph and a madrigal that is an ironic paraphrase of a well known 
poem by Montale (167). But the story in question does something that is more 
pointed and elusive than inserting a quotation: it “rereads” a certain Montalean 
persona whose impact on other writers was determinant at a time when they felt 
uncertain about what place writing and storytelling occupied in the world 
(Jeannet, ch. 1, “Literature and Doubt”). The poetic voice — faced with the 
public’s incomprehension and the crude attempts at being coopted by totalitarian 
regimes — hesitated between a refusal of contact with the “real” (almost a fear 
of contagion), a retreat into Hermetic poetry, and a disillusioned meditation on 
human affairs. Tabucchi’s character in the short story is a great poet, who is 
caught between the memories of what he once was and a cantankerous old age. 
He relishes flattery, particularly coming from young women, and responds to it 
by acting the part of the wise old poet. At the same time, he is condescending 
and impatient with his admirers, and mulls cynical thoughts. “La poesia é 
menzogna... tutta la scrittura ¢ un peccato contro se stessi” (L 'angelo nero 103). 
His disillusionment is such that he plots a scheme to have some of his latter bits 
of poetry published in small doses posthumously, to provide a young admirer 
with a profit and to show spite toward all critics. The references to Montale’s 
own life are quite plain, although it is debatable how much the poet might have 
been flattered by them. 

Requiem returns with greater urgency to the same theme, the connections 
between literature, world events, and retreat from involvement. A note at the 
beginning of the volume tells the reader what choices the writer made, choices 


160 Angela M. Jeannet 


that would have pleased Montale: “[...] ho preferito suonare la mia musica non 
con un organo, che é@ uno strumento proprio delle cattedrali, ma con 
un’armonica, che si puo tenere in tasca, 0 con un organetto, che si puo portare 
per strada” (8; my emph.). The ghost of a Portuguese writer, who accompanies 
the novel’s protagonist and narrator to a small Lisbon restaurant famous for local 
specialties, is quite frank and explicit about his former attitudes. He had been 
“della razza che rimane a terra,” to quote Montale again. And the narrator 
rejoins: “comodo... davvero comodo” (121). Unfazed, his interlocutor replies: 


“Eh, si [...] forse sono stato un po’ vigliacco, mi capisce?, ma lasci che le dica che 
proprio dalla vigliaccheria sono nate le pagine piu coraggiose del nostro secolo, pensi per 
esempio a quel cecoslovacco che scriveva in tedesco,... che coraggio aver scritto quel 
libro meraviglioso, sa? quel libro sulla colpa. // processo?, domandai, dev’essere // 
processo. Si, certo, disse lui, é il libro pit coraggioso del nostro secolo, ha il coraggio di 
affermare che tutti siamo colpevoli. Colpevoli di che?, domandai. Come di che?, disse 
lui, di essere nati, forse, e delle cose che sono successe in seguito, siamo tutti colpevoli.” 

(121-22) 


But spreading the blame is not helpful, and looking for noble alibis not 
convincing: the ghosts of history past are not so easily exorcised. Many ghosts 
return in Tabucchi’s works to fill the narrators’ nights and stifling afternoons, 
and sometimes they bring with them their own dreams (Sogni di sogni). It is as if 
a bothersome itch, a remorse, a disquiet persecuted them, even beyond the grave. 
We are told that the dreamer is the one who calls back the shadows of the dead 
(Requiem 63), so we must pay attention to which ghosts Tabucchi’s narrators do 
recall. Here comes a Majakovski who dreams of the moment when he betrayed 
poetry, a Goya whose brush fights with the horrors of human ferocity and 
despair, and a Lorca who dreams of his own assassination. They are the ghosts 
the European conscience has not put to rest. And we know that, while he was 
writing those short pieces out of a tangle of memories and dreams, Tabucchi was 
elaborating a more complex narrative, which he entitled Sostiene Pereira. 

Bruno Ferraro, in his 1995 article, analyzes thoroughly the tendentious 
polemics that swirled around the novel. Once we set aside the crudely political 
issues that occupied many critics, and even the explicitly developed thematics, 
what intrigues us is a rhetorical device: the repetition of a single verbal form 
echoing from the title on throughout the entire narration: “sostiene.” A 
disembodied voice reports the statements made by a character, the journalist 
Pereira, providing at the same time the distancing of a third-person narration and 
the testimony of an eyewitness who is privy to all the actions and emotions of the 
protagonist. The hidden metaphor is that of an inquisitorial space, where 
everything is supposedly already known, but must be brought out of the lips of 
the accused bit by bit, word by word, sentence by sentence, in self-accusatory 
fashion, through all sorts of doubts, repetitions, reticences, and re-evocations. 
The journalist’s character is a resurrection and recasting of the Montalean type 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 161 


of literary man. He is middle-aged, physically on the decline, and a lover of 
literature who aspires to remain aloof from history, particularly his country’s 
disastrous political events, even though to do so makes him feel uneasy. The 
novel is the story of Pereira’s slow and painful coming to awareness in the form 
of a deposition/confession. Pereira “sostiene,” that is to say “asserts” his truth, 
and feels impelled to provide full and detailed support for the statements 
referring to each moment in his recent existence. Tabucchi chooses to set aside 
his familiar surrealistic techniques in this novel, and he even provides an ironic 
and dramatic closure to the protagonist’s existence. This choice is a significant 
departure from his earlier style; it is a shift that parallels the traumatic 
transformation taking place in the protagonist’s conscience. Pereira could have 
gone on tolerating the small and large humiliations imposed by the authoritarian 
regime ruling his country. He is a solitary and skeptical man, with many 
memories and no hopes. But the turning point comes for him when he sees and 
hears a human body being massacred. When he must face the corporeal evidence 
of tyranny, only then does a sense of outrage awaken, and he is “forced” to rebel 
and take action. Tabucchi’s writing chooses to adhere to an_ individual 
experience that is so sudden, intense, and basic as to require the use of the 
witnessing mode and the simplest, most repetitious constructions. In Sartre’s and 
Camus’s fictions the focus of narration was Man, Sisyphus, or the Stranger, 
those mythical entities that were supposed to indicate the universality of 
experience; for Lévinas the Other is the Face, a philosophical construct; for 
Tabucchi’s narrator the Other is the Body, made of far from abstract flesh, with 
its miseries, suffering, and resistance. Pereira’s fault was one of omission: he had 
stubbornly avoided looking outside and inside himself for as long as he had been 
able to, in part under the pretext of philosophical aloofness and largely to protect 
a melancholy but quiet living. His confession/testimony must therefore be total, 
specific, unassailable, cruel and almost bureaucratic in its exactness. The 
innumerable ‘“‘sostiene” that punctuate the story express astonishment in their 
repetitiveness: astonishment at the accumulating evidence of cowardice, 
obtuseness, and self-deception; and astonishment at witnessing Pereira’s own 
sudden transformation, which he himself had not expected and for which he was 
not perhaps prepared. 

Similar in this aspect to other characters in Tabucchi’s fiction, Pereira is a 
peculiar journalist, lost in nostalgia for the myths of the past — France, Paris, a 
journalism of elite and romantically adventurous — and he is often visited by 
dreams and evocations of personal memories. In the end, he uses precisely the 
written language he was accustomed to manipulating and refining self- 
complacently, in order to play a dangerous stratagem; by denouncing political 
violence and injustice, he revises his attitude toward life and his relationship to 
writing. Language is of the essence here. The sequence of events is recorded by a 
self that is other from oneself, as someone had said earlier in a tale full of 
mysterious happenings: “tu sei un altro” (Notturno indiano 68). The words 


162 Angela M. Jeannet 


reported in Sostiene Pereira come from an observer who is as knowledgeable of 
the other as of his own conscience, as merciless as an inquisitor, and who is as 
invisible as a ghost. In the same manner, when a penitent judge in an Amsterdam 
café — in Camus’s La chute — confessed to an anonymous customer his failure 
to become involved in another person’s tragedy, the silent listener was a 
shadowy presence. The eye that sees what is within us is our own, and the 
witnessing takes place before our double, for there are no judging or punishing 
gods facing either Jean-Baptiste Clamence or the journalist Pereira.° We were 
told implicitly the reasons for such loneliness by the atheist physician in 
Notturno indiano (23-32), who is in turn a brother to the physician of La peste. 
If there is a zone undermined by doubt, or something discredited and mute in the 
face of human joys and suffering, that is preciseiy the universe of metaphysics. 


5. Returns 

The verba! form “‘sostiene,” which is a fundamental element in Sostiene Pereira, 
is not the only repetitive tactic used by the writer through the years, to construct 
his fictional universe. As if retracing its steps and deepening its exploration, 
Tabucchi’s fiction is an endless circling around the fundamental issues facing its 
own narrative and its readers, by means of an endless recalling of distinctive 
elements. 

Proper names reappear like clues, at times with only minimal changes. We 
first find Los Hermanos Montero in Piazza d'Italia (69), then echoed by 
Monteiro Rossi, the assassinated young man in Sostiene Pereira, and then again 
twice José Monteiro de Alameda (Damasceno Monteiro 52) and the decapitated 
Damasceno Monteiro. Specific images and metaphors also become narrative 
constants. Obsessively, each of Tabucchi’s stories is haunted by a body bruised, 
maimed, wounded to death, or mutilated, a body to which all events lead, and 
from which other events inexorably derive. Also, obsessively, as each story 
unfolds, a blinding sunlight and an oppressive heat pervade the scene. In the 
short story from L’angelo nero mentioned earlier, the profuse sweating of 
inquisitor and accused is a leit-motiv in the narration (73-76, 79). Heat torments 
the anguished characters and drenches them in sweat in Requiem (13, 16, 19, 23, 
26, 28, 31, and so on). In Sostiene Pereira “Il sole era implacabile” (88). “Il 
cielo era di un azzurro feroce” in Damasceno Monteiro (48, 123). This repetitive 
pattern is perhaps a rhetorical device less noticeable than others because of its 
apparent realistic touch, since the stories are located in Mediterranean countries 
under the summer sun. And yet, unbearable heat and light are clearly also 
memory and symbol in this fictional universe; they are traces connecting it to 
other narrations. The Sartrean hell where only late confessions could be 


° Bertone suggests that there is indeed an external listener, perhaps an analyst. However, 
Tabucchi’s characters usually seem to be rather skeptical about outside explorers of their 
psyches. 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 163 


rendered, was stifling to its inhabitants because of inescapable heat; Camus’s 
Meursault attributed his crime to the blinding light of the Algerian summer; and 
an oppressive heat characterized the atmosphere of the courtroom in which he 
sat, accused of murder. The matrix of Tabucchi’s tales is the same that nourished 
the last century of reflection on issues of violence, guilt, justice and injustice. 


6. Investing the Other with value 

What is left out of a story is as significant as what is included. A shift from the 
inclusion to the exclusion of a lexical element provides a clue to a changed focus 
in Tabucchi’s fiction. Negative expressions including the word “importanza” 
appeared often in his early works; even the title of a collection of short stories 
displayed it: Piccoli equivoci senza importanza (1985). Although at times, as in 
this title, it was used ironically, that expression and others from the same 
semantic field contributed to the construction of characters who had become 
listless or indifferent because of what they perceived as the intractable quality of 
the real. The boundaries between their waking and their dream worlds were 
fluid, and events took place in a frustrating flow that led nowhere; their world 
was enveloped in a sort of persistent fog, or rather, in the hallucination-inducing 
haze of a perpetual summer. Already in Notturno indiano expressions such as “‘it 
doesn’t matter” appeared a few times, but they increase in frequency in Piccoli 
equivoci senza importanza (10, 14, 81, 92, 117, 127), and become an essential 
component in // filo dell’orizzonte. \n the latter novel, the protagonist’s interest 
in a young man, who apparently was assassinated in an obscure episode of 
political violence and whose body remains unclaimed in the morgue, meets with 
surprise from those around him: “perché t’interessa?” (40; also 46, 69). The 
protagonist is told that the dead young man was a stranger, somebody who did 
not matter: “é uno sconosciuto... non conta niente nella tua vita” (80). Even the 
protagonist’s musings keep returning to the same statement: “quel morto... non 
importava a nessuno” (35; also, again 35, and 79). But these negative phrases 
and sentences, precisely because they echo everybody else’s point of view, 
indicate a discomfort and a lack of conviction, ultimately paralleling the 
protagonist’s increasing involvement in trying to solve the mystery of that death. 
Significantly, the dead man’s name is at first heard as “Carlo Nobodi” rather 
than as “Carlo Noboldi,” its correct form. In fact, a casual statement made by the 
protagonist’s woman-friend, and his insistence on returning to view and observe 
the lonely corpse, begin to undermine the protagonist’s and our own certainty 
about the boundaries of the two men’s identities. “Ma chi é lui per te?” (80) 
becomes an important question. “Potresti essere tu” — said the woman-friend 
when the body was described to her—and the remark was offered casually, 
“come se fosse un’osservazione senza importanza” (32). Yet, it may really lead 
to such fundamental questions as: Who is a stranger for us, what connection is 
there between ourselves and the Other? These questions may lead to another, 
especially essential question: “E tu... tu chi sei per te?” (80). The young man is 


164 Angela M. Jeannet 


dead, while the older man, the protagonist of the story, is alive (but why?), 
although he lives in a world “sbiadito” (13), “stanco” (37), di “cose trapassate” 
(13), where only the “archeologia di un passato prossimo” (14) barely survives. 
That “unimportant” thing, one man’s mysterious death by violence, and the 
protagonist’s life, an existence “senza importanza” surrounded by the signs of a 
violent past, become so intertwined that the story closes on the protagonist’s own 
mysterious disappearance. 

What may be important and what may be “senza importanza” is the core 
issue in Damasceno Monteiro, where the shift to a different plane is marked by 
the disappearance of the entire semantic family that distinguished the earlier 
novel. The techniques used in // filo dell’orizzonte owe much to the 
whodunit/mystery genre, even though Tabucchi insisted on foregrounding the 
ethical question that shapes the events in his character’s life. In Damasceno 
Monteiro, even the para-textual elements suggest a reading that must take into 
account a very specific perspective. The reproduction on the front cover shows a 
detail taken from the many studies Picasso drew for Guernica, as everyone easily 
recognizes. The volume’s epigraph is a quotation that includes a word play 
between “essere” and “esistere” that leads to a reflection on our entire culture’s 
value system: “Come puo esistere... un essere che nell’esistere mette un cosi 
grande annullamento dell’esistenza?” Tabucchi weaves his story around that 
contradiction. 

Damasceno Monteiro is Tabucchi’s most complex novel to date, where the 
role of protagonist keeps shifting, or perhaps divides, between several 
characters. The first character is a young reporter, rather self-satisfied and 
timidly full of himself. Although he likes to sport a blasé attitude, he harbors a 
naive fascination with the myth of France, and is enthusiastic about politically 
committed literature. But when he is in the presence of two strong 
personalities—Dona Rosa, hotel owner extraordinaire, and Fernando Diogo 
Maria de Jesus de Mello Sequeira, a big Oporto lawyer—he becomes an eager 
schoolboy. The adjectives that accompany and complement his gestures and 
actions are explicit: he is “umile,” “interdetto,” “perplesso,” and “imbarazzato” 
to the point of blushing (149). His name is given simply as “Firmino.” 

The implicit narrator is obviously older and more settled than the reporter. 
He is gently ironical when he observes Firmino, who aspires to the life of a 
scholar, and is all absorbed into questions that are by now “dead and gone” 
(“‘trapassate” in the novel) such as Vittorini’s writings. Firmino’s study of the 
works of the Italian writer is presented by the narrator as a generous but useless 
enterprise; and yet it may be so only apparently, since that literature has an 
important part in the young man’s formation as he moves toward what one could 
call a new awareness of the world. The narrator is very close to the young 
journalist, he can intepret his feelings, experience his sensations (22, 32, 51), and 
understand his naivete. 

But the truly new character in this novel is Don Fernando, who soon 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 165 


occupies the center of the narrative. The aura of mythical American films, where 
gruff lawyers fight courageously for justice, surrounds him, as his nickname 
proves: everybody knows him as “Loton,” a transliteration of Laughton, that is 
Charles Laughton. He, like Pereira, is obese and tormented by the hellish heat of 
the climate; he, too, like several characters in Requiem, loves authentic 
Portuguese cooking, rather coarse but tasty. But above all he nourishes a deep 
passion for justice, and insists on practicing his profession precisely in the 
tribunals of a country marked by long years of dictatorship. His other passion is 
literature, more specifically poetry. His peremptory statements about writers and 
politics, justice and the law, are far from conventional, and intimidate the young 
journalist. Curtly undercutting any attempt to define people and events by 
conventional measures, he makes sure to pre-empt the inevitable clichés: “Non 
pensi di trovarsi in una situazione kafkiana,” he says brusquely to Firmino (110) 
when the latter appears perplexed by the seemingly incongruous leaps in the 
lawyer’s conversation. Don Fernando views the fact that he is the defensor of 
those who suffer violence as “una specie di correzione tardiva della Storia” 
(111), since he is the descendant of a family that once held power over other 
human beings. He has studied “le ragioni che hanno indotto gli uomini a 
elaborare i codici [...] le ragioni che vi erano sottese, in senso magari astratto” 
(113), and has discovered the existence of that basic norm from which the 
arrogance of power descends, which offers all the alibis, and ultimately provides 
might with legitimacy, claiming for it the investiture from a divine source. 
Having become fascinated with the theoretical dimension of the law and its 
relationship to power, he has planned to devote his life to writing about it. Yet, 
he has also realized that the edifice of the Law is “un incerto edificio” (169), 
under whose weight the poor and oppressed get crushed. So, he has decided after 
all to practice law, although he is not sure of where the greater usefulness might 
be. He was certain of one thing, that “é piu umile [...] andare in tribunale a 
difendere coloro che subiscono [la tortura]” (178). There seems to be a paradox, 
here, as the novel, precisely by using writing, chooses to pursue the path Don 
Fernando abandoned, and indulges in a reflection on justice and commitment. 
What could be the use of that? But, as Don Fernando asserts, “in letteratura tutto 
c’entra con tutto” (129). 

We never hear the entire statement that Don Fernando pronounces in the 
courtroom for the prosecution of members of the police, who are drug dealers 
and also Damasceno Monteiro’s torturers and assassins. Significantly, the 
recording made by the young journalist on a defective tape-recorder preserves 
only fragments of it. On the one hand, the mishap underlines the reporter’s 
imperfect comprehension of the lawyer’s thought; on the other hand, those 
fragments point to the difficulty of articulating a coherent discourse about justice 
and opening up a higher vision of it, in the face of the actual state of human 
affairs. The three fragments separated by dots that remain from the opening of 
the lawyer’s address point to the bases of a belief system that invests the Other 


166 Angela M. Jeannet 


with value.’ Now, more clearly than in Tabucchi’s earlier texts, the characters’ 
concern is for the living: it is “being against death,” a crucial theme for much of 
the twentieth-century fiction that called the world “‘absurd.” The first fragment is 
a question: “cosa significa essere contro la morte?” (213). The second one states: 
“ogni uomo € assolutamente indispensabile a tutti gli altri e tutti sono 
assolutamente indispensabili a ciascuno.... ciascun uomo é radice dell’essere 
umano” (213). In a world devoid of meaning, all certainties have gone, except 
one, the value of human solidarity. Thus the lawyer Loton seems to answer the 
character who, in // filo dell’orizzonte, had asked the protagonist Spino why he 
insisted on trying to uncover the identity of a man who was a victim of 
assassination: “Ma chi é lui per te?.... € uno sconosciuto, non conta niente nella 
tua vita” (80). The third incomplete sentence reads, in a Camusian vein: “é per 
l’uomo essere contro la morte, ma poiché I’uomo non ha esperienza della propria 
morte ma solo della morte altrui, dalla quale solo per riflesso puo immaginare e 
temere la propria...” (214). That is also a statement that parallels the words of 
Lévinas: “[...] éthique surgit dans le rapport a autrui, et non pas, d’emblée, par 
une référence a |’universalité d’une loi. [...] Dés lors, importance du rapport a 
autre homme comme incomparable, comme dépouillé de tout ‘réle’ social et 
qui, ainsi, dans sa nudité—son dénuement, sa mortalité—s’impose d’emblée a 
ma responsabilité” (Aeschlimann 9). But Lévinas’s conclusion of that quotation 
is based on a trascendence that is not satisfied with a secular solidarity, and goes 
on to define responsibility as “bonté, miséricorde ou charité” (9). 

The last four pages of the novel confirm and make more explicit Don 
Fernando’s function as the heir of a European tradition that painfully attempted 
to address the issues of oppression, injustice, and human solidarity. Through the 
lawyer’s words the reader meets indirectly an unexpected eyewitness to 
Damasceno Monteiro’s torture and beheading; the person’s name may be 
Eleuterio, or Wanda, “un travestito, [...] schedato per prostituzione” (238). Don 
Fernando had already explained to Firmino that he felt himself to be part of the 
same humanity he defended: “Io difendo gli sciagurati perché sono come loro 
[...] come i disgraziati che difendo credo di aver conosciuto le miserie della vita, 
di averle capite e anche assunte” (126). When the journalist expresses doubts as 
to the reliability and credibility of such an eyewitness (““Figuriamoci’’—he says), 
Don Fernando responds with a gesture and few words: “Punto l’indice contro il 
soffitto, come se si rivolgesse all’aria, poi lo punto verso Firmino, e poi lo 
diresse verso il suo petto. — E una persona, disse, si ricordi questo, giovanotto, 
prima di tutto é una persona” (238). And indeed, Wanda’s very existence and 
courage is going to make it possible to hear a voice in a court of justice testifying 
to the violence that was perpetrated against another human being. 


’ According to a note Tabucchi placed at the end of the volume, they are the words of a 
philosopher, Mario Rossi. 


A Matter of Injustice: Violence and Death in Antonio Tabucchi 167 


7. Conclusion: le “pari de Pascal” and “il gioco del rovescio” 

Brizio states that in Tabucchi’s universe “non é possibile conoscere la realta e 
arrivare alla verita” (115), but that may be only partially accurate. The “truth” in 
Tabucchi’s universe has to do with the rejection of the dualism supported by 
Thomas Mann’s conclusion: “In the final analysis, there are only two basic 
attitudes, two points of view: the aesthetic and the moral” (Booth 382). Truth is 
not selecting a partial vision, or choosing the frame of a small window to look on 
the world; as the protagonist said in Notturno indiano when he was shown a 
cropped photo, “pas de morceaux choisis”; truth lies in seeing the whole picture, 
all the joy and horror of the world (101-02), and giving it a voice. 

As a matter of fact, reversing Pascal’s bet in a sort of “gioco del rovescio,” 
Tabucchi’s characters repeat the question that was asked more than once: What 
if there were no God? The absence of all certainties leaves open the possibility 
of human solidarity, whose roots may be too remote to discover, but which may 
become the founding factor in the struggle against injustice and death. Each 
person matters — Tabucchi’s protagonists say — but only if in the Other’s body 
I can see my own body, its misery and its death. In his introduction to a 
collection of essays (3-25), Ferraro spoke of Pirandello’s presence in Tabucchi’s 
work, since both authors explored the themes of the plurality of selves, the 
appearance of the double, and the problematical relationship of art to life. | 
would add that for Tabucchi, as for Pirandello, suffering is what allows the self 
to become aware of its own existence and humanity. 

Tabucchi’s writing is rich in voices, languages, and silences; his pages are 
bright, playful, and rich in irony, sometimes also dark, quietly dramatic, and 
broken by ellipses. His characters are caught in a net of official lies, and 
terrorized by the arrogance of power. They display indifference, cowardice, or 
courage, while others engage in the struggle against a culture of injustice and 
death. His writing explores the relationship between itself and human existence, 
thereby subscribing to a form of engagement; it does so not by providing 
answers, but by asking the questions that matter, by its passion for the corporeal 
presence of language, and by a pervasive curiosity about all facets of our 
existence. “Ma la scrittura pud salvare da un’umiliazione incancellabile?” (222). 
The question that Don Fernando poses, the terrible question human beings have 
too often asked, remains without an answer. After several more fragments of his 
courtroom speech, one word stands alone on the recording: “... Pinfamia...” 
(224), which is the name of what is constantly present in history. Yet, there are 
witnesses to injustice. It is the voice of Don Fernando that brings out the details 
of the story of Damasceno Monteiro, a story told in spite of the incompleteness 
of the document that speaks of it, and the uncertain fate of the case’s judgement. 
It is the voice of Wanda, a casualty in society’s warfares, that will testify in 
court. It is by the pen of a writer named Antonio Tabucchi that the few lines of a 
report printed in an obscure newspaper in some European country, and a few 
personal memories, have metamorphosed into the “truth” of fiction. And what is 


168 Angela M. Jeannet 


a writer if not the voice of uncelebrated history? There was a character in 
Requiem who was a failed author by all the measures of literary success, but who 
had become a “‘story-seller” (Requiem 112). The one who tells stories—that 
novel tells us—is a magician (Requiem 125) because, thanks to his voice, death 
is never total (Requiem 109-11). To cite Joseph Francese: “The act of writing 
[...] is for Tabucchi a bridge between individuals and between past, present and 
future [...] for strengthening social bonds” (Annali d’italianistica 194-95); or to 
cite Tabucchi himself: “La letteratura é testimonianza della memoria” (166). 
Here lies the ethical dimension of Tabucchi’s written world. 

Franklin and Marshall College
