Giancarlo Lombardi 





Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions 
of Accountability in the Cinema of the stragi di stato 


Brescia, Piazza della Loggia, May 17, 1974. Trade unionist Franco Castrezzari is 
addressing a crowd gathered to protest against neo-fascist resurgence when a bomb 
explodes, disseminating fear and destruction: 8 people die, 103 are injured. From his 
podium, Castrezzari witnesses the arrival of the police and of carabinieri who 
immediately begin to wash the blood out of the pavement. Along with the blood, most 
precious traces that would have helped the investigation disappear forever. 


The Tyrrhenian Sea, in that stretch of water that separates Ponza from Ustica, June 27, 
1980. Itavia DC-9 I-TIGI, departed from Bologna and headed to Palermo, suddenly 
disappears from the radar, plunging into the water for a mysterious reason: the bodies of 
the 81 victims are slowly recovered. Some of them come afloat, surfacing on the sea, 
many others are trapped in what remains of the plane, held prisoner at the very bottom of 
the Tyrrhenian waters. Many years later, a French company takes charge of the recovery 
of the Itavia plane, yet not much is gained from the black box. What the public does not 
know, however, is that this French company, based in Marseille, is closely linked to the 
French Secret Services. 


Bologna railway station, August 2, 1980. Just as the summer exodus is about to begin, a 
bomb explodes in an economy-class waiting room crowded by tourists of all ages: 85 
people die, and 200 are seriously injured. A country in shock immediately places the 
blame on Valerio Fioravanti and Francesca Mambro, founders of the “Nuclei Armati 
Rivoluzionari.” The married couple of neo-fascist terrorists is soon apprehended and 
subsequently tried for that and many other crimes. While assuming full responsibility for 
every other terrorist attack, Fioravanti and Mambro strenuously deny their involvement in 
the Bologna massacre. If this were true, then much would remain to be questioned of the 
activity of the Italian Secret Services, responsible for derailing official investigations 
under mysterious circumstances. 


At the intersection of these three tragic events begins my discussion of the 
encounter between ethics and cinema. And while cinema can hardly shed light 
on the darkness surrounding these mysterious episodes of our recent history, it 
may still accomplish a unique task, that of proposing daring interpretations 
through which responsibility is inevitably assigned. Questioning accountability, 
placing blame, and asking viewers to bear testimony, this specific cinema fully 
engages in that ethical discourse rooted, according to Emmanuel Lévinas and 


Annali d'Italianistica 19 (2001) 


286 Giancarlo Lombardi 


other philosophers, in responsibility for and towards the other. ' 


Philosophy and Cinema 

Before I embark on my analytic quest, a methodological reflection is necessary, 
which is prompted by a recent work authored by an Argentinian philosopher, 
Julio Cabrera. In Da Aristotele a Spielberg, Cabrera discusses the use of cinema 
as medium for students’ introduction to philosophy’s most nuanced concepts. In 
consonance with the opinions of pathic thinkers such as Schopenhauer, 
Nietzsche, Kierkegaard, and Heidegger, Cabrera maintains that 


Almeno alcuni aspetti fondamentali del reale (se non proprio il reale nella sua totalita) 
non vanno soltanto enunciati e articolati logicamente per essere pienamente intesi, bensi 
devono esser introdotti sensitivamente grazie alla “comprensione logopatica,” al tempo 
stesso razionale e affettiva; [...] quest’introduzione sensitiva nel discorso deve produrre 
un certo tipo di impatto in chi stabilisce un contatto con essa. E [...] grazie a questa 
introduzione sensitiva “impattante” si raggiungono verita che possono tranquillamente 
esser sostenute a spada tratta in quanto posseggono pretese di validita universale. Non 
rappresentano quindi semplici “impressioni” psicologiche ma esperienze fondamentali 
che sono un tutt’uno con l’umana condizione, cioé con /'umanita in quanto tale, e 
posseggono pertanto una diretta valenza cognitiva. Queste tre caratteristiche tipiche dei 
pensatori patici mi sembra d’altra parte che definiscano anche il linguaggio del cinema, 
visto pero da un punto di vista filosofico. 


(9) 


Because of its ability to have a direct impact on viewers, cinema succeeds 
better than literature at helping viewers to achieve a different level of 
understanding, which encompasses rational and affective realms: 


Cid che invece offre il cinema é una specie di “superpotenziamento” delle possibilita 
concettuali della letteratura, riuscendo infatti ad ampliare notevolmente I’impressione di 
realta e quindi la creazione di quell’esperienza indispensabile allo sviluppo del 
concettimmagine, con conseguente aumento dell’impatto emotivo che lo caratterizza. 
[...] Il cinema rappresenta perd la pienezza di un’esperienza viva, dal momento che 
include in esso la temporalita e il movimento tipici del mondo reale con tutte le difficolta 
annesse, anziché limitarsi a offrire tutti gli ingredienti necessari affinché lo spettatore (0 il 
lettore) li utilizzi per creare l’immagine, che esso offre gia direttamente. 


(15) 


And what is most valid, in the encounter between cinema and philosophy 
staged by Cabrera in his essay, is cinema’s intrinsic ability to manifest the 
problematic nature of philosophical thinking. True cinema, in fact, resists 
conceptual closure, defeating and defying reductionist attacks: 


' Emmanuel Lévinas discusses this issue in most of his works. For a systematic and 
thorough presentation of Lévinas’s thought, see Frangois-David Sebbah. 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 287 


I concettimmagine, per i problemi filosofici sfiorati, offrono soluzioni logicamente 
epistemicamente e moralmente aperte e problematiche (e a volte segnatamente amorali e 
negative, comunque mai definitivamente affermative e rassicuranti); se anche un regista 
si ponesse l’obiettivo di dare una soluzione definitiva, il linguaggio delle immagini vi si 
opporrebbe, e finirebbe per fare il film che non vorrebbe fare. [...] L’immagine 
cinematografica non pud mostrare senza render problematico, destrutturare, ricollocare, 
tirare, distorcere. 

(18; my emph.) 


Three films that tell three versions of the three dramatic episodes described 
at the inception of this essay are the films that | have chosen to analyze in order 
to stage this suggestive encounter between ethics and cinema. Of these films, | 
will question the representation of historical events, teasing out ideological 
discourses informing their way of turning history into story. And by looking at 
their narrative choices, at the /oci in which emphasis is more critically placed, | 
will attempt to identify the perlocutionary effect that these movies are meant to 
have on their audiences. How do these films stage an ethical debate? How do 
they bear witness to three of the most dramatic events in recent Italian history? 
How do they act on their viewers by shaping their experience of the narrated 
event? These questions, and others, will be raised in analyzing // muro di 
gomma (Marco Risi, 1991), Per non dimenticare (Massimo Martelli, 1992), and 
Le mani forti (Franco Bernini, 1996). 


Brescia, May 17, 1974 

A Brescia non si colpi la variegata folla presente in una stazione ferroviaria in un periodo 
di vacanze, né l’eterogenea clientela di una banca, né il microcosmo che spontaneamente 
Si costituisce in una carrozza ferroviaria o nella carlinga di un aereo di linea. In piazza 
della Loggia, all’atto dell’esplosione, era in corso una manifestazione democratica; partiti 
e sindacati avevano riunito nell’agora cittadini per protestare verso il clima di violenza 
eversiva che da tempo avvelenava la vita cittadina e che aveva chiara e indiscussa matrice 


di destra. 
(Pellegrino 102) 


Giovanni Pellegrino, head of the Commissione Stragi that has long been 
investigating the dramatic events of the anni di piombo, maintains that what 
happened in Piazza della Loggia 27 years ago, on May 17, 1974, was quite 
different from the bombing of Piazza Fontana in December 1969, the bombing 
of the Italicus five years later, that of the Bologna railway station in August 
1980, or the mysterious explosion of the Itavia airplane flying over Ustica in 
June of that same year. What differentiated these events from the one that 
occurred in Brescia was the specific targeting of a group of victims, the men and 
women who had chosen to attend a political rally in a square of a city that had 
been devastated by too many acts of violence. Terrorism, we are told, strikes 


288 Giancarlo Lombardi 


when least expected, choosing random targets out of ordinary people: it is called 
terrorism exactly because its exceeding randomness disseminates terror in entire 
populations. This is terrorism as we have come to experience it in the US, with 
the bombing of the Twin Towers and that of Oklahoma City; this is terrorism as 
Italy had come to experience it at the very end of the Sixties, and in the decade 
that followed. Not that the Red Brigades, in choosing specific targets among 
Judges, politicians, journalists, and academics, could be called anything less than 
terrorists. Indeed, they were terrorists albeit of a different type. During the most 
obscure years of its first republic, Italy was indeed ravaged by two terrorisms, 
spurred by two opposing extremist factions that had chosen to wage their civil 
war in rather different fashion. Left-wing terrorism, through the actions of the 
Red Brigades and Prima Linea, disseminated terror through systematic knee- 
cappings, abductions, and homicides, while Right-wing terrorism mostly took a 
different path, spreading fear in the entire country through a series of bombings 
that caused countless victims. 

Much has been written about the strategia della tensione, devised by the 
Secret Services during those years in order to keep the Left away from power.’ 
Most of the Right-wing bombings, in fact, were initially ascribed to anarchist or 
leftist groups so as to scare uncertain voters into casting their ballot in favor of 
the center-right. Much has been written about the influence of NATO and CIA, 
and about the operation Stay Behind, according to which most West-European 
countries were asked to organize military and paramilitary groups that would be 
mobilized should Communists ever come to power.’ Much has indeed been 
written about Gladio, the organization whose existence was finally revealed by 
Giulio Andreotti in 1991, at the same time that Aldo Moro’s last papers were 
found inside a wall in Via Montenevoso.* Those papers, authored by the 
President of the Christian Democrat Party shortly before his death, sketched 
those same revelations proffered by Andreotti.© They talked about a secret 
paramilitary organization that had been active since the fifties, an organization 
whose existence had been known only to the Secret Services and few other 
politicians and whose members identified themselves as gladiators, defenders of 
the Italian nation against the Red scare. The 622 members of Gladio had 
received extensive training on the Sardinian coast. Their training did not differ 
much from that received by other right-wing sympathizers who had formed 
infamous groups such as Ordine Nuovo, Avanguardia Nazionale, the Rosa dei 
Venti, and the Movimento di Azione Rivoluzionaria, which was said to be 
responsible for the bombing of Piazza della Loggia. What was different, 


* See De Lutiis; Fasanella, Sestieri, and Pellegrino. 

* See Sassano; Galli; Gianni and Antonio Cipriani; Faenza and Fini. 

* See Gualtieri; Anzerilli; Bettini; Flamigni. 

* Aldo Moro’s “prison” memoir, along with the letters he wrote in the same period, was 
recently published in // mio sangue ricadra su di voi. 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 289 


however, was that Gladio was created and directed by the Secret Services, which 
only briefly colluded, instead, with these organizations. 

My contribution to this debate, within this particular context, addresses one 
of the many fictional representations of the tragic events that made the anni di 
piombo. In the attempt to identify rhetoric and visual patterns that characterize 
the fictional reading of a historical event, I set out to define the possible 
“vantage points” offered to the reader, or better, the spectator of this particular 
visual text. 

Leaving aside history, therefore, let me now turn to fiction, to the cinematic 
portrayal of the hypothetical solution of the mystery surrounding the bombing of 
Piazza della Loggia, as powerfully depicted by Franco Bernini in Le mani forti. 
Claudia and Dario, the two protagonists, are not just a psychotherapist in 
training and her patient. Claudia is a woman who has been deeply affected by 
terrorism, her sister being one of the eight victims of the bombing in Piazza 
della Loggia, while Dario is the man of the Secret Services who actually planted 
that very bomb in 1974. Thirteen years later, he has come to Claudia in order to 
beg forgiveness. Fear for his own personal safety forces him to assume a 
disguise, that of Tancredi, a journalist who goes into therapy because he cannot 
cope with the massacres recently witnessed in Bosnia. The woman whose 
gruesome death he has witnessed, however, is not Bosnian: her eyes, her clothes, 
and her blue earrings are those of Lorenza, Claudia’s sister. Before long, 
Claudia comes to this realization and to a decision that will affect her life 
forever. Caught in a dramatic spiral of events, she becomes one of the two key 
witnesses in a trial that sees the state prosecuting itself. All this comes to be 
narrated in a most complex fashion, alternating sequences from the actual trial, 
occurred in 1998 (two years after the movie was made) with disconnected 
sequences showing the events that led up to that trial. 

Back to history for a brief moment. In November 1983, talking about the 
recruiting of former soldiers for possible acts of resistance against the victory of 
Communism, an officer of the Secret Services said that such army was needed 
“per non restare alla finestra, ma per intervenire, per sedare la situazione, 
bloccarla e poi eventualmente decidere in merito” (De Lutiis, Storia dei servizi 
segreti 119). The male protagonist of Le mani forti is one of these men, recruited 
during the military service into an organization whose strong Kafkian hierarchy 
constitutes its very power. Early in their sessions, he says to Claudia: “[...] c’é 
una sapienza nella gerarchia, questa sapienza é il vero potere, quello pit. grande 
di tutti, quello che non morira mai, io ho scelto questo tipo di lavoro per motivi 
che vanno aldila di me, dei miei problemi personali”. When this speech begins, 
the viewer has yet to be introduced into Claudia’s office. Dario’s words act as 
soundtrack to a close-up of trees shaken by a powerful storm, the noise of 
thunders being juxtaposed to his own voice. To the storm outside is juxtaposed 
the internal storm that has led Dario to Claudia, into whose office the film cuts 
immediately. As the man, always dressed in black, reiterates the motives that led 


290 Giancarlo Lombardi 


him to join his yet unmentioned “agency,” viewers are treated to both storms 
simultaneously, the trees having receded to the very background of the frame. 
They are waving under the heavy rain seen through a window, whose square 
bars already evoke a sense of imprisonment shared by both characters. Prey to 
his guilt, Dario is further imprisoned by a commitment to an organization he is 
unable to leave. Prey to her past suffering, Claudia is suffocated by a different 
duty, felt by those who survived the victims of random acts of violence only to 
bear constant remembrance of the tragedy. Furthermore, another prison for 
Claudia, in this specific context, is that established by her profession’s ethical 
code, according to which she is bound to keep her patient’s privacy. It is against 
this very code, this very ethics, that Claudia initiates her struggle. 

The trees located outside Claudia’s office were the object of a different 
portrayal, at the core of the very first establishing shot of this film. Le mani forti 
opens, in fact, on a panning crane shot of a park, on a sunny morning like many 
others. Children are playing volleyball and women with strollers are taking their 
newborns for a walk in the park just as the camera slowly pans downward, 
getting closer to a group of parents who are joined by their children. As a 
soundtrack, Dario’s voice tells the slaughter of Piazza della Loggia, and the 
horror construed by his words is powerfully juxtaposed to the joyful images 
captured in the park. Again, with a series of cuts, the camera pans up on the 
trees and into Claudia’s office, framing its windows, and slowly unveiling the 
presence of Dario and Claudia’s bodies. This powerful juxtaposition, mediated 
by the presence of a window, key element in Bernini’s film, can be seen as a 
symbolic re-staging of the scenario for a terrorist attack. Those that have come 
to be remembered as stragi di stato indeed took place most unexpectedly, there 
where Italians of all ages had come together in most natural fashion, to wait for 
a train that would take them on vacation, or to go about their ordinary business 
of the day. What could possibly give a stronger sense of innocence than a shot of 
a park on a sunny morning? On the other hand, what better place could 
conceivably hide the danger lurking in everyday life? That same park, at night, 
eventually becomes the place where the Secret Services lie spying Claudia’s 
every move. From that very park, they begin a chase scene in which they 
attempt to follow her flight towards justice, marked by her crossing of a tunnel 
at the other end of which she finds herself in a jungle of concrete, whose 
overbearing buildings represent the towering presence of that very same justice 
system that has come to try itself in a most Kafkian fashion. 

At the center of such Kafkian creation lies another therapist’s office, that of 
Claudia’s supervisor, the man who eventually betrays her to the Secret Services. 
It is when a distraught Claudia seeks his advice that we first see his office, 
located inside one of the buildings of the University of Milano. As she walks up 
a spiraling staircase, Claudia reaches a room that seems suspended in mid-air. Its 
walls made of transparent glass, the office allows Claudia’s supervisor to gain 
full vision of its surroundings, and total control of the entire student body. 





Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 291 


Virtual reenactment of Jeremy Bentham’s panopticon, theorized by Michel 
Foucault as a primary instrument of discipline and punishment that works most 
effectively because it allows unreciprocated vision, the supervisor’s office is the 
ultimate harbor of the Secret Services. Descending its ominous staircase, 
Claudia sees a series of posters that denounce that very strategy devised by the 
services: “24 anni da Piazza Fontana. Ancora una bomba’”’. 

The very betrayal of Claudia at the hands of a supervisor whom she saw as 
a father figure is a powerful mise-en-abime of a higher treason by the forces of a 
state that betrays its own citizens through the staging of terrorist attacks. 
Dissatisfaction with Italy, with a country that fails its own children, is voiced in 
the film since its inception. The viewers first meet Claudia’s husband in a scene 
where he discusses, with a group of friends, potential reasons to keep living in 
Italy. Through his words, and those of his friends, Italy is portrayed as an 
unfriendly country that needs to be abandoned, “Ma € possibile che non 
riusciamo a trovare un buon motivo per restare a vivere in Italia?” he says to his 
friends. Upon leaving his wife, he and his friends indeed organize a long trip to 
Senegal, which provides a safe repair from the Kafkian landscape that Italy 
appears to offer in this film. The same character also voices his dissatisfaction 
and fear in the scene that leads to his separation from Claudia. When the Secret 
Services barge into Claudia’s house staging a robbery in order to come into 
possession of a set of tapes containing Claudia’s sessions with Dario, her 
husband proclaims his fears for an involvement that could potentially destroy 
both of them. Begging her to dismiss her patient, and thus reenter anonymity, 
Claudia’s husband maintains that “l’unico modo per vivere in Italia € stare a 
guardare alla finestra”. Claudia, however, refuses to stay behind that window, 
that very window which recurs so pervasively throughout the entire film. Fate, at 
the hands of another emissary from the Secret Services, indeed attempts to strike 
at her just as she steps outside another window and into a balcony, that of the 
hotel in which she has agreed to meet Dario in order to talk him into 
collaborating with justice. Dario, not unlike his epic namesake Tancredi, comes 
to the rescue, and eventually agrees to testify at the trial. 

Just as much as Claudia, the director of Le mani forti cannot simply look 
from a window: his film is not just a simple representation, a display of events 
viewed from a distance. Throughout the entire film, viewers are always 
reminded of the presence of the director’s eye that commands their gaze. Fast 
camera movements, witnessed by constant panning from one object to another, 
camera angles that are often high or low, and almost never place the viewer at 
eye level with the actors, shots edited together in defiance of continuity rules, 
alternation of sequences that portray different time periods, linked through a 
subtle red line that could easily at first hide viewing: these are some of the 
elements that exemplify the “writerliness” or better, the “directorliness” of this 
film. The choices of a burning topic and of this specific interpretation of the 
events make Le mani forti even more daring. While adopting cool colors to 


292 Giancarlo Lombardi 


create an atmosphere that would indeed foster the viewers’ critical distance 
necessary for processing such political statements on the recent past, Bernini 
indeed seems to tell his viewers that, in making this film, he has made a moral 
choice, and like his female protagonist, he has chosen not to stay behind a 
window. Will viewers partake of his experience and cross the same critical 
threshold? 


Ustica, June 27, 1980 
La sera del 27 giugno — mentre proseguiva il ponte aereo americano per il Cairo, mentre 
al confine egiziano-libico erano ammassate le Forze armate dei due schieramenti in stato 
di allerta, mentre era in corso un’esercitazione aerea nelle acque del Tirreno con la 
presenza di una portaerei britannica, mentre sembrava fosse in corso anche 
un’esercitazione di forze francesi, mentre era in programma un volo Tripoli-Varsavia 
con a bordo il colonnello Gheddafi — alle ore 21 |’aereo civile “DC9” dell’ Itavia, in volo 
da Bologna a Palermo con a bordo 81 persone, venne colpito da un missile. L’aereo 
precipito in mare tra le isole di Ponza e Ustica. Due ore dopo la caduta del DC9 
dell’Itavia e la morte di 81 persone, il Sios marina e i vertici dell’Aeronautica militare 
erano gia all’opera per depistare e occultare cause e responsabilita della strage. 

(Flamigni, / fantasmi del passato 145) 


The evocative words that paint such a complex background to the Itavia 
crash belong to Sergio Flamigni, former Left Democrat Senator and member of 
the parliamentary commission investigating Aldo Moro’s death. Caught in the 
midst of an undeclared war between Libya and NATO destined to remain secret, 
the Itavia I-TIGI flight met its demise in a very mysterious fashion. Soon after 
the tragic accident occurred, radar transcripts and other documents bearing 
testimony to the event suddenly disappeared, were burned, locked away, or 
misfiled. The Italian Air Force immediately ascribed the accident to the poor 
functioning of the airplane, attempting to stave off those persistent rumors 
according to which the DC-9 had indeed been destroyed by a missile or a bomb. 
Structural decay of the Itavia plane was ruled out shortly thereafter, and the 
possibilities of internal bombing or external missile attack were juxtaposed to 
yet another theory, according to which the plane had actually fallen off the sky 
because another plane had hit it. After long investigations, this last explanation 
was recently deemed as the most plausible, and several members of the Air 
Force were subsequently indicted. 

The Ustica tragedy deeply affected the country’s perception of its own 
institutions. When it finally became clear that the highest members of the Air 
Force had intentionally withheld crucial information from the public, and it 
became obvious that no respect had been paid to the safety of civilians caught in 
a war that had been kept secret, Italians lost faith in their government, their 
representatives, and in their military force. What little trust remained was placed 
in the hands of the judge and the parliamentary commission that carried on the 
investigation amidst countless obstacles. Along with them, the voices of 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 293 


journalists and the victims’ relatives remained strong, as in Marco Risi’s 
cinematic rendition of these events, // muro di gomma. 

Directed by Risi eleven years after the event, // muro di gomma presents a 
strong, authorial, and authoritative reading of the Itavia crash. Daria Bonfietti, 
president of the Associazione Parenti delle Vittime di Ustica, collaborated on the 
screenplay, co-written by Sandro Petraglia, Stefano Rulli, and Andrea Purgatori. 
Investigative journalist of the Corriere della Sera, Andrea Purgatori also co- 
authored, four years later, A un passo dalla guerra. Ustica, storia di un segreto 
inconfessabile, a long essay from which it becomes clear that the protagonist of 
Risi’s film was indeed Purgatori’s cinematic alterego. Yet the term protagonist, 
in // muro di gomma, seems quite inappropriate. When thinking of a 
“protagonist,” film viewers are usually expecting a strong figure designed to 
carry the action successfully, fostering the spectators’ identification with 
someone bigger, better, and stronger than themselves. Psychoanalysis defines 
this figure the ego-ideal. Yet Rocco, played by Corso Salani, hardly fits this 
image. The protagonist of // muro di gomma is an everyman: not beautiful, not 
strong, and not, by any means, a winner. In the economy of a film that has 
elected to bear witness to institutional crisis, and to the helplessness of the 
citizens who are its victims, Rocco demonstrates Quixotic traits in his attempt to 
challenge higher powers that will inevitably withstand his attack. 

Opening on a close-up of the radar from which the DC-9 suddenly 
disappears, Risi’s film immediately cuts to a different area of the airport, the 
arrival gates where friends and relatives of the victims are anticipating an 
encounter that shall never take place. The authorities have long ascertained the 
disappearance of the flight, yet families have not been notified. Thus, through 
the initial juxtaposition of these first two sequences, the tone for the entire film 
is already set. Knowledge is power, Michel Foucault taught us, and power, in 
this particular situation, is evidenced by how such knowledge is indeed 
withdrawn from those who are more rightfully entitled to it. A dolly shot of the 
arrival terminal, panning on the impatient and anxious faces of friends and 
victims, finds as its counter-shot a close-up of the electronic display signaling 
that the flight’s arrival is now announced as indeterminato. The following 
sequence, in a different area of the same terminal, finds the same people facing a 
man who announces the disappearance of the flight: once again, the tone is set 
for the remaining part of the film, dividing its characters in two sides set against 
one another. Anger, fear, and despair color the faces of the men and women on 
the “good” side. Among them, two faces stand out, that of a middle-aged man 
who protests the lack of information, and that of a younger woman who sits in 
despair hugging her child as the airport officer reads the passengers’ list. The 
camera lingers on a powerful representation of traumatic loss, the woman’s quiet 
and composed pain being signified by hands that grip, eyes that stare, lips held 
tight together, as the names of those who have died are proffered in a solemn, 
hieratic rhythm. Portrayed by Angela Finocchiaro, a famous TV comedian of the 


294 Giancarlo Lombardi 


period, this character is unique, paradoxically, because, like Rocco, she bears no 
striking traits, she is just una donna qualunque. Finocchiaro is quite skilled at 
playing up the immense astonishment felt by someone who has just received 
such unexpected tragic news. Depressed stupefaction colors the words she utters 
in response to Rocco’s enquiry, later in the film, in the sequence set outside the 
morgue, where a few bodies have been returned right after the accident. Asked 
by the journalist about the physical condition in which these bodies lie, 
Finocchiaro responds that they are mere corpses, thereby reminding the viewers 
of those bodies’ extreme dehumanization, their reduction to mere pawns in the 
hands of higher powers who have determined their expendable nature. Right 
before that scene, with a voice ever so soft and colorless, she asks back, “Lei 
pensa di essere importante?,” making a powerful statement whose strength lies 
exactly in the helpless discoloring of that very voice. It is the affirmative answer 
provided by Rocco, slightly nuanced with skepticism evidenced by his word 
choice — “Si, credo di si” — that ideally drives the remaining part of the film, 
making these two statements pivotal elements in the director’s reading of these 
events. 

The same old man who had been complaining to airport officials earlier in 
the film reappears in this sequence. Viewers learn that he has lost several 
members of his family in the plane crash. His pain is rendered most effectively 
by a camera that pans around his face, as he looks up to the sky searching for 
answers he shall not receive. By reducing film speed at this very moment, the 
director effectively conveys to viewers the shock that lies in_ the 
acknowledgement of the extremeness of this tragedy. 

In a film that dwells most exclusively on a scenario of relentless pain, the 
few joyful exchanges portrayed on screen belong to the military world, which 
seems to be holding the key to the mysteries surrounding the Itavia crash. 
Searching for official comments to the news that a missile could have caused the 
accident, Rocco tracks down the highest officials of the Italian Air Force in a 
restaurant where they are celebrating some unknown event with families and 
friends. Viewers are ushered into a different world now, that of power and 
privilege. To the despair and sorrow of the relatives of the victims, just 
portrayed interacting with an insurance agent who has reduced to (minimal) 
figures the lives of their dead beloved, the director juxtaposes the loud 
cheerfulness of these other wives and children, who clap their hands following 
the rhythm of a song belted out by a high officer of the Air Force. Following a 
rhythm in the same way they would follow orders, these people hum to the notes 
of Nessun dorma, from Giacomo Puccini’s Turandot. The tale of a Princess who 
kills her suitors because they fail to solve the riddles she set for them, Turandot 
and specifically Nessun dorma are superbly woven into the intricate texture of // 
muro di gomma. Towering over his guests, the high officer sings: “Ma il mio 
mistero é chiuso in me, il nome suvo nessun sapra! No, no, sulla tua bocca lo 
dird, quando la luce splendera! Ed il mio bacio sciogliera il silenzio che ti fa 





Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 295 


mia! Dilegua o notte! Tramontate, stelle! Tramontate, stelle! All’alba vincero! 
Vincero! Vincero!” He is not entirely faithful to the original lyrics of the aria. 
Not only is he portrayed eliding the first stanza of the aria, a stanza which sets 
the aria’s background mentioning Turandot and thus the romantic content of the 
entire piece, but he is also shown replacing the possessive adjective in the 
second verse, from nome mio to nome suo. What was originally meant to be 
hidden, in the aria, was the name of the person singing it, while in this context 
what comes to be silenced is the name of the mystery itself. Holding the keys to 
what happened in the sky around Ustica, this officer symbolically declares his 
intention not to reveal anything, and his firm belief in a victory that the last three 
verses of the aria, emphasized in a crescendo cheered by the entire banquet 
party, inevitably predict. Rocco witnesses this scene from a distance. In order to 
come closer to the table, he has to overcome a series of obstacles, powerfully 
signified by a camera that moves around the restaurant in jerky movements, as if 
being handheld, taking sharp corners, moving under half-closed doors, zooming 
in and out. 

Each time that Rocco has a brush with power and the truth it withholds, 
Risi’s camera is there to remind us of the presence of obstacles and of the 
ominous magnitude of this very power. The frequent use of bird’s-eye-view 
shots, coupled with the directorial preference for high and low angle shots to 
shots that align viewers and objects represented, act as powerful reminders of 
this uneven encounter. Whether signifying the presence of a mysterious and 
silent observer or reasserting Rocco’s Lilliputian size vis-a-vis the Italian Air 
Force and its entire establishment, these shots are meant to disturb viewers, 
shaking them from the hypnotic effect that conventional cinema rules may have. 
That same hypnotic effect can be indeed translated outside the darkness of the 
movie theater and into our society, where many of us have been coopted into 
believing that our institutions are always working on our behalf. Shaking its 
viewers out of their stupor, Risi’s film is indeed demonstrating its coscienza 
civile not through words, but through shots, through cinematic language. Two 
sequences, in particular, demonstrate the disproportion between Rocco and the 
giants against which he has waged war. The first sequence finds him in a 
museum, where he meets with an informant to discuss the nature of a cryptic 
document he has recently received. The features of the two characters stand out 
rather sharply against the whiteness of the walls of the museum and of the long 
bench on which both characters sit to look at what turns out to be the original 
radar transcript of the DC-9. The ethereal yet majestic surroundings, in their 
neo-classical nature, remind viewers of the white governmental anticamera in 
Bernardo Bertolucci’s Conformista, where the protagonist is waiting to be given 
his next assignment, or another scene in the same film, in which the same 
character, played by Jean Paul Trintignant, visits his father in an insane asylum 
conspicuous for its uniform whiteness. In both films, whiteness comes to 
represent the very opposite of the innocence it usually connotes, acting as 


296 Giancarlo Lombardi 


background to a world of mysteries, betrayal, and extreme injustice. What is 
most striking about this particular scene, in Risi’s film, is the way in which the 
museum’s columns, extreme in their width and height, mark the path taken by 
the two characters during the last part of their conversation. Just as the informant 
is alerting Rocco to the risks he is taking by writing articles found deeply 
disturbing by the state authorities, the two men walk around the columns as if 
they were eliding obstacles in a skiing competition. The same contrast in height 
and size is reaffirmed later in the film when Rocco meets again his informant, 
this time in Paris. By now, the journalist seems to have genuinely accepted 
failure, and his informant seems quite pleased with his attitude. As they walk 
through the streets of Paris, the informant claims the impossibility of ever 
arriving at the truth: “In questo gioco vince chi da le carte... Come si fa a 
chiedere la verita a quelli che sanno?... La gente non deve sapere... A che serve 
dire la verita alla gente? A creare confusione, sfiducia nelle istituzioni.” Firm in 
his discouragement of Rocco’s investigation, the informant continues talking to 
himself as he leaves the journalist behind. Far away from a camera that moves 
upward to a bird’s-eye-view of Salvator Dali’s fountain at Beaubourg, a 
monument that celebrates the convolutions of the present world, the man 
screams his last ironical warnings to Rocco: “Faccia il suo mestiere, si informi, 
vedra che sorprese.” He then turns to Paris, stage of mysteries, betrayals, and 
state treason for centuries, yelling “Che grande citta.” 

At the very end of the film, viewers find Rocco attending the trial in which 
the state is finally prosecuting itself, knowing quite well that not much will 
come out of it. Just as the Capo di Stato Maggiore exits the room in which he 
was just interrogated by the Commissione Stragi, a room in which he 
unsuccessfully attempted to blame journalists for building up a case against the 
Italian Air Force, Rocco comes near him and asks him repeatedly, “Chi mi ha 
pagato, voglio sapere chi mi ha pagato, chi paga noi giornalisti!” The Air Force 
Chief remains silent, erecting the same muro di gomma so forcefully built 
around the events occurred in Ustica on the night of June 27, 1980. Questions of 
responsibility are thus raised and rebuked, seemingly in vain. The movie ends as 
Rocco, in a phone booth, is dictating an article for the journal. Outside, it rains, 
yet his face is not wet with rain, but tears. Satisfaction mixed with 
disillusionment, with the certainty that much, after all, will not change, informs 
his piece, whose creating process we are invited to witness. Words and entire 
lines are inserted, then removed, as the camera alternates extreme close-ups of 
his face, where tears stream down to his chin, with images of the very presses 
from which the newspaper eventually sees the light, bearing the title, “Ustica, 
vergogna di stato”: 


“Ci sono voluti 10 anni, 10 anni di bugie, 10 anni di perché senza risposte. Perché chi 
sapeva € stato zitto? Perché chi poteva scoprire non s’é mosso? Perché questa verita era 
cosi inconfessabile da richiedere il silenzio, l’omerta, l’occultamento delle prove? C’era 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 297 


la guerra quella notte del 27 giugno 1980: c’erano 69 adulti e 12 bambini che tornavano a 
casa, che andavano in vacanza, che leggevano il giornale 0 giocavano con una bambola. 
Quelli che sapevano hanno deciso che i cittadini, la gente, noi non dovevamo sapere: 
hanno manomesso le registrazioni, cancellato i tracciati radar, bruciato i registri, hanno 
inventato registrazioni che non sono mai avvenute, e poi, hanno fatto la cosa piu grave di 
tutte: hanno costretto i deboli a partecipare alla menzogna, trasformando onesta in vilta, 
la difesa disperata del piccolo privilegio del posto di lavoro,” no cancella, “in mediocrita, 
in bassezza. Ora, finalmente, mentre fuori da questo palazzo, dove lo stato interroga lo 
stato, piove, a molti sembra di vedere un po’ di sole.” No, non mi piacciono queste due 
righe, cancella. Metti solo: “Perché”? 


The dissatisfaction evidenced by the removal of the only two positive lines, 
because an article on Ustica could never end with such a phrase as “un po’ di 
sole,” is further proved by the decision to deny any credibility to the reasons 
adduced by those who justified their lies with the fear of losing their jobs. To 
them, and to all the other people involved in the cover-up of this tragic event, 
only mediocrity can be ascribed, mediocrity and bassezza that have only brought 
shame on an entire country. 


Bologna, August 2, 1980 
Vent’anni fa, dieci e venticinque, 2 agosto, Stazione di Bologna. Caldo e sudore. C’é un 
paese in vacanza, che prende treni e fa code per spostarsi dalle citta, laggit fino al mare. 
2 agosto. Caldo, fumo di sigarette e odore di fatica. Si prende il biglietto alla stazione e si 
aspettano, sotto la pensilina, altri treni in partenza e nuovi arrivi. Gente felice che si 
incontra, bambini che giocano, famiglie. L’altoparlante annuncia ritardi. Dieci e 
venticinque. Poi c’é il silenzio irreale, la morte improvvisa, lo scoppio, il boato di una 
forte esplosione. 

(Biacchesi 11) 


Placed in a suitcase left in the waiting room of the railway station, the bomb 
claimed the life of 85 people only a month after Ustica shook the entire 
population to its roots. 1980 will always be remembered as the year of the 
stragi, of mysteries whose solution has always been tentative. After the potential 
explosion of the central heater was ruled out, it became clear that Bologna had 
indeed fallen victim to another terrorist attack, the one that, to this very day, has 
caused the highest number of victims in the history of Italy. While right-wing 
terrorists were immediately held suspects for this massacre, the Secret Services, 
following directions from Masonic Chief Licio Gelli,’ attempted to derail the 
investigations disseminating false evidence that would lead to foreign terrorist 
groups. By the end of August of that same year, however, several neo-fascist 
terrorists were indicted, and most of them were immediately apprehended: their 
leaders, Valerio Fioravanti and Francesca Mambro, were taken into custody 


© For a detailed discussion of Licio Gelli, the Masonic Order, and P2, see Alberto Cecchi; 
Michele Gambino; Sergio Flamigni, 7rame atlantiche. 


298 Giancarlo Lombardi 


during the year that followed. 

Founders of the Nuclei Armati Rivoluzionari, Fioravanti and Mambro had 
already made a name for themselves by murdering a number of people, chief 
among them judge Mario Amato, head of the investigation on neo-fascist 
terrorist organizations for a number of years. Of the 5,000 people imprisoned for 
terrorism during the past thirty years, Francesca Mambro is the woman who has 
collected the highest number of sentences of life imprisonment. She has actually 
been sentenced to life imprisonment for twelve different homicides, while her 
husband Valerio Fioravanti, once a child actor in Italian TV, follows right 
behind with eight similar sentences. After almost twenty years of imprisonment, 
Mambro and Fioravanti have been recently moved to a half-way house: during 
the day, they work for a non-profit organization, while at night they return to 
their cells. To this very day, more than twenty years after the bombing, they 
strenuously deny their participation in the event, which they claim was not 
compatible with their previous terrorist activity. While taking full responsibility 
for all the other crimes committed, always directed towards a specific target, be 
it a policeman, a judge or a militant communist, Mambro and Fioravanti 
maintain that they could have never disseminated the kind of indiscriminate 
terror unleashed by the Bologna bombing. 

Much journalistic writing has been dedicated to their case since their first 
imprisonment. They have authored three books and a play during the past five 
years, and the echo of their voices has often overshadowed the lament of those 
who have suffered the most, the relatives of the many people killed on that torrid 
summer day. For these people, who still believe that much has yet to be unveiled 
on this tragedy, the news that Mambro and Fioravanti had been put in semi- 
liberty was more than shock. It was an insult, an insult to the memory of the men 
and women these two terrorists had supposedly killed. 


Un treno ripercorre la pianura padana. Con quel dolore lontano, molti hanno imparato a 
convivere. Le amicizie perdute, i figli ridotti a polvere, ricordi di padri e madri, e gente 
incontrata per caso, mentre l’orrore bruciava tutti gli attimi vissuti. Dall’estate dell’80, il 
2 agosto lasciano la loro vita e vanno a Bologna. Vogliono vivere il ricordo davanti a 
quella lapide, deposta sulla facciata esterna della sala d’aspetto. Prendono quel treno. 
Sentono che quel dolore non é stato spento, forse per qualcuno si € anche affievolito ma 
la consapevolezza é rimasta. Intatta negli anni. Quella di non voler mai dimenticare. 
Hanno bussato alla porta di magistrati, di politici. Hanno visto sfilare i generali dei 
servizi segreti con i loro “non ricordo,” “non so”. Hanno preso treni per Roma, con la 
speranza che il Parlamento e lo Stato si accorgesse di loro. E sono tornati nelle case di 


sempre con tante promesse e nessuna certezza. Soli. 
(Biacchesi 41) 


On one of those trains described by Daniele Biacchesi in this passage, with 
that same pain in his heart, and the same desire to pay respect to the memory of 
the 85 victims, rides the protagonist of Per non dimenticare, the short film 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 299 


directed by Massimo Martelli and co-produced by RAI, the city of Bologna, as 
well as its region, Emilia-Romagna. 

Heading a cast of stars who have graciously appeared in significant cameo 
roles, Giuseppe Cederna plays the nameless survivor of the massacre, a man 
who escaped the bombing simply because he had left the waiting room shortly 
before its explosion in order to buy a pack of cigarettes. “Io c’ero”, he says 
repeatedly in a voice-over that accompanies the 45-minute film, during which 
viewers are shown a tranche de vie, the ordinary events that took place during 
the very last minutes in the life of the ordinary people who died that August 2, 
1980. And it is no accident that a handful of famous actors and actresses have 
lent their voices and their faces to this singular narration. Their ability to act in 
the lowest possible key is pivotal in the recreation of a very realistic universe 
that is meant to remain, in its extreme simplicity, in the minds of viewers long 
after they have first seen it. 

Husband and wife fight over their in-laws. Two friends try to convince a 
third one to join them on a trip to Riccione, land of sexual perdition. Two old 
lovers re-unite and discuss the impossibility of ever living together. A middle- 
aged man argues with his old mother, who will spend the entire summer with 
him at the beach. A drug addict makes a pass at two soldiers and a priest, trying 
to come up with money she desperately needs. These and many more are the 
stories chosen by Martelli for his film: the narrator acts as their silent witness, 
moving from the waiting room to the bar, and then to the terminal, areas which 
will all be devastated by the explosion. The same distance separates him from 
the men and women he encounters, a distance signified by the full or medium 
shots used by Martelli, who limits the use of close-ups only to the narrator, as if 
to invite viewers into his head, his reflections, his trauma. 

The images portraying the men and women whose lives would tragically 
end at 10:25 a.m. that day are constantly alternated. Viewers are treated to 
different stories that proceed at the same, fast pace, and the shots depicting them 
are interspersed with the same insert, that of the Italian landscape seen from a 
train window, from which the narrator is looking outside while looking inside 
himself, into his memories. The alternating of the many different stories actually 
follows a pace that is virtually fast as a train. Events happen fast, they follow 
one another at a rhythm that cannot be stopped, and, equally, their protagonists’ 
unconscious march towards death cannot be arrested. This march towards death 
is, in the eyes of the survivor who is left to witness and remember it as 
Coleridge’s Ancient Mariner, inevitable in its endless recurrence. 

At the center of this measured portrayal of the momentous beauty of 
ordinary life, a beauty that can only be acknowledged once it is forever 
destroyed, lies another portrayal, which acts as mise-en-abime of the entire film. 
In the waiting room, a young man sees a beautiful woman and lets his eyes 
follow her every movement. The woman eventually sits on a bench across from 
him: their eyes do not meet, her eyes are wandering away, lost in contemplation 


300 Giancarlo Lombardi 


of undetermined thoughts. Suddenly, a dream is shattered: the woman is not 
alone, she is not available. A man holding a child goes up to her, and asks her to 
hold the baby. Their relation is never determined, yet we are left to believe that 
the child might be theirs. Our young man reacts back: he pulls out a sketch pad, 
and starts drawing frenetically. Once he is done, he stands up, places at her feet 
the page on which he has drawn her beautiful face, then he leaves the room. As 
she looks down to pick up the picture, and then looks at him, he stops, turns 
around, and their eyes finally lock: his face breaks into a timid smile as he turns 
again and walks away. Shortly before the portrait is done, however, the young 
painter engages in a small conversation with another man sitting next to him. 
Common appreciation of the woman’s looks leads the painter to discuss the 
nature of a face and of the tales it may hide: “E incredibile: le persone, da una 
parte ci sono i volti, dall’altra le loro storie, non c’é mai rapporto, uno si diverte 
a indovinarne le vite, scoprirne il carattere, ma € impossibile. Quell’uomo chi é? 
I] marito o un parente?” Faces are opaque: they hide stories that can be imagined 
but not be ascertained, yet each one of us is naturally bound to draw 
conclusions, make a film in one’s own head about the lives of the many people 
one casually encounters each day. Like the young man in this sequence, the 
director has chosen to sketch a number of faces, portray a number of characters, 
granting us only few details: the same details granted to a person who would 
encounter them accidentally. Out of the few exchanges overheard by the 
narrator, who is our only vantage point in this film, out of the few images seen 
by and through his eyes, viewers are then left to weave stories. These stories are 
tales witnessed from a distance: that of the time that separates us viewers from 
the event, and from the physical distance experienced by the narrator, survivor 
and unlikely flaneur in a world that is about to collapse. From this distance, 
viewers are asked to bear indirect testimony to an event that shattered far too 
many lives, an event whose immensity was so atrocious that it could not even be 
portrayed. The film ends as the clock, in the waiting room, reaches 10:25 a.m., 
and the only noise left is that of cutlery shaking on a table in the coffee shop 
adjacent to the waiting room, simply because a man hit the legs of this small 
table. The quiet before the storm, the very last moment of composure, is where 
Martelli decides to end his narration, choosing to spare viewers from the 
obscenity and the disarray that follow the explosion. The viewers shall not 
witness such an event because, by witnessing it, we would cross the threshold 
signified by that distance from which each story and each character were shown. 
Crossing that threshold would imply forfeiting the discretion and the respect 
with which the director has chosen to treat such delicate subject matter. To 
witness the discomposure of the bodies shaken and disparaged by the explosion 
would indeed be inappropriate and, in its own particular way, obscene. One last 
sequence closes Martelli’s film, a close-up of the monument erected to the 
victims, accompanied by an extreme close-up of a series of figures. These 
figures, placed next to the name of each victim, are there to remind the world at 


Terrorism, Truth, and the Secret Service: Questions of Accountability 301 


what age each one of the 85 people met its untimely demise. 

Like the eight people who died in Brescia, the eighty-one passengers of the 
Itavia flight and the many other victims of the stragi from Piazza Fontana, Gioia 
Tauro, Peteano and many other places, Bologna’s eighty-five victims are forever 
to be inscribed in the minds of those who are left and those who are still 
searching for a truth far too difficult and obscene to be told. Ethics teaches us 
not only to think about the other but also to act on behalf of the other, while 
prompting us to seek and question accountability. 


Vent’anni dopo, chi si ricordera di loro, invisibili vittime della strategia della tensione? 
Eppure quelle storie sono ancora scritte, nella storia e nella memoria. E come se 
parlassero, quelle vittime che hanno un nome. II loro é un canto corale, collettivo. Un 
unico grande grido di rabbia. 

(Biacchesi 41-42) 


What Biacchesi writes about Bologna could easily apply to other stragi 
discussed in this article and to the ethical debate raised by the three movies that 
portray them, opting for different portrayals that still remain, however, equally 
forceful. All three movies, in fact, virtually join in the canto corale described by 
Biacchesi, giving shape to new memories, new forms of remembrance of the 
most traumatic period experienced by Italy in its national history. /nchiesta, 
memoria, and denuncia are thus equally inscribed in these cinematic texts, 
where politics and poetry merge inextricably, and disdain and remembrance 
become opposite sides of the very same coin.
