Eugene TeSelle 


pugaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: 
A Retrospective Analysis 


We are familiar with “academics” and “professionals” and “experts” of many 
kinds. Usually they are not controversial, and in fact they rank fairly high on the 
ladder of social prestige. Educated people often take part in public life as 
employees of (or volunteers with) government, advising those who shape public 
policy, or engaging in planning and what past generations called “social 
engineering.” 

Such persons become an identifiable social group, labeled “intellectuals” to 
indicate their specific difference, when they intervene in public affairs as critics 
or advocates; then both they and their actions can become highly controversial. 
The term “intelligentsia” became current in Russian in the middle of the 
nineteenth century, and “intellectuals” during the Dreyfus affair. (Clemenceau 
used it as a term of reproach, but it was appropriated as a badge of honor in the 
“Manifeste des intellectuels,”’ published the day after Zola’s ‘\/J’accuse” in 
January of 1898). André Malraux popularized the expression “engaged 
intellectuals,” which he applied to those who acted, not merely commented, on 
the political scene. 

In the U.S. we often hear about “public intellectuals,” a group that consists 
largely of those who used to be called “men of letters,” writing for “journals of 
opinion” or publishing books commenting on the current scene in culture, 
society, and politics, earning their income primarily from writing (and often 
lecturing, too) as a full-time activity. They are, then, “free-floating intellectuals” 
or, in economic terms, “independent intellectuals”; unfriendly right-wing critics 
have now taken to calling them “the chattering classes.” In addition, we should 
not forget the novelists, dramatists and poets, painters and composers who so 
often shape, or express, expose, or challenge the spirit of their times, not 
excluding public policy. 

A different group of intellectuals, those who make their living in other ways 
but intervene in public life on an occasional basis, is especially interesting. 
Cumulatively they provide a number of “test cases” inviting inquiry as to why, 
and on what occasions, they emerge from their libraries, laboratories, and 
classrooms. Most often they are humanists and social scientists, and there are 
expressions of surprise when scientists like Andrei Sakharov in the U.S.S.R., 
Fang Lizhi in the People’s Republic of China, and Noam Chomsky in the U.S. 


Annali d'Italianistica 19 (2001) 


304 Eugene TeSelle 


intervene vocally in public affairs. Their field of specialization seems less 
important than the role they find themselves able to play in attacking injustices 
and championing human rights. It is an old tradition. Bartolomé de las Casas, 
Milton, and Voltaire were precursors of those who defended Dreyfus, Sacco and 
Vanzetti, victims of the Red Scare, and “dissidents” in the Soviet bloc. 

Being an engaged intellectual can be dangerous. Some have been killed, 
either for challenging those in power or for having been too closely associated 
with those who held power and lost it. Federico Garcia Lorca was executed by a 
pro-Franco death squad in 1936, Giovanni Gentile by Italian partisans in 1944; 
Pablo Neruda died while awaiting medical care after Pinochet’s coup in 1973. 
Some have languished for years in the prisons of the Soviet Union, Cuba, 
Indonesia, Myanmar, and Turkey. The campaigns of Amnesty International and 
PEN are often devoted to securing the release of intellectuals through the 
pressure of public opinion. A fatwa was proclaimed against Salman Rushdie. 
Oscar Romero was made archbishop of San Salvador because he seemed to be a 
harmless intellectual; then his experiences as a pastor led him to speak out so 
firmly and with such consistency that he had to be assassinated. The execution- 
style killing in El Salvador of six Jesuit professors, their housekeeper, and her 
daughter in 1989 was especially symbolic, their brains being carved out to 
convey a message about what happens to the “intellectual authors” of subversive 
ideas. 

Why should intellectuals think their comments are worthy of public 
attention, and why should the public take them seriously?! What is most obvious, 
of course, is that they are educated at a higher level than most around them — in 
many communities the intellectual may be the schoolteacher, the priest, the 
newspaper editor, or the physician — and thus are presumed to be able to 
comment on public affairs with more expertise or insight than others. In addition 
they are expected to rise above the narrow self-interest of their profession or the 
group that pays them. Even when they operate on the basis of conventional 
values, it is assumed that they have a steadier and more comprehensive view of 
the situation and will not be swayed by passion or interest alone. As Lasch has 
put it, the intellectual is 


a person whose relationship to society is defined, both in his eyes and in the eyes of the 

society, principally by his presumed capacity to comment upon it with greater detachment 

than those more directly caught up in the practical business of production and power. 
(1965, ix) 


Especially in the modern world — and this, in fact, is one of the defining 
features of “modern” — intellectuals have become increasingly independent, 


' For varied and inclusive discussions of intellectuals and their roles, see Huszar, 
Maclean, Fink, Jennings and Kemp-Welch, and Donatich. 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 305 


filling the role played in more traditional societies by seers, prophets, bishops, 
and theologians. While some continue to be supported by traditional institutions, 
especially the university, church, or government, many of them are free-lance 
intellectuals, supported by the new market for newspapers, books, and 
periodicals, and public speakers and “talking heads” on television programs. 
Gouldner suggests that modern intellectuals have created a distinctive kind of 
reflexive and critical discourse, what he calls “edited speech” (28-30, 58-60, 83- 
85). The public role of intellectuals is powerful because of increasing literacy 
and the broadening of participation in public affairs, not only through elections 
but through many other ways of influencing the formation of policy, ranging 
from the institutions of civil society to public opinion polls and protest 
demonstrations. 


Intellectuals in Twentieth-Century America 

Intellectuals have played many public roles through the centuries, sometimes as 
advisers to rulers, sometimes as critics (one thinks especially of the 
Enlightenment). The stakes were raised with the French Revolution of 1789, 
which began an epoch of social and political upheaval in which intellectuals 
often played a major role. There are some who see the symbolic end of that 
epoch exactly two hundred years later, with the collapse of Soviet power and the 
emergence of the U.S. as world hegemon in 1989. 

Since the situation is different in every country, even in Europe, I intend to 
concentrate on the United States, where the relationship of intellectuals to 
economic and political life has been more problematical than elsewhere and has 
been analyzed repeatedly, with varying emphases. Grand theories, even those of 
deconstruction, tend to reflect the situations of their time. Therefore I shall begin 
with historical description and move cautiously toward attempts at 
generalization. Having a life span that now approaches seventy years, | have 
been impacted by the writings of many of the players. | know how attractive they 
could be, and I also know how many mistakes they — and I, in following them 
to some degree or other — were able to make (see the autobiographical 
reflections in the Appendix). 

Two periods are generally viewed as the glory days of intellectual influence 
in public life: the Progressive era, roughly 1890 to 1920, and the New Deal and 
its sequels, 1933 to 1968. Much was accomplished in the legislative realm in 
those periods, and yet many difficulties were encountered and many 
compromises were made. 

In a recent book, Judis defends the role of public-spirited “elites” in U.S. 
history, especially during the Progressive era and the New Deal. A hundred years 
ago, labor and populist dissent made the problems of an industrial society 
unavoidable. Many people, of course, wanted economic revolution. The 
“progressive elites” (including many people in the business community) 
persuaded legislators to achieve at least some of their goals. Labor laws, food 


306 Eugene TeSelle 


and drug regulation, and a graduated income tax were achieved. Large 
corporations began to be regulated by government, usually under terms that 
enabled the corporations to participate in, perhaps even to guide, the regulatory 
process. Since that time the progressive view has been that government must 
solve problems, the conservative view has been that government is the problem. 

Religion also played an important public role during these periods, 
prompting and then legitimating the progressive agenda.” There was a broad 
consensus that society ought to be transformed in accordance with the message 
of the prophets, Jesus, and the early church. It was a “soft” progressivism, of 
course, not only because of its religious posture but because the social location 
of progressive Christianity was the professional-managerial middle class, 
persons who inevitably felt that they were called to resolve the problems of 
society using their own expertise. 

Evaluations of the Progressive Era, the New Deal, and the Great Society 
vary widely. There are a few who think that federal legislation veered far to the 
left, largely as a result of the activities of intellectuals. Milton and Rose 
Friedman, publicists for the “free market,” see in Fabian socialism and New Deal 
liberalism a tide of opinion that swept past all obstacles for a number of decades 
(284), and they place the primary blame upon college professors. (They could 
mention Protestant ministers, too, who were widely influenced by the social 
gospel, and Catholic priests, who were following the papal social encyclicals.) 
“In our opinion,” they say, “the Socialist party was the most influential political 
party in the United States in the first decades of the twentieth century” (286-86). 
Because it had only limited electoral success, they reason, it could afford to be 
“a party of principle,” while the major parties had to avoid extremism and seek 
the middle ground. And yet many planks in the Socialist Party’s 1928 platform, 
the Friedmans complain, have since become law: flood relief, electric power, 
unemployment insurance, public works, health and accident insurance, old age 
pensions, child labor laws, and heavier taxes on the wealthy (311-12). 

Others, in far larger numbers, argue that the influence of “progressive elites” 
in these periods was a constructive one, making much-needed changes and, in 
the process, saving a capitalist economy. Some suggest that the role of “old 
money” and of the professional classes is far preferable to the dogmatic 
advocacy of the “free market” that is often found among small businessmen, the 
nouveaux riches, and the upwardly mobile. (In the U.S. in 2001 we have heard 
billionaires oppose abolition of the estate tax, taking seriously the ideology of 
“equal opportunity.”) One result is that the business community, especially its 


> Crunden shows how the Progressive ethos grew out of the spirit of “Trinitarian New 
England.” Dorrien and McCarraher offer comprehensive and thoughtful surveys of 
Christian critiques of U.S. society up to the present, considering Catholic as well as 
Protestant thinkers and activists. For a careful examination of one of the leading 
periodicals, see Hulsether. 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 307 


most elitist and internationalist segments, has maintained influence upon the 
Democratic Party and the formation of public policy. 

Even at their best, the role of the intellectual elites during the Progressive 
and New Deal eras was limited by class and race. While they defended the rights 
of labor, their links with labor were rather tenuous, usually mediated by the 
Socialist Party which included both occupational groups. While there was much 
concern about race relations, and especially about lynching and 
disenfranchisement in the South, progressives were not closely associated with 
W.E.B. DuBois, certainly a major “organic intellectual” and a precursor of the 
creative new group of African-Americans in our own time, mostly academics, 
whom some have celebrated as the only “public intellectuals” worthy of the 
name during the Nineties (Boynton 53-54). 

There has always been suspicion that the role of elites was not merely to 
negotiate between populist movements and those in power, but to co-opt and 
moderate the campaigns of those who sought more fundamental changes in 
society. (In answer, of course, it could be said that such changes might not have 
occurred at all if they had not been framed in more widely acceptable terms; “the 
art of the possible” will always be haunted by such uncertainties.) At the level of 
political philosophy, there are some who say that the state’s role is to assert the 
common good against the claims of interest groups; but the result may turn out to 
be little better than “corporatism,” the attempt to gain harmony among social 
classes rather than diminish differentiations of class and power. But what, in the 
absence of full-scale revolution, is the alternative? Only the continuation of 
dissent and struggle, strike and boycott, propaganda and political action, all 
leading to both advances and setbacks as the general economic and political 
environment shifts. 

An even more interesting critique of progressivism looks not at the political 
gains that it made but at the cultural factors that inspired it. In fact, a “cultural 
revolution” directed against much that progressivism represented began almost 
as soon as it was born. Progressivism was an outgrowth of middle-class 
moralism, even Puritanism, with an element of “uplift” that involved a 
considerable degree of social control of what the previous generation had called 
“the dangerous classes.” The radicals and bohemians who flourished after the 
turn of the century saw this feature of progressivism and expressly criticized it. 
Lasch, taking seriously the radicals’ sense of disillusionment and their awareness 
of cultural and psychological factors, devoted much of his career to examining 
their writings and their actions, scrutinizing them in the manner of a 
psychologist. He suspected that their brand of radicalism was motivated in large 
part by psychological factors: rebellion against parents who had already been too 
permissive; disillusionment about a middle-class culture that had no place for 
critical thinking and high culture; contempt for not only the middle classes but 
also the working classes; a shift from political to cultural and even psychological 
emphases; a kind of “hard-boiled realism” that has expressed itself variously in 


308 Eugene TeSelle 


cultural criticism, political radicalism, or in Cold War or neo-conservative 
politics; and in the end, most pitiably, withdrawal into alternative institutions 
where one need not encounter the masses at all. 

In the U.S. the spirit of democratic populism and commercial boosterism 
has often been hostile to intellectual activity, which has then had to find its 
“social carriers” in limited, often embattled groups. In the worst of times it found 
shelter in the “genteel tradition” of the upper classes and their elite colleges. 
Then it was able, after 1890, to gain access to the popular press and influence the 
middle class, especially its professionals. After 1910 it generated a small but 
relatively independent class of its own (Hofstadter 393-432). Several historians 
have suggested that this new group of public intellectuals was made possible by 
the growth of a critical mass of “bohemians” early in the century and again 
during the Fifties and Sixties. New York’s Greenwich Village, and college towns 
around the country, attracted enough bohemians and radicals, they argue, to 
support each other’s journalistic ventures and have an impact on local life 
(Jacoby 27-53; Biel 85-98). It is not that the universities themselves were 
especially tolerant of new ideas; the troubles of Richard Ely, Thorstein Veblen, 
Scott Nearing, and many other public intellectuals indicate how authoritarian 
even the best universities could be. These free-floating or independent 
intellectuals lived on their margins, but they flourished in the atmosphere they 
stimulated. 

It may be that the artists of the time, especially the six or eight who were 
called in retrospect “the Ashcan School,” were freest and most wide-ranging, 
perhaps because of the non-verbal suggestiveness of visual art. They sold 
illustrations to the middle-class magazines of the time, and yet several of them 
were Socialists and contributed cartoons to The Masses. Their art can be 
variously characterized as realism, or stylistic innovation, or Whitmanesque 
evocation of the variety and vitality of urban life (especially in its public spaces), 
as a celebration of working people and popular entertainers, as satire on the 
upper classes, and as implied criticism of the economic causes of the urban 
geography they depict (Zurier 85-189). 


Problematizing the Intellectuals, Issue #1. 

The phenomenon of “cultural revolution” seems to confirm a classic criticism of 
intellectuals. They are often assumed to belong to the “ivory tower” or the 
dreamy realm of the creative arts; when they intervene in public affairs, the 
argument goes, they soon find themselves out of their element, unable to 
understand political realities or to accomplish much in this alien realm. The 
cultural rebellion of the bohemians and radicals, disillusioned with attempts at 
direct intervention in political life, seems to confirm the assumption that 
intellectuals are irrelevant to the rough-and-tumble of economic and political 
life. These cultural rebels were simultaneously criticized from the left, too, on 
the grounds that they were interested only in self-expression at the expense of 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 309 


commitment to political change. 

These instances in U. S. history highlight a classic problem, one that can be 
formulated at its greatest generality in this way: If the proper concern of 
intellectuals is with transcendent values, abstract ideas, theories with universal 
intent, or artistic representations that have potentially universal appeal, then 
how should this concern relate to particular social and political issues and to 
the intellectual’s own location within history? Platonists will have one answer, 
thinking of ideas as independent and formative, though imperfectly realized in 
the world of ordinary experience. Historicists will acknowledge that ideal values 
are “emergent,” without reducing them to the factors that occasioned them. 
Liberals and market theorists will focus upon free choice among a multiplicity 
of concepts and values. ‘“Tough-minded realists,” disillusioned about the 
choices that are made both by consumers and by political decision makers, will 
emphasize the need for force, deterrence, or social contracts. Communitarians 
will insist upon the need for agreed values before there can be further 
discussion. Post-modernists, of course, will ask penetrating questions about the 
legitimacy and coherence of any claims to abstract or universal truth, for even 
the historical process through which they emerge is suspect. 

It could be, however, that these same conflicts and controversies will be 
evaluated more positively. Perhaps intellectuals, writers, and artists are not 
abandoning their vocation but exercising it appropriately when they offer 
perspectives on public affairs. Perhaps the intense rage that is directed against 
them attests to the relevance and power of what they are doing. Thus far, 
however, we have only identified the problem, not brought it to resolution. 


The relationship between intellectuals and the general populace is a troubled 
one, and Antonio Gramsci is noteworthy for having grappled with it quite 
directly. He refused to draw a rigid dichotomy between the professional 
intellectual and the ordinary person. There are no non-intellectuals, he asserted; 
all people are intellectuals, because all people think about their own situation. 
What is needed, he said, is to enable them to make their reflective activity more 
“critical.” But, of course, a genuinely critical consciousness must eventually be 
carried by a group of professional intellectuals (1971, 5-14, 323-43; 1975 12 §1; 
11 §12). 

Suggestions like his are often encountered in considerations of the social 
role of intellectuals, for their task is usually understood to be one of “educating 
the public.” Both terms, however, as well as the nature of their relationship, 
demand reflection. By the public we can mean everyone from government to 
“the masses.” By education we can mean procedures ranging from gaining the 
ear of decision makers to socratically educing insights from the populace. And 
what do we mean, finally, when we expect intellectuals to relate to “the people”? 
In the U. S., for example, there has been perpetual controversy over the nature of 
“populism” in its generic sense. It may be a noble expression of grass-roots 


310 Eugene TeSelle 


democracy and a corrective to the arrogance of intellectuals. On the other hand, 
populism is characterized by a rhetoric of stereotyping and scapegoating, 
sometimes of the powerful, sometimes of the educated, sometimes of the poor or 
minority groups. As Bender says, “Populism demonizes; its iconography is in 
fact a demonology [...]. Symbols always count far more than the understanding 
of structures, processes and causes” ( 352). 

Most intellectuals would say, upon reflection, that their proper role is 
neither elitist, doing things over the heads of the people, nor populist, submitting 
to their control or appealing to their worst impulses; it is to take the initiative in 
raising new questions and challenging popular opinions, inevitably exercising a 
kind of authority precisely in their function as intellectuals, but with willingness 
to have their statements tested in debate and practice.’ Gramsci’s own focus, of 
course, was not upon literary activity but upon the modern political party, one of 
whose achievements, he said, is to transmit the results of intellectual activity to 
more people, and to help them rise above the characteristic split between 
fatalism about their immediate situation and reverence for the conventional 
culture that is given them by the schoolteacher or the priest, or in our own day, 
by the mass media. 

It was in thinking about political strategy after Mussolini’s success that 
Gramsci explored two possible approaches. The French Revolution had invented 
one successful strategy, what he called ‘“Jacobinism” — seizure of leadership, 
defending positions against all attackers, becoming the vanguard that is able to 
mobilize and lead other social groups — and he characterized it in military terms 
as “war of maneuver.” This strategy had been followed successfully in Russia by 
Lenin. But similar attempts in Western Europe after the First World War had 
failed; the situation, Gramsci thought, called for a different strategy, one that he 
called “war of position.” Where there is a robust “civil society,” he said, it 
functions like a system of earthworks and fortifications lying behind the overt 
force of the state; if it is to be overcome, it will be not with swift cavalry 
maneuvers but only through trench or siege warfare (1971, 52-90, 229-39; 1975 
19 §24: 13 §24, 7 §16, 6 §138). Under such conditions, culture becomes 
especially important. Against the “hegemony” exercised by the dominant class, a 
“counter-hegemony” must be developed among those seeking something new 
and better, and once they achieve this counter-hegemony they will be in a 
position to exert political power. 

Gramsci’s analysis has been widely used for the reason that it seemed 
appropriate to twentieth-century realities. His theory of “war of position,” with 
its attention to civil society and the role of culture, not only has seemed to be an 


3 Michael notes that even the “pedagogy of the oppressed,” which claims to educe 
insights from the people themselves, must, by the nature of the case, be manipulative in 
its methods, no matter how valid one thinks one’s insights are or how willing one is to 
have them discussed and tested in popular discussion (50-56, 62-63). 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 311 


accurate reflection of social realities. It highlights the importance of intellectuals, 
whose influence usually depends upon a complex “public” rather than “the 
state,” and who under conditions of political confusion often find it preferable, 
as we have already seen, to concentrate their activities in the area of culture. A 
strong case can be made, furthermore, that Gramsci’s strategy was a viable one: 
in both Italy and France the Communists, like the Catholics, created a broad 
array of cultural and social activities, appealing to all age and occupational 
groups, in addition to strong unions in the workplace, while the attention of 
Socialists and Radicals was drawn almost entirely to parliamentarism (Birnbaum 
55, 139-40, 270-72, 278-79). 

Gramsci himself should not be misidentified, however, as a_ benign 
champion of cultural revolution through voluntary organizations. He was a 
committed Leninist who preferred “Jacobin” methods and fell back upon “war of 
position” to pursue the same strategy with different means. While Social 
Democrats were committed to parliamentarism, Communist intellectuals saw it 
as their task to be the vanguard of the revolutionary party, the “Modern Prince” 
which would lead an unformed proletariat into a total (he even accepted the word 
“totalitarian”) reorganization of society (Harding 208-16, 216-19). One 
hegemony was to be succeeded by another. 

This is, to be sure, one possible role for intellectuals, especially under 
conditions where there is not a strong “civil society.” It has been argued that this 
role was the endemic stance of intellectuals in Russia, who opposed the 
absolutism of the Russian state but aspired to the same authoritarian role for 
themselves, advocating for the people against the state but never comfortable 
acting with the people (Burbank 106-11). Lasch has suggested that most 
intellectuals have this kind of elitist streak somewhere in their makeup (1995, 3- 
22). 

To stop with these observations, however, would leave Gramsci isolated in 
his time and place, when it is clear that his observations have much wider 
relevance. In the U.S. today we have become accustomed to speaking of “culture 
wars”; the term is intentionally reminiscent of the Kulturkampf between 
Bismarck and the Vatican. Culture wars have an intrinsic tendency toward 
dualistic thinking: they are based in competing moral visions, such that those 
who disagree are placed beyond the bounds of legitimacy (Hunter 1991, 107- 
32), and there is an urge to “force political solutions” rather than trust continued 
dialogue (Hunter 1994, 215-26). Culture wars are made possible by the very 
character of civil society in the U.S.: the multiplicity of voluntary organizations, 
including religious, political, and “issue” organizations with strong value 
commitments; First Amendment protections of free speech and freedom of 
religion; and steadily enhanced communication through the press, radio and 
television, and now the Internet. Under such circumstances there is every reason 
to try to gain cultural hegemony, or at the very least to deny hegemony to other 
tendencies. The political achievements of the New Deal and the Great Society 


312 Eugene TeSelle 


were zealously contested in their own time. Then new divisions arose during the 
Sixties because of the civil rights movement, the “counter culture,” and 
opposition to the Vietnam War. They continued in debates over women’s rights, 
apartheid in South Africa, and U.S. policy in the Third World during the 
Seventies and Eighties. Now the end of the Cold War has only intensified culture 
wars over issues of feminism, same-sex relations, “family values,” and 
reproductive rights; and what these have in common, as Hunter points out, is that 
they raise the question of who will control the human body, a symbol of society 
itself (1994, 3-4). The well-funded campaigns of the religious and secular Right 
have a strange resemblance to the Gramscian quest for cultural hegemony, and in 
fact the Radical Right is a grotesque mirror image of the Leninist vanguard 
party, which is its greatest fear and which it uses as an excuse to impose 
traditional values upon a frightened society. 


Problematizing the Intellectuals, Issue #2. 

Often we hear what might be called the ‘damage theory,” the accusation that 
intellectuals are about as helpful to society as a bull in a china shop. Plato 
hoped that philosophers might become kings and straighten out political life; it 
turned out that his ideal polity was built upon inequality, totalitarian control, 
and the noble lie. All too frequently, it is said, this is the fate of intellectuals. 

To be sure, intellectuals have been associated with totalitarian or near- 
totalitarian movements; some regard it as symptomatic that Pol Pot, the creator 
of one of them, studied at the Sorbonne. At a far less dramatic level there is the 
accusation (voiced especially by Lasch) that intellectuals tend to be elitists, 
having excessive confidence in big government and technological progress, 
especially when they can be in charge. There is a widely diffused theory that 
intellectuals constitute a “new class” with its own interests, perhaps even 
representing historical destiny, yet with the limitations that belong to any class.” 
An East European has come to the doleful conclusion that “if you enter the 
political market-place where there is no place for culture values as such, you 
inevitably submit to its laws” (Szacki 237). Conservatives typically attack public 
intellectuals (except when they are their own) on the grounds that, in venturing 
into an area in which they lack expertise, they let “politics” intrude into 
“culture.” 

On the metaphysical plane there is a notable tradition, linked especially 
with Hegel and Marx, that denies or redefines the problem. When ideas become 
too abstract and independent, the argument goes, it is a sign of their invalidity, 
for the proper role of ideas is to express reality and to change it. There remains, 


* Gouldner suggests that while intellectuals may at times constitute a dangerous 
revolutionary party with totalitarian aspirations, or be the servants of the “old class,” they 
usually function as a “flawed universal class” with a unique perspective on the next stage 
to which human history ought to move (7, 83-85). 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 313 


even in this tradition, a distinction between the real and the rational, but their 
relationship is an organic one, such that the work of artists and intellectuals has 
value primarily in bringing to expression the spirit of the age, mediating it to 
itself and promoting the next advance in human society. This tradition is 
monistic, although in a complex way, and it expects one hegemony to be 
replaced by another. Metaphysics is reflected in politics where there is 
absolutism, liberalism seems to be a weaker remedy than a new form of 
absolutism. 

The mirror image of Hegelian idealism and Marxist praxis is the perverse 
realism of Nietzsche, Sorel, and Maurras, which no longer has confidence in the 
dialectic of history and therefore tries to force it through an exertion of will 
(Leninism has something of this, too). This tradition was the specific target of 
Benda’s La trahison des clercs. For Benda the clercs are those whose activities 
do not have practical ends, whose realm is ‘not of this world.” He saw it as an 
especially dramatic betrayal, therefore, when they subjected thought to 
“reality” and pandered to the political passions of the laics. 


A worthy counterpart to Gramsci in considering issues of Marxist strategy, 
but with a very different outcome, is the group called the “New York 
intellectuals.” They were a fairly cohesive group, mostly Jewish, with a 
distinctive development — a history that illustrates the many dilemmas faced by 
progressive intellectuals over a span of several decades, offering a kind of 
“longitudinal study” of the reactions of the same set of individuals to often 
drastic changes in the external situation, and to the endless internal bickering 
within the left during the same decades. They, like most U.S. intellectuals during 
the Thirties, tended to be progressive or socialist in their orientation. This group 
was distinctive, however, in that they turned away from the Communist Party out 
of revulsion against Stalinism and attached themselves to Trotsky, until they 
discovered how authoritarian he could be, too (Wald 1987 46-74; Dorman 41- 
82: Biel 192-221). Their reasons for dissent from Stalinism were numerous: its 
expulsion of dissenters, its factional behavior during the Spanish Civil War, the 
show trials of the late Thirties, the manipulative behavior of the Party during the 
Popular Front period, the flip-flops in the party line from pro-war to anti-war 
during the Hitler-Stalin pact of 1939-1941, and then to full participation in the 
war effort (to the extent of issuing a no-strike pledge and muting their previously 
vocal agitation for civil rights during World War II), the armed takeover of 
governments in Soviet-occupied Eastern Europe and a new round of show trials, 
the Khrushchev speech of 1956 and the suppression of the Hungarian revolt, and 
sO on. 

The consequence was that they, like the Socialist Party of Norman Thomas, 
did not participate in the Popular Front alliance with the New Deal in the mid- 
1930s. To some historians on the left it means that they missed the biggest 
opportunity in U.S. history to make an impact on social and economic 


314 Eugene TeSelle 


legislation; to them, however, it meant not being co-opted into supporting 
changes that merely made the capitalist system more tolerable. 

It also meant that they took a dim view of the cultural movement 
characterized as “proletarian” or “socialist realism.” They saw it as an 
expression of capitalist culture at its worst, a cynical manipulation of public 
opinion through the “vulgar political coding” of images, a violation of both 
intellectual and cultural freedom (Wald 1987, 221; 1992, 228-29). As early as 
the Thirties they championed modernist art as more innovative, critical, and 
profound. In continuity with the earlier generation of bohemians, then, they were 
critics of popular culture in the name of what was coming to be called “high- 
brow” culture. They became such critics with full awareness that modernism in 
the arts was often linked with the conservative New Criticism, but they wanted 
art to be creative, and like the earlier bohemians they recognized that it gained 
critical distance from contemporary society. 

We should not be simplistic about these issues. Futurism and constructivism 
had flourished in the early years of the U.S.S.R. A turn away from the most 
angular and dissonant kinds of modernism had already begun during the 
Twenties, especially with the “neo-classicism” of Stravinsky and Respighi, not 
motivated by a proletarian impulse at all. In any case, the New York intellectuals 
set themselves on the fringes of some of the major artistic achievements of the 
time, which ranged from murals in post offices and public schools across the 
U.S., the more controversial murals of Diego Rivera, Aaron Douglas’s 
celebrations of the Harlem Renaissance, the “agitation art” of Ben Shahn, and 
the photographs of Walker Evans, to the music of Kurt Weill and Marc 
Blitzstein, Aaron Copland, Roy Harris, and Virgil Thomson, characterized 
variously as “folk” or “nationalist” in character. 

It was the same among writers. The bohemianism of the Twenties was 
superseded by a new awareness of social and political issues, triggered first by 
the execution of Sacco and Vanzetti in 1927, then by the Depression and the 
Spanish Civil War (Cowley 223-42). Writers signed many pronouncements, and 
the Communist Party presented itself as the connecting strand among of them. It 
was never clear, however, to what extent writers were acting as /iberals or as 
Communists, or as an ambiguous mixture of the two, and most of them reserved 
the right to dissent from Soviet-style dogmatism while not making themselves 
captive to the existing social order.° The “proletarian novels” of the Thirties 
turned out to be more effective when they were written by independent novelists 
like Dos Passos, Hemingway, and Steinbeck rather than by those who tried to be 
“politically correct” and follow the party line.° Wald, in a sober moment of 


> A comprehensive and sympathetic survey of the many currents during these 
decades can be found in Aaron. 

° The expression “politically correct” was originally used on the left, of course, to 
criticize the conformist spirit of those who were under the discipline of the Communist 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 315 


reconsideration, acknowledges that the left had difficulty standing apart from the 
Communist Party, even when there were qualms about its tactics, because of its 
strong advocacy for justice and the dedication of its members; he concludes that 
“the Communist Party was central and that the other traditions too narrow to 
constitute an independent foundation” (1922, 123). 

The same dilemma haunted the political world. Henry Wallace was perhaps 
the chief embodiment of the New Deal, and he had immense popular appeal (for 
this reason F.D.R. chose him as vice-presidential candidate in 1940). It also 
meant that he aroused hostility from big city bosses, Southern Democrats, and 
conservative Catholics within the Democratic Party (the reason why F.D.R. 
dumped him in favor of Harry Truman in 1944); from industrialists, conservative 
Republicans, the military, the State Department, and the F.B.I. at home; and 
from champions of the British and French Empires as the Second World War 
drew to a close (Culver and Hyde 217-23, 339-66). The tragedy of the years 
1945-1948, as many of us who were coming to maturity during that time can see 
in hindsight, is that Wallace, who always identified his own program as 
“liberalism,” failed to detach himself sufficiently from Stalinism, while the 
“realists” and the anti-Stalinists failed to detach themselves sufficiently from the 
forces of reaction at home and abroad. 

With the Soviet-sponsored takeover of Eastern Europe and the rise of the 
Cold War, the options seemed to have narrowed. The New York intellectuals 
joined in the general framing of the alternatives as “Stalinism or democracy,” 
meaning by the latter the admittedly flawed social order of Western Europe and 
the U. S.: an order they had once tried to transform fundamentally but now saw 
as the best achievable for the moment, to be “critically” affirmed and defended 
against Stalinism (as they had earlier defended it against Fascism). Many of 
them, with the penitential spirit of those who regret their past errors and the zeal 
of new converts to what has come to be called “democratic capitalism,” were 
ready to join the Cold War. 

Anti-Stalinist liberals in the Democratic Party helped open the door as early 
as 1947 to the investigations by the House Un-American Activities Committee 
and later by Senator Joseph McCarthy; the hearings were then used to taint and 
discredit nearly all liberal and progressive movements. Persons under attack 
were expected not only to detach themselves from Stalinism, as most of them 
had already done, but to repent publicly, denounce former associates, and “name 





Party. Starting with Bloom (1987) it has been used by neo-conservatives to suggest that 
most progressive proposals — especially the movement known as “multi-culturalism” — 
are the result of bullying by a few opinion-setters and a conformist mentality on the part 
of their followers. A major polemic (or screed, to use a good Anglo-Saxon term) Is by the 
wife of the new Vice-President, Lynne V. Cheney, who dredges up all the horror stories 
that could be found. Chapter mottoes are quotations from Orwell’s /984, and the general 
thesis of the book is that political correctness is an attack on Western culture as a whole. 


316 Eugene TeSelle 


names.” Most intellectuals joined in the ideological warfare, if only out of 
revulsion at the manipulative behavior of the Communist Party. It was especially 
easy for Trotskyites to become militant anti-Stalinists; from among them came 
leaders of the C.I.A. front organizations that exerted their influence upon 
organized labor throughout Latin America, Europe, Asia, and Africa. In sum, 
then, Progressives were fatally divided by various attempts — from Stalinists, 
Trotskyites, and “realists” alike — to monopolize leadership. The situation was 
made worse by the willingness of the anti-Stalinists to make unholy alliances 
with enemies of liberal or progressive policies. 

Alternatives did exist. Henry Wallace, although he was naive about both 
Communism and American political realities, tried to build a broad coalition that 
would maintain the New Deal agenda and work through the United Nations. I. F. 
Stone began his clear-eyed documentation of the Cold War and the 
disinformation that it fostered. Irving Howe and others maintained continued 
loyalty to “independent socialist” goals, and C. Wright Mills wrote searching 
critiques of culture and society. Several plays by Arthur Miller dissected the 
inquisitorial spirit of the times. 

Perhaps the most dramatic moment came when Lillian Hellman, called 
before the Congressional committee in 1952, sent a letter offering to tell 
everything asked about herself but declining to answer questions about others. 
She explained her position in personal rather than ideological terms: 


I cannot and will not cut my conscience to fit this year’s fashions, even though I long ago 
came to the conclusion that I was not a political person and could have no comfortable 
place in any political group. 

I was raised in an old-fashioned American tradition and there were certain homely things 
that were taught to me: to try to tell the truth, not to bear false witness, not to harm my 


neighbor, to be loyal to my country, and so on. 
(93) 


When the Committee, confused about what to do, entered her letter into the 
record, her attorney immediately distributed copies to members of the press, one 
of whom said audibly, “Thank God somebody finally had the guts to do it” 
(Hellman 109). Two years later, during the McCarthy-Army hearings, Boston 
attorney Joseph Welch similarly turned the tide by saying, “Have you no sense of 
decency, sir?” Personal courage in appealing to society’s own understanding of 
virtue still has a powerful appeal, bypassing political and ideological disputes. 

Very few of the intellectuals, however, were able to walk the fine line 
between defending the rights of the accused, dissenting from their now 
unfashionable ideology, and championing progressive goals. To be sure, it was a 
difficult situation. But the dichotomy between Soviet totalitarianism and 
American freedom has seemed to many, both at the time and since, to be too 
simple an alternative, and defense of the capitalist world too limited a goal, 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 317 


especially when it meant alliances with forces of repression. There were many 
who wanted to be more inclusive at home and to end imperialism abroad. The 
“realists,” in turn, viewed these other possibilities as utopian fantasies and 
concentrated their attention on what could be accomplished in the world of 
political power. While intellectuals are usually able to stretch the imagination of 
political decision-makers, they may succeed only in rationalizing existing 
policies. 

More changes came with the surprise victory of Fidel Castro in Cuba on the 
first day of 1959, and the transformation of all of Latin America into a potential 
theater of revolution. Simultaneously a new home-grown spirit of radical 
engagement arose during the 1960s: first the civil rights movement, then the 
“counter culture,’ then opposition to intervention in Vietnam. Older 
intellectuals, who already had unfavorable memories of the Old Left, deplored 
the uncritical enthusiasms of the New Left, many of whose members were 
students at Berkeley and Columbia where they taught. Reaction against the 
student movements, the realignment of the electorate by the campaigns of 
George Wallace and Richard Nixon in 1968, and a general conservative turn 
brought a change in political mood that has lasted for thirty years now. 

Some writers have bemoaned the “de-politicization” of intellectuals in 
recent decades. Many explanations can be found: the steady discrediting of the 
Soviet experiment, which has seemed to many to be a discrediting of Marxism or 
Socialism altogether; the popularity of the “end of ideology” theory, which, Biel 
suggests, has “fetishized” realism, the two-party system, a mixed or capitalist 
economy, and social stability (233); and the “apostasy of the elites” after 1970, 
when they abandoned their civic responsibility and became lawyers and lobbyists 
for corporate interests or flocked to the new think-tanks funded by conservative 
foundations (Judis 156-79). Jacoby has an even longer list: the “enclosure” of 
intellectuals in the university as the chief, and perhaps the only, locus of 
intellectual activity (153); the decline of newspaper readership, the loss of small 
bookstores, and the control of book publishing by mega-corporations; the move 
to the suburbs by much of the educated public, leaving decaying cities behind 
them; and most recently the gentrification of the bohemian neighborhoods in 
which public intellectuals flourished (19-22, 40-53, 224, 232). 

Boggs has written much about this spirit of “de-politicization.” He indicts 
the increased “corporate colonization of society” as reflected in a privatized 
landscape of shopping malls and gated communities, mass marketing and 
concentrated control of communications. He reminds intellectuals, however, that 
they are not merely victims but perpetrators. He sees “antipolitics” as a major 
theme in many of the trends in recent decades: the counter culture with its 
distrust of authority, the “new social movements,” ecology and the turn to 
simpler lifestyles, even the spirit of localism promoted by the emphasis on 
participatory democracy and community organizing. At the level of ideology 
there is the vogue of “post-modernism,” an attack on all attempts to unify human 


318 Eugene TeSelle 


history and to find in it trends worth joining or ends worth seeking. 


Problematizing the Intellectuals, Issue #3. 

While intellectuals are often accused of being too theoretical, or of being 
potentially totalitarian, there is also a “middle” accusation of short- 
sightedness, partisanship, and deception. When intellectuals claim to be 
independent, this argument goes, it is illusory, for they remain bound to those 
who pay their salaries or give them grants, who buy their books or publish them 
in the first place, who can deny them passports or call them before 
Congressional committees. In other words, they are bound to the existing set of 
social relationships, expressed on the consumer side by what the public (guided 
by advertising agencies) will buy or watch, and on the producer side by the 
government agencies, corporations, foundations, and think-tanks which subtly 
shape the agenda even for innovative research and creative works of art. We are 
familiar with this line of analysis in Marxist circles, which see ideologies as an 
expression of power relationships. But there are equivalents in exchange 
theories, which are intensely aware of the marketing of ideas as well as 
commodities, and among conservatives, who are concerned about legitimacy 
and thus are alert to counterfeit claims to it. Benda suggested that writers, 
dependent upon the middle classes who buy their books, are tempted not only to 
join them in their hatred of the poor but to imitate the aristocracy’s arbitrary 
authority and its scorn for equality (1928, 23-24, 165-68). Intellectuals are 
especially dangerous, the argument continues, because their ties are not as 
obvious as those of the “kept” intellectuals of past centuries, directly dependent 
upon patrons who gave them the task of entertaining, celebrating, and 
legitimating their power; nor are they like the advisers and technical experts, 
“hired guns” and “spin artists,” who so evidently do the bidding of 
governments or corporations. This kind of critique is the most damaging, or 
perhaps merely the most humiliating, since it exhibits the intellectual as a 
fallible human being like all the others, but with the potentiality to engage in 
deception and self-deception to a far greater degree. 


Groping Toward a Strategy 

In examining the actual involvement of intellectuals in public life during the 
twentieth century, I have tried to isolate the basic criticisms, finding at least three 
of them: (1) Intellectuals may be so caught up in ivory-tower research or artistic 
self-expression as to be irrelevant to politics; (2) when they do engage 
themselves in politics, they too often become dangerously fanatical; and (3) in 
all cases they may be deceived about both their independence and their lack of 
self-interest. We have the right to hope, however, that these problems are not 
totally pervasive; even error and perversion give testimony to truths and values 
that are being feigned. These three sets of problems may help us map out the 
dead ends into which intellectuals have strayed so that others can be warned 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 319 


against making the same mistakes. 

What, then, can be said, with the wisdom of hindsight, about the proper task 
of intellectuals? Responses tend to be of two kinds, procedural and substantive. 
Clearly intellectuals have felt an obligation to defend the rights of all, and 
especially of intellectuals and creative artists like themselves, against 
totalitarianisms of the right, left, or center. While Benda is often cited by those 
who denounce the intervention of intellectuals in politics, he was a Dreyfusard 
who felt that intellectuals are not treasonous when they defend reason and truth 
and justice against the passions of the moment (1928, 50-51). In many cases this 
task means defending human rights against abuse of power by governments or by 
impassioned popular movements. 

The tragedy of the American left during the Thirties and Forties, and again 
during the Sixties, also suggests another procedural obligation: to denounce 
factional attempts at monopoly control. Precisely when there are vigorous 
popular movements and appealing programs, those who have strong “control 
needs” will be tempted to jump in front of the parade for their own 
aggrandizement, and those who repudiate them may be tempted in turn to make 
self-defeating alliances. The alternative is to keep moving ahead on a broad 
front, ignoring inappropriate behavior. 

When it comes to dealing with substantive issues, we have seen an 
abundance of dogmatism about ends and means. At the end of the twentieth 
century, however, dogmatic attitudes are limited largely to the “end of ideology” 
school and those who hold to the Thatcherite TINA principle (“there is no 
alternative” to the standing order). The left is often in the position of calling, 
with Carl Boggs, for some kind of “repoliticization.” What does that mean, and 
what might it look like? By politics Boggs means 


a linkage of immediate struggles to long-range goals, the development of a conscious, 
planned framework of action connected to societal issues and priorities, to a broadening 


of social governance. 
(2000, 227) 


In sum, one relates one’s own issues to the public realm and its debates and 
struggles. What, then, would be its content? Boggs does not suppose that any 
single theory or belief system will offer the solution, and if it did, it would have 
to be imposed by totalitarian means. He expects a continuation of the “vibrant 
multiplicity of discourses, identities, and movements” that has become a durable 
feature of our epoch (2000, 236). His own recommendation is what he calls 
“developmental democracy,” which “links expansion of democratic forms to the 
ideals of both human self-development and social progress” (2000, 253). But he, 
like the rest of us, is aware of its difficulties. 

The world about which intellectuals were concerned throughout the 
twentieth century was clearly a dangerous one. They often raised their voices to 


320 Eugene TeSelle 


protest injustices: savagery in conquest and warfare, abuses of legal process, 
violations of human rights, cynical deception of groups and whole nations. The 
world is appreciably better because of the role they played, for they sounded the 
alarm about many issues that might otherwise have been ignored. The very fact 
that they raised their voices caused political leaders to be more cautious in their 
actions, yet they did not by themselves achieve any major transformations. 
Imperialism in European, American, Asian, even Islamic and African forms has 
continued until the pressure of events forced concessions. Totalitarian 
governments were halted, in the end, only through open warfare or through 
sustained economic and diplomatic pressures. And even those achievements 
were ambiguous: Fascist and Nazi governments were permitted to grow because 
fear of Communism was even greater; the Cold War against Communism 
supported, and in fact created de novo, totalitarian regimes around the world; 
after the end of the Cold War the world is awash in a surplus of dangerous 
firearms that had been supplied by both sides to their surrogates; and the 
Pentagon is now depicting itself as the sole guarantor of order in the uncertain 
world of the twenty-first century. 

The last half of the twentieth century saw perhaps the greatest and most 
widely shared prosperity, at least in the First World, that has ever existed in 
human history. It cannot continue except through increased military, diplomatic, 
and economic controls. And if it does continue, we shall see a world in which 
local economies are disrupted by market forces, and employment becomes 
increasingly dependent upon a globalized market. Attempts to guide economic 
life toward humane ends will be overpowered by transnational corporations, able 
to purchase the favor of the major political parties. They can intimidate not only 
Third World governments but cities and states in the First World through threats 
to move jobs elsewhere, and persuade the world’s governments to replace their 
regulatory frameworks with the shadowy rulings of the World Trade 
Organization. Assaults on the environment — logging of rain forests, depletion 
of fossil fuel resources, increase of “greenhouse gases” in the atmosphere — will 
continue without effective controls. Human misery will be compounded by the 
pressures of population, environmental degradation, and global markets. 

This is the welcome that the twenty-first century extends to us. Intellectuals, 
as we say in the vernacular, “have their work cut out for them.” But it is a 
challenge that they cannot decline any more than their predecessors did in past 
centuries, including the one just passed. 


Appendix 

The author has a special interest in the kind of intellectual who earns a living in 
academe but is involved in public issues on a “volunteer” or “part-time” basis. 
To use Gramsci’s now-classic distinction, | have been both a “traditional 
intellectual” (and with a vengeance, dealing with the classical tradition of 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 321 


Christianity, figures like Augustine and Aquinas, and often defending the 
tradition against ill-informed attacks from liberationists) and an “organic 
intellectual,” closely associated with local organizations and quite specific social 
movements.’ Others beside Gramsci, of course, have made similar 
differentiations, e.g., between the “great tradition” and the “little tradition,” or 
between so-called “universal knowledge” and “local knowledge.” 

My activities as a “traditional intellectual” have turned out to be rather 
recondite in character, getting responses primarily from other scholars. I have 
not been either a popularizer or a “public intellectual,” building a career around 
commentary on current events. Although there have been those in Nashville who 
regard me as primarily an agitator who teaches at Vanderbilt “for cover,” my 
local activities have been more like an avocation, “volunteer work” rather than a 
profession. My writing in that area has not been the sort of thing that secures 
academic promotion and tenure, even as a public intellectual. It has been frankly 
journalistic, consisting of letters to the editor, short pieces in the newsletters of 
local and regional organizations, longer pieces in a few national publications of 
small circulation, and now on web pages. Through long experience | have 
learned fairly well how to write more or less effectively in these “little genres.” 

To be specific, my local activities have been centered in an interracial 
neighborhood with about 2000 households. We began organizing at just the time 
when the 1968 Fair Housing Act and the 1971 Charlotte-Mecklenburg decision 
on school desegregation made it possible to show that an_ interracial 
neighborhood was viable even in Nashville. These were also the years when the 
“urban movement” was gaining national visibility, and we were part of the effort 
to pass the Community Reinvestment Act which required federally insured 
lenders to meet the credit needs of all segments of their communities, including 
minority and lower-income communities and their residents. 

Despite being labeled a “community activist” (or perhaps because of it), I 
have never been part of the club of attorneys and business people, labor union 
officials, and office holders who are bound together by ties of influence and self- 
interest. In fact, | have been unappointable to any public boards and 
unconfirmable by the Council even if | were to be appointed by the Mayor. In an 
era of globalization I have focused upon neighborhoods, housing, community 
economic development, matters that definitely belong to the “little tradition.” We 
are of minor value to office-holders and power-brokers, except on those rare 


’ Gramsci did not scorn the traditional intellectual; he defended classical education and 
knew the importance of knowing and critically assessing the best that has been thought. 
While he knew that intellectuals always come from some social class and are likely to be 
the defenders of the dominant class, he also recognized the tendency of intellectuals to 
become an independent and autonomous group, especially when they become alienated 
from the ruling ideology, and he knew that some of them would join the “subaltern” 
groups and become their “organic intellectuals” (1971, 5-23, 1975, 12 §1). 


322 Eugene TeSelle 


occasions when they need to identify a site for some new federal program or find 
a local illustration of some newly fashionable trend such as the “new urbanism.” 
I think I understand what it is to be an “organic intellectual,” and one basically 
alienated from the Establishment, though my situation is dramatically different 
from that in Gramsci’s Italy. 

My activities as an intellectual, | was saying, have been bifurcated because I 
have also played a public role as a Presbyterian minister. During the 1980s I 
became involved in the Witherspoon Society, the “liberal caucus” in the 
Presbyterian Church (it is named for John Witherspoon, the only member of the 
clergy to sign the Declaration of Independence), and for more than ten years I 
have attended meetings of the annual General Assembly and taken part in the 
political maneuverings there. 

Thus I have had three quite different public roles: as “Professor Eugene 
TeSelle,” as “Rev. Eugene TeSelle,” and as “Citizen Gene TeSelle.” These are, 
as we say, three different “hats” that | wear, and while there must be at least a 
rough consistency among my activities, the differentiations between them are 
important. When I write letters to the editor of the Tennessean, | do not “pull 
rank” as either a professor or a minister, on the principle that what I say must 
make good sense as public argument, without claims of authority. In return | 
have been thanked at times for not associating my views with Vanderbilt 
University or the Presbyterian Church. Differentiation of roles, of course, is a 
characteristic feature of the modern age. 

While intellectuals sometimes act as though it would be demeaning to pay 
too much attention to local issues, there are many good reasons to make the 
connection. For one thing, involvement with local issues quickly sends one to the 
library, the bookstore, or the Internet, looking for precedents, perspectives, and 
analyses, in the process broadening one’s awareness of many disciplines. It is 
what intellectuals are trained to do. Local problems are likely to be the result of 
more general factors: economic pressures, demographic trends, laws and policies 
framed in Washington. One soon discovers not only resources but active allies, 
movements to be joined, campaigns to be waged. Conversely, local involvement 
is a good way to test and modify the grand theories and policy recommendations 
that are the usual domain of intellectuals. Of course the local situation may not 
be typical, so that what is feasible in some places is not in others, but if a 
proposal turns out to be unrealistic in too many places it will need to be 
rethought. And at the very least one must ask how to communicate grand 
theories and policies so that they will seem both persuasive and feasible ‘on the 
ground.” 

You can never tell when your activities will have a “conjuncture” with 
larger events. Then you become a foot soldier in the larger campaigns that are 
being waged, and you only hope that your perceptions and responses will be 
appropriate to both the local and the broader setting. 

During the fight against aid to the contras in the 1980s, we think it was 


Engaged Intellectuals and the Politics of Culture: A Retrospective Analysis 323 


important that opposition surfaced even in Nashville, the symbolic home of 
country music. Conservatives understood the importance of that symbolism, too; 
a conservative think-tank, the Institute for Religion and Democracy, set up a 
related organization, Presbyterians for Democracy and Religious Freedom, with 
headquarters in Nashville to demonstrate how “grass-roots” they were. 

I must also mention Bridgestone-Firestone, which got into the news during 
the year 2000 because of tread separation on certain kinds of tires, especially 
those used on large sports-utility vehicles. Many of the defective tires, it was 
discovered, had been made by “replacement workers” during the strike in 1995- 
96. Since the corporation’s U.S. headquarters are in Nashville, | was one of the 
local activists who took part in several demonstrations by the Rubber Workers 
(later merged with the Steel Workers). Union members came by the busloads 
from around the country and marched toward the corporate headquarters with 
huge papier-maché puppets of Mother Jones and other labor heroes. The 
Bridgestone-Firestone management limited the delegates who could enter the 
building. Once I went in alone, and I was scolded for taking part in such 
activities as a member of the clergy. On another occasion two were allowed in, 
and I was joined by a United Methodist university chaplain (most ministers 
serving congregations would not feel free to take part in labor demonstrations). It 
was clear at the time that Bridgestone-Firestone was feeling intense pressure 
from the Japanese corporation that had recently taken it over. Only now can the 
full effects be seen. 

On such occasions one will be, at best, like the chorus in Greek or 
Shakespearean drama, observing and commenting on events that are essentially 
outside one’s own control, but playing one’s part as best one can. All of that 
belongs to the proper work of the intellectual, too. 


Vanderbilt University 


324 Eugene TeSelle
